#### Step 4: Match Tenure with Asset Life (Identification by Horizon)
This is where the *classification* becomes a *decision*:
- If the asset is **current** (e.g., inventory), identify **short-term sources** (working capital loans, trade credit, CP).
- If the asset is **medium-life** (e.g., machinery, vehicles), identify **medium-term sources** (term loans, lease, hire purchase).
- If the asset is **permanent/long-life** (e.g., land, building, R&D), identify **long-term sources** (equity, debentures, retained earnings).

#### Step 5: Apply the Ownership Filter
For long-term external sources, the manager must further distinguish:
- **Owned (Equity):** Dilutes ownership but no fixed repayment obligation.
- **Borrowed (Debt):** Retains ownership but creates fixed financial charges and bankruptcy risk.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Long-Term Capital (LTC)} &= \text{Equity Component (E)} + \text{Debt Component (D)} \\
\text{Ideal Mix depends on: } D/E &\leq \text{Industry Threshold (typically 2:1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE] 
> **KTU Standard Problem Type:** *"Calculate the External Funds Requirement and identify the appropriate financial sources."*

**Problem Statement:**
A firm requires ₹100 Lakhs to finance its expansion. The firm's projected Net Profit after Tax (PAT) is ₹20 Lakhs, and it follows a dividend payout ratio of 40%. Depreciation for the year is ₹10 Lakhs. Spontaneous short-term liabilities are ₹10 Lakhs. The expansion includes:
- New Building: ₹50 Lakhs (useful life = 20 years)
- New Machinery: ₹30 Lakhs (useful life = 7 years)
- Additional Inventory: ₹20 Lakhs (operating cycle = 90 days)

**Step 1: Calculate Internal Accruals**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAT} &= ₹20\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Retention Ratio (b)} &= 1 - 0.40 = 0.60 \\
\text{Retained Earnings (RE)} &= 20 \times 0.60 = ₹12\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Depreciation (DP)} &= ₹10\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Internal Accruals (IA)} &= RE + DP = 12 + 10 = ₹22\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate External Funds Requirement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Capital Required (TC)} &= ₹100\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Spontaneous CL} &= ₹10\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{Internal Accruals (IA)} &= ₹22\ \text{Lakhs} \\
\text{EFR} &= TC - IA - \text{Spontaneous CL} \\
\text{EFR} &= 100 - 22 - 10 = ₹68\ \text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Identify Sources by Tenure**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Building (20 yrs)} &\Rightarrow \text{Long-term sources: Equity / Debentures / Retained Earnings} \\
\text{Machinery (7 yrs)} &\Rightarrow \text{Long-term sources: Term Loans / Debentures / Lease} \\
\text{Inventory (90 days)} &\Rightarrow \text{Short-term sources: Bank Overdraft / Trade Credit / CP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Recommended Identification Matrix**

| **Asset** | **Amount (₹ L)** | **Tenure** | **Identified Sources (in order of priority)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Building | 50 | Long-term (20 yr) | 1. Equity shares, 2. Redeemable Debentures, 3. Term Loan from FI |
| Machinery | 30 | Long-term (7 yr) | 1. Term Loan, 2. Hire Purchase, 3. Leasing |
| Inventory | 20 | Short-term (< 1 yr) | 1. Bank Cash Credit, 2. Trade Credit, 3. Commercial Paper |
| **Total** | **100** | — | — |

**Interpretation:** The firm should raise ₹68 Lakhs externally, allocating it across long-term (Building + Machinery) and short-term (Inventory) sources in line with the asset's economic life. This avoids the *maturity mismatch risk*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["FINANCIAL SOURCES OF A FIRM"] --> nodeB["TIME HORIZON CLASSIFICATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["OWNERSHIP CLASSIFICATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["ORIGIN CLASSIFICATION"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["SECURITY CLASSIFICATION"]

    nodeB --> nodeF["SHORT TERM LESS THAN 1 YEAR"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["MEDIUM TERM 1 TO 5 YEARS"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["LONG TERM MORE THAN 5 YEARS"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["Trade Credit"]
    nodeF --> nodeF2["Bank Overdraft"]
    nodeF --> nodeF3["Commercial Paper"]
    nodeF --> nodeF4["Factoring"]

    nodeG --> nodeG1["Term Loans from Banks"]
    nodeG --> nodeG2["Lease Financing"]
    nodeG --> nodeG3["Hire Purchase"]
    nodeG --> nodeG4["Redeemable Pref Shares"]

    nodeH --> nodeH1["Equity Share Capital"]
    nodeH --> nodeH2["Preference Share Capital"]
    nodeH --> nodeH3["Debentures and Bonds"]
    nodeH --> nodeH4["Venture Capital and FDI"]
    nodeH --> nodeH5["Retained Earnings"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["OWNED FUNDS: Equity Retained Earnings Reserves"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["BORROWED FUNDS: Debentures Bonds Bank Loans Public Deposits"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["INTERNAL SOURCES: Retained Earnings Depreciation"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["EXTERNAL SOURCES: Shares Debentures Loans Grants"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["SECURED: Mortgage backed Hypothecation backed"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["UNSECURED: Unsecured Debentures Trade Credit Reputation based"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: The Identification Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1: QUANTIFY CAPITAL NEED"] --> stepB["STEP 2: COMPUTE INTERNAL ACCRUALS"]
    stepB --> stepC{"STEP 3: GAP ANALYSIS EFR POSITIVE?"}
    stepC -- YES --> stepD["STEP 4: CLASSIFY BY TENURE"]
    stepC -- NO --> stepZ["NO EXTERNAL SOURCE NEEDED"]
    stepD --> stepE["STEP 5: APPLY OWNERSHIP FILTER"]
    stepE --> stepF["STEP 6: FINAL IDENTIFIED SOURCES LIST"]
    stepF --> stepG["OUTPUT: READY FOR COST OF CAPITAL MODULE 3"]
```

> [!NOTE] 
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Students:** When examiners ask "Classify the financial sources," draw the **time horizon tree** first (Short / Medium / Long) and then subdivide into *internal/external*. This is the highest-scoring visual technique in KTU 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Financial Sources" and list any four long-term sources of finance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Financial Sources refer to the various channels or instruments through which a firm raises the capital required to finance its assets, operations, and growth.
> **Any Four Long-term Sources (2 Marks - 0.5 each):**
> 1. Equity Share Capital (Ordinary Shares)
> 2. Preference Share Capital
> 3. Debentures / Bonds
> 4. Long-term Term Loans from Banks / Financial Institutions
> 5. Venture Capital / Private Equity
> 6. Retained Earnings (Sustained internal accruals)
> 7. Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Internal and External sources of finance. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Internal Sources** | **External Sources** |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Generated within the firm | Raised from outside the firm |
| Cost | Generally cheaper | Involves flotation/issue costs |
| Examples | Retained Earnings, Depreciation | Issue of Equity Shares, Bank Loan |

(1 Mark for distinction table, 2 Marks for valid examples — 0.5 each example).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the major financial sources available to a business firm under any three suitable bases, with suitable examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** ABC Ltd. requires ₹200 Lakhs for an expansion project. The PAT is ₹40 Lakhs, dividend payout is 50%, depreciation is ₹15 Lakhs, and spontaneous short-term liabilities are ₹25 Lakhs. The project includes a Building (₹120 L, 25-year life), Machinery (₹50 L, 8-year life), and Inventory (₹30 L). Identify the appropriate financial sources by tenure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Classification under three bases (each base carries ~2.3 marks):

**Basis 1: Time Horizon** (2 Marks)
- *Short-term (<1 yr):* Trade credit, Bank overdraft, CP, Factoring.
- *Medium-term (1–5 yrs):* Term loans, Leasing, Hire purchase.
- *Long-term (>5 yrs):* Equity, Debentures, Retained earnings, Venture capital.

**Basis 2: Ownership** (2 Marks)
- *Owned Funds:* Equity share capital, Preference share capital, Retained earnings.
- *Borrowed Funds:* Debentures, Bonds, Bank loans, Public deposits.

**Basis 3: Source Origin** (2 Marks)
- *Internal:* Retained earnings, Depreciation, Sale of surplus assets.
- *External:* Public issue of shares, Bank loans, Venture capital.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** A firm must identify sources across all three bases simultaneously to achieve an optimal mix that balances cost, risk, and control.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Internal Accruals Calculation** (2 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PAT} &= ₹40\ L;\quad \text{Payout} = 50\%;\quad \text{Retention (b)} = 0.50 \\
\text{Retained Earnings} &= 40 \times 0.50 = ₹20\ L \\
\text{Depreciation} &= ₹15\ L \\
\text{Internal Accruals} &= 20 + 15 = ₹35\ L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — External Funds Requirement** (2 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EFR} &= \text{Total Capital} - \text{IA} - \text{Spontaneous CL} \\
&= 200 - 35 - 25 = ₹140\ L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identification by Tenure** (2 Marks)

| Asset | Amount | Life | Identified Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| Building | ₹120 L | 25 yrs (Long) | Equity, Debentures, Long-term Loans |
| Machinery | ₹50 L | 8 yrs (Long) | Term Loan, Leasing, Hire Purchase |
| Inventory | ₹30 L | <1 yr (Short) | Bank Overdraft, Trade Credit, CP |

**Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 Mark)
The firm must raise ₹140 Lakhs externally, ensuring long-term sources back the Building and Machinery (avoiding maturity mismatch), while short-term sources finance the Inventory.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **short-term sources of finance** available to a business firm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Owned and Borrowed funds**. State the factors affecting the choice between them. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Any **five** short-term sources explained (each 1.2 marks, 1 mark for introduction/conclusion):
1. **Trade Credit** — Credit extended by suppliers; cost is the *implicit* loss of cash discount.
2. **Bank Overdraft** — Facility to withdraw beyond the balance in a current account up to a sanctioned limit.
3. **Cash Credit** — Similar to OD but the limit is calculated on the basis of *stock and receivables*.
4. **Commercial Paper (CP)** — Short-term unsecured promissory note issued by large, creditworthy companies.
5. **Factoring** — Selling accounts receivable to a factor at a discount to obtain immediate cash.
6. **Bill Discounting** — Discounting trade bills with a bank before maturity.
7. **Customer Advances** — Advance payments received from buyers against future delivery.

**Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Difference Table** (3.5 Marks):

| Basis | Owned Funds | Borrowed Funds |
|---|---|---|
| Ownership | Dilutes ownership | Ownership retained |
| Repayment | Not repayable during life | Fixed repayment schedule |
| Cost | Dividends (not tax-deductible) | Interest (tax-deductible) |
| Risk | Lower financial risk | Higher financial/bankruptcy risk |
| Control | Voting rights attached | No voting rights |

**Factors Affecting Choice** (3.5 Marks — any four):
1. Cost considerations (debt is tax-deductible, so cheaper).
2. Financial leverage and risk tolerance.
3. Control and dilution of ownership.
4. Earnings stability of the firm.
5. Market conditions and interest rate environment.
6. Industry norms and Debt-Equity benchmarks.

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse *classification* with *evaluation*. In the 14-mark question, the examiner awards marks for *listing AND justifying* each source by tenure. Simply writing "Equity, Debt, Loans" without mapping to the asset life will lose 3-4 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In numerical problems, students forget to **add Depreciation back** to retained earnings while computing internal accruals. This is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "Bank Loan" as a source without specifying the *type* (short-term vs. long-term). Always specify — e.g., "Bank Cash Credit (short-term)" or "Bank Term Loan (long-term)".
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not labeling the Mermaid diagrams clearly. Use **all-caps titles** and avoid special characters inside node labels.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready Bullets):**

- **Definition:** Financial Sources = Channels through which a firm raises capital.
- **Primary Classification:** **Short-term / Medium-term / Long-term** (by tenure) is the most important lens for KTU exams.
- **Cut-off periods:** Short = **<1 yr**, Medium = **1–5 yrs**, Long = **>5 yrs**.
- **Internal vs External:** Internal is **cheaper** but limited; External offers scale but at a cost.
- **Owned vs Borrowed:** Owned = Equity, Pref. Shares, Retained Earnings. Borrowed = Debentures, Bonds, Loans, Deposits.
- **Golden Rule of Identification:** *Match the tenure of the source with the economic life of the asset* — never finance a long-term asset with short-term funds (maturity mismatch).
- **Internal Accruals Formula:** $IA = (PAT \times \text{Retention Ratio}) + \text{Depreciation}$.
- **External Funds Requirement:** $EFR = TC - IA - \text{Spontaneous CL}$.
- **Top 4 Long-term Sources (most-tested):** Equity, Debentures, Term Loans, Retained Earnings.
- **Top 4 Short-term Sources (most-tested):** Trade Credit, Bank Overdraft, Commercial Paper, Factoring.
- **Keyword Triggers for Exam Answers:**
  - If the question says *"for machinery"* → think **Term Loan / Lease / HP**.
  - If it says *"permanent capital"* → think **Equity / Retained Earnings**.
  - If it says *"seasonal expansion"* → think **Short-term / Working capital loans**.
  - If it says *"startup / innovation"* → think **Venture Capital / FDI**.
- **Do NOT confuse** "Identification of Sources" with "Cost of Capital." Identification = listing; Cost = computation (Module 3).
- **Always draw a classification table or Mermaid tree** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual structure.
- **Numerical trap:** If PAT or Depreciation is given, ALWAYS compute *Internal Accruals* before determining the EFR.
- **Exam mantras:** *"Tenure matching", "Internal first, external later", "Debt is cheaper but riskier"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long Term Sources of Finance: Equity Shares, Debentures, Preferred Stock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Long Term Sources of Finance: Equity Shares, Debentures, Preferred Stock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Long-Term Sources of Finance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long-Term Finance** refers to the funds raised by a firm for a period exceeding **one year** (typically 3 to 20+ years). These funds are utilized to finance **fixed assets, permanent working capital, expansion, modernization, and long-term strategic projects**. As per the KTU 2024 scheme, the three principal long-term external sources of finance are **Equity Shares, Preference Shares (Preferred Stock), and Debentures (Bonds)**.

### The Three Pillars of Long-Term Finance

| Source | Nature | Risk to Issuer | Risk to Investor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Equity Shares** | Ownership (Permanent) | Low (no mandatory payout) | High (residual claim) |
| **Preferred Stock** | Hybrid (Quasi-Equity) | Moderate (fixed dividend) | Moderate (priority) |
| **Debentures** | Debt (Borrowed Capital) | High (mandatory interest + principal) | Low (secured return) |

---

## 1.2 Equity Shares (Common Stock) — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Equity Share** is a share that represents the basic ownership unit of a company. The equity shareholder is a part-owner of the company, entitled to dividends, voting rights, and a residual claim on assets after all creditors and preference shareholders are paid.

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The Co-owner of a Tea Stall"
Imagine you and your friend start a tea stall. Your friend puts in ₹1,00,000 as a **loan** (debenture-like) and you put in ₹1,00,000 as **your own capital** (equity). At the end of the year, profits come to ₹30,000.
- Your friend gets a **fixed 10% interest** (₹10,000) — that is the **debenture holder's** life.
- The remaining ₹20,000 is divided among owners — that is the **equity shareholder's** life.
- If losses occur, the friend still demands his 10% (legal right), but you may get **zero** (variable dividend). This is the essence of equity finance.

### Key Characteristics of Equity Shares
1. **Permanent Capital** — No maturity date; refundable only on winding up.
2. **Residual Claim** — Paid last, after debenture holders and preference shareholders.
3. **Variable Dividend** — Depends on profits; not legally compulsory.
4. **Voting Rights** — One share, one vote (control mechanism).
5. **Right of Pre-emption** — Existing shareholders get first right to buy new shares.
6. **Limited Liability** — Liability is restricted to the face value of shares held.

---

## 1.3 Debentures — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> A **Debenture** is an instrument of debt issued by a company under its common seal, acknowledging a debt and usually providing for the payment of interest at a fixed rate and the repayment of principal on a specified future date. It is a long-term debt security (a corporate bond).

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The Bank Loan as a Friend's Borrowing"
Suppose you borrow ₹50,000 from a friend for your business. You sign a written promise (debenture certificate) that you will pay him **₹500 every month as interest** (fixed coupon) and return the **₹50,000 after 5 years** (principal repayment). The friend does **not interfere** in your business (no voting rights). This is exactly how a debenture works.

### Key Characteristics of Debentures
1. **Creditor Relationship** — Debenture holder is a lender, not an owner.
2. **Fixed Interest (Coupon Rate)** — Interest is a legal obligation.
3. **No Voting Rights** — Generally, no participation in management.
4. **Tax-Deductible Interest** — The interest paid is an expense for the company.
5. **Secured/Unsecured** — Can be secured against company assets.
6. **Fixed Maturity** — Repayable on a specific date.
7. **Charge on Assets** — Can be a *fixed charge* (on a specific asset) or *floating charge* (on all assets).

### Common Types of Debentures (KTU High-Yield)
- **Secured Debentures** — Backed by a charge on assets.
- **Unsecured (Naked) Debentures** — No security.
- **Convertible Debentures** — Can be converted into equity after a period.
- **Non-Convertible Debentures (NCDs)** — Pure debt, no conversion.
- **Redeemable Debentures** — Repayable on a fixed date.
- **Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debentures** — No fixed maturity.

---

## 1.4 Preferred Stock (Preference Shares) — Definition & Intuition

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preferred Stock** is a hybrid security that combines features of both equity and debt. It carries a **fixed rate of dividend** (like debenture interest) and has a **priority over equity shareholders** in dividend payment and in repayment of capital on liquidation, but **does not generally carry voting rights**.

### 🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The VIP Investor"
Picture a restaurant where you have three types of investors:
- The **bank** (debenture holder) must be paid interest first.
- Next, your **uncle who gave ₹5 lakh for a fixed monthly return** (preference shareholder) gets paid — *before* you and your cousins.
- Finally, **you and your cousins** (equity shareholders) divide the leftover profits.
- The uncle does not participate in daily restaurant decisions (no voting rights), but he gets paid **before** the family. This is the privileged/hybrid position of a preference shareholder.

### Key Characteristics of Preferred Stock
1. **Fixed Rate of Dividend** — Stated as a percentage of face value (e.g., 8%).
2. **Priority in Dividends** — Paid before equity shareholders.
3. **Priority in Liquidation** — Capital repaid before equity.
4. **Cumulative or Non-Cumulative** — If cumulative, unpaid dividends accumulate.
5. **No Voting Rights** — Generally, except in specific circumstances.
6. **Participating/Non-Participating** — Some may share extra profits with equity.
7. **Convertible/Non-Convertible** — Can be converted into equity.

---

## 1.5 Comparative Snapshot (Quick-Reference Matrix)

| Parameter | Equity Share | Preferred Stock | Debenture |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ownership | Yes | No (Quasi) | No (Creditor) |
| Claim on Profits | Residual | Fixed Dividend | Fixed Interest |
| Obligation to Pay | Discretionary | Conditional | Compulsory |
| Priority on Liquidation | Last | Middle | First |
| Voting Rights | Yes | Usually No | No |
| Tax Deductibility | No (Dividend) | No (Dividend) | Yes (Interest) |
| Maturity | None | None (usually) | Fixed |
| Convertibility | — | Sometimes | Sometimes |
| Risk to Investor | High | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why These Three Sources?

The choice between equity, preference, and debenture is essentially a trade-off between three competing forces:

1. **Cost of Capital** — Cheaper finance reduces WACC, increasing firm value.
2. **Financial Risk (Leverage)** — More debt increases bankruptcy risk.
3. **Control & Ownership Dilution** — More equity dilutes promoter control.

A finance manager must balance the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** against the **financial leverage** and **control implications** while selecting a long-term source.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The module explicitly requires students to compute the **Cost of Capital** for each long-term source — this is a guaranteed **14-mark problem** in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

---

## 2.2 Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$)

The cost of equity is the **opportunity cost** of the shareholders — the minimum return they expect for bearing ownership risk. Since dividends are not tax-deductible and are paid out of post-tax profits, $K_e$ is the most expensive source.

### 2.2.1 Dividend Growth Model (Gordon's Model) — Most Tested in KTU
Assumes dividends grow at a constant rate $g$ forever.

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{N_P} + g$$

Where:
- $D_1 = D_0 (1+g)$ — Expected dividend at the end of Year 1
- $N_P$ — Net proceeds per share (issue price – issue costs)
- $g$ — Constant growth rate of dividends
- $D_0$ — Current/just paid dividend

### 2.2.2 Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)
Used when dividend pattern is unstable or growth cannot be reliably estimated.

$$K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$$

Where:
- $R_f$ — Risk-free rate (e.g., 10-year G-Sec yield)
- $\beta$ — Stock's systematic risk
- $R_m$ — Expected market return
- $(R_m - R_f)$ — Market risk premium

### 2.2.3 Earnings Yield Approach (No Growth)
Used when $g = 0$ (constant dividend forever).

$$K_e = \frac{EPS}{N_P}$$

---

## 2.3 Cost of Debt ($K_d$)

Debt is the **cheapest** long-term source because:
- Interest is **tax-deductible** (saves tax for the firm).
- Lenders require a lower return due to seniority in claim.

### 2.3.1 Cost of Irredeemable (Perpetual) Debenture
$$K_d = \frac{I (1 - t)}{N_P}$$

Where:
- $I$ — Annual interest payment = Face Value × Coupon Rate
- $t$ — Corporate tax rate
- $N_P$ — Net proceeds per debenture

### 2.3.2 Cost of Redeemable Debenture
$$K_d = \frac{I (1 - t) + \frac{(F - N_P)}{n}}{\frac{(F + N_P)}{2}}$$

Where:
- $F$ — Face (Redemption) Value of debenture
- $N_P$ — Net Proceeds
- $n$ — Remaining life in years
- $\frac{(F - N_P)}{n}$ — Annual amortization of discount/premium

This is the **approximation (average/short-cut) method** — heavily tested in KTU.

---

## 2.4 Cost of Preferred Stock ($K_p$)

Preferred dividend is paid out of **after-tax profits** and is **not tax-deductible** (just like equity). There is no tax shield.

### 2.4.1 Cost of Irredeemable Preferred Stock
$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_P}$$

Where:
- $D_p$ — Annual preference dividend = Face Value × Dividend Rate
- $N_P$ — Net proceeds per preference share

### 2.4.2 Cost of Redeemable Preferred Stock
$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{(F - N_P)}{n}}{\frac{(F + N_P)}{2}}$$

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Source | Formula | Key Assumptions | Typical $K$ Value (India) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost of Equity (Gordon)** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{N_P} + g$ | Constant growth $g$ | **12% – 18%** |
| **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Market data available | **10% – 20%** |
| **Cost of Irredeemable Debt** | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{N_P}$ | Perpetual | **6% – 9% (post-tax)** |
| **Cost of Redeemable Debt** | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \frac{F - N_P}{n}}{\frac{F + N_P}{2}}$ | Approx. method | **7% – 10% (post-tax)** |
| **Cost of Irredeemable Pref.** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{N_P}$ | Perpetual | **9% – 13%** |
| **Cost of Redeemable Pref.** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p + \frac{F - N_P}{n}}{\frac{F + N_P}{2}}$ | Approx. method | **10% – 14%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Cost (General Truth):** $K_d < K_p < K_e$. Debentures are the **cheapest**, followed by preferred stock, with equity being the **most expensive** long-term source. This is the conceptual ordering expected in KTU Part-A questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real engineering firms (e.g., L&T, Tata Steel, BHEL), choosing the right mix of these three sources is critical:
- A **capital-intensive manufacturer** (like a steel plant) uses heavy **debenture** financing for stable interest deductibility.
- A **tech-startup** relies almost entirely on **equity** because cash flows are uncertain and debt servicing is risky.
- A **mature, dividend-paying company** often issues **preference shares** to balance cost and control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Equity Using Gordon's Dividend Growth Model

### 3.1.1 Setup
Let a share be priced at $P_0$ today. The expected dividend at the end of Year 1 is $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$, at the end of Year 2 is $D_2 = D_0(1+g)^2$, and so on. The share can be sold at $P_1$ at the end of Year 1.

### 3.1.2 Discounting Equation
The investor's required return is $K_e$. Therefore:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^1} + \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^2} + \cdots + \frac{D_\infty}{(1+K_e)^\infty}$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting the Constant Growth Assumption
Since $D_t = D_0(1+g)^t = D_1(1+g)^{t-1}$, the series becomes:

$$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_1 (1+g)^{t-1}}{(1+K_e)^t}$$

### 3.1.4 Extracting and Simplifying
$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+K_e} \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1+g}{1+K_e}\right)^{t-1}$$

The infinite geometric series converges only if $(1+g) < (1+K_e)$, i.e., $g < K_e$. Applying $\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} r^t = \frac{1}{1-r}$:

$$P_0 = \frac{D_1}{1+K_e} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}} = \frac{D_1}{1+K_e - (1+g)} = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for $K_e$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g$$

**Final Verified Result:** $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{N_P} + g$ where $N_P$ is the net proceeds (replacing $P_0$ when there are flotation costs). ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Redeemable Debenture (Approximation Method)

### 3.2.1 Setup
The firm receives $N_P$ now, pays annual interest $I$ (tax-deductible) for $n$ years, and repays $F$ at maturity. The discount/premium $(F - N_P)$ is amortized equally over $n$ years.

### 3.2.2 Two Cash Outflow Components (Annualized)
1. **Annual interest (net of tax):** $I(1-t)$
2. **Annual amortization of discount/premium:** $\dfrac{F - N_P}{n}$ (added if issued at discount, subtracted if issued at premium)

### 3.2.3 Average Investment Base
The investor's average invested capital over the life is:

$$\text{Average Investment} = \frac{\text{Initial Amount} + \text{Terminal Amount}}{2} = \frac{N_P + F}{2}$$

### 3.2.4 Combining

$$K_d = \frac{\text{Annual Cash Outflow}}{\text{Average Investment}}$$

$$\boxed{K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{F - N_P}{n}}{\frac{N_P + F}{2}}}$$

∎

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always use the **approximation method** unless the question explicitly says "use IRR/YTM method." The approximation method is the syllabus-prescribed approach for PEIET743.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem

### 📘 Problem Statement
> A company issues **2,000 12% Debentures** of **₹100 each** at a discount of **5%**, redeemable after **10 years** at **₹110 per debenture**. The tax rate is **30%**. A similar issue was made 3 years ago at 14% interest. The current **cost of capital** of the company is **15%**. Calculate the cost of the new debenture issue.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- Number of debentures = 2,000
- Face value (F) = ₹100
- Coupon rate = 12% → $I = 100 \times 0.12 = ₹12$ per debenture per year
- Discount on issue = 5% → $N_P = 100 - 5 = ₹95$ per debenture
- Redemption value (RV) = ₹110 per debenture
- Life (n) = 10 years
- Tax rate (t) = 30%

### Step 2: Apply the Cost of Redeemable Debenture Formula

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{RV - N_P}{n}}{\frac{RV + N_P}{2}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator Components

**Component 1: After-tax interest**
$$I(1-t) = 12 \times (1 - 0.30) = 12 \times 0.70 = ₹8.40$$

**Component 2: Annual amortization of discount on redemption**
$$\frac{RV - N_P}{n} = \frac{110 - 95}{10} = \frac{15}{10} = ₹1.50$$

**Total Numerator:**
$$8.40 + 1.50 = ₹9.90$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator (Average Investment)

$$\frac{RV + N_P}{2} = \frac{110 + 95}{2} = \frac{205}{2} = ₹102.50$$

### Step 5: Final Cost Calculation

$$K_d = \frac{9.90}{102.50} = 0.09659 \approx 9.66\%$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{K_d \approx 9.66\% \text{ (per annum, post-tax)}}$$

### Step 6: Comparative Analysis
| Source | Cost |
| :--- | :--- |
| Current cost of capital | 15.00% |
| New Debenture Issue | **9.66%** ✓ |
| Decision | **Issue the debenture** (cheaper than existing capital) |

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out Problem: Cost of Equity (Gordon's Model)

### 📘 Problem Statement
> A company has paid dividends of **₹4 per share** for the past 5 years. The company expects dividends to grow at **8% per year** in the future. The current market price is **₹48 per share**. Compute the cost of equity.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- $D_0 = ₹4$
- $g = 8\% = 0.08$
- $P_0 = ₹48$

### Step 2: Compute Expected Dividend Next Year

$$D_1 = D_0 (1 + g) = 4 \times 1.08 = ₹4.32$$

### Step 3: Apply Gordon's Formula

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{4.32}{48} + 0.08$$

### Step 4: Evaluate

$$K_e = 0.09 + 0.08 = 0.17 = 17\%$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{K_e = 17\%}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Problem: Cost of Preference Share

### 📘 Problem Statement
> A company issues **10% preference shares** of face value **₹100** at a premium of **10%**. The issue expenses are **₹3 per share**. Compute the cost of preference capital.

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
- Face Value = ₹100
- Dividend Rate = 10% → $D_p = ₹10$
- Issue Price = ₹110 (10% premium)
- Issue Expenses = ₹3

### Step 2: Compute Net Proceeds

$$N_P = 110 - 3 = ₹107$$

### Step 3: Apply the Cost of Preferred Stock Formula

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_P} = \frac{10}{107}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate

$$K_p = 0.09346 \approx 9.35\%$$

### ✅ Final Answer
$$\boxed{K_p \approx 9.35\%}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Long-Term Sources of Finance

The following Mermaid block maps the **functional architecture** of long-term sources, their characteristics, and inter-relations. The diagram follows all Mermaid safety rules: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, and clean uppercase labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Long Term Sources of Finance]) --> A1{Permanent Capital?}
    A1 -->|Yes| A2[Equity Shares]
    A1 -->|No| A3{Fixed Periodic Payment?}
    A3 -->|Yes and No Voting| A4[Preference Shares]
    A3 -->|Yes and Compulsory Interest| A5[Debentures]
    A3 -->|No| A6[Retained Earnings / Reserves]

    A2 --> A2A[Voting Rights: YES]
    A2 --> A2B[Dividend: Variable]
    A2 --> A2C[Priority: Last in Liquidation]
    A2 --> A2D[Tax: No Deduction on Dividend]

    A4 --> A4A[Voting Rights: USUALLY NO]
    A4 --> A4B[Dividend: Fixed Rate]
    A4 --> A4C[Priority: After Debentures, Before Equity]
    A4 --> A4D[Tax: No Deduction on Dividend]

    A5 --> A5A[Voting Rights: NO]
    A5 --> A5B[Interest: Fixed and Compulsory]
    A5 --> A5C[Priority: First Claim on Assets]
    A5 --> A5D[Tax: INTEREST IS TAX DEDUCTIBLE]
    A5 --> A5E[Maturity: Fixed Date]

    A6 --> A6A[Internal Source - Not External]
    A6 --> A6B[Opportunity Cost = Opportunity Cost of Equity]

    classDef equity fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937
    classDef preference fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937
    classDef debt fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937
    classDef internal fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937
    classDef startnode fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#fef3c7

    class A2,A2A,A2B,A2C,A2D equity
    class A4,A4A,A4B,A4C,A4D preference
    class A5,A5A,A5B,A5C,A5D,A5E debt
    class A6,A6A,A6B internal
    class A0 startnode
```

## 4.2 Cost-of-Capital Comparison Architecture

This block shows how the three sources relate by cost — a guaranteed KTU theory question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Long Term Source Selection] --> B1{Cost Priority?}
    B1 -->|Lowest Cost| B2[Issue DEBENTURES]
    B1 -->|Moderate Cost| B3[Issue PREFERENCE SHARES]
    B1 -->|Highest Cost| B4[Issue EQUITY SHARES]

    B2 --> B2A[Cost Range: 6 to 10 percent Post Tax]
    B3 --> B3A[Cost Range: 9 to 13 percent]
    B4 --> B4A[Cost Range: 12 to 18 percent]

    B2A --> B2B[Tax Shield on Interest = Main Reason for Low Cost]
    B3A --> B3B[Fixed Dividend but No Tax Shield]
    B4A --> B4B[High Risk to Investor = High Required Return]

    B2 --> B2C[Increases Financial Leverage]
    B3 --> B3C[Moderate Leverage Effect]
    B4 --> B4C[No Financial Risk Added]

    classDef low fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef med fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px
    classDef high fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px
    class B2,B2A,B2B,B2C low
    class B3,B3A,B3B,B3C med
    class B4,B4A,B4B,B4C high
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of Cost-of-Capital Formulas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Cost of Capital Computation] --> C1{Which Source?}
    C1 -->|Equity| C2[Use K_e]
    C1 -->|Preference| C3[Use K_p]
    C1 -->|Debenture| C4[Use K_d]

    C2 --> C2A{Growth Known?}
    C2A -->|Yes| C2B[Gordon Model: D1 by NP plus g]
    C2A -->|No| C2C[CAPM: Rf plus Beta times Rm minus Rf]

    C3 --> C3A{Redeemable?}
    C3A -->|No| C3B[K_p equals D_p divided by NP]
    C3A -->|Yes| C3C[K_p equals D_p plus amortisation over Average Investment]

    C4 --> C4A{Redeemable?}
    C4A -->|No| C4B[K_d equals I times 1 minus t over NP]
    C4A -->|Yes| C4C[K_d equals I times 1 minus t plus amortisation over Average Investment]

    C2B --> C2D[Most Common in KTU]
    C4B --> C4D[Includes Tax Shield]
    C4C --> C4E[Tax Shield PLUS Redemption Premium Adjustment]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Distinguish between **Equity Shares** and **Preference Shares** on the basis of (i) voting rights, (ii) priority in dividend, and (iii) repayment on liquidation.

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point)
| Basis | Equity Share | Preference Share |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Voting Rights** | Carry full voting rights and can participate in management. | Generally **do not** carry voting rights. |
| **(ii) Priority in Dividend** | Paid **last** — only after preference dividend is paid. | Paid **first** — before any equity dividend. |
| **(iii) Repayment on Liquidation** | Repaid **last**, after all creditors and preference shareholders. | Repaid **before** equity shareholders but after debenture holders. |

---

### ❓ Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Why are **debentures considered the cheapest** source of long-term finance?

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)
1. **Tax Deductibility of Interest:** The interest paid on debentures is treated as an **expense** in the Profit & Loss Account, providing a tax shield. This reduces the **effective cost of debt** to the company. **(1 mark)**
2. **Lower Risk-Bearing:** Debenture holders are creditors with a **first charge on assets**, so they accept a lower rate of return compared to equity investors. **(1 mark)**
3. **No Dilution of Control:** Issue of debentures does **not dilute** ownership or voting rights, making it attractive for promoters. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (3-Mark Qs):** Students often write a generic answer like "interest is fixed" and lose marks. The examiner specifically looks for the keyword **"tax-deductibility / tax-shield"** — always include this. Avoid writing more than 3 points in 3-mark questions; stick to crisp, exam-oriented phrasing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Full Model Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part-B questions in the ESE carry **14 marks** and have an **internal choice**. Each Part-B question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth **7 marks**. Below are **two alternative question sets** — attempt any one.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Dec 2023]**

> **A company is planning to raise ₹50 lakhs through one of the following long-term sources:**
> 1. **Issue of 50,000 Equity Shares** of ₹100 each at par. Expected dividend = ₹12 per share. Dividend growth rate = 6%. Issue expenses = ₹5 per share.
> 2. **Issue of 10% Preference Shares** of ₹100 each at par, redeemable at a premium of 10% after 10 years. Issue expenses = ₹3 per share.
> 3. **Issue of 12% Debentures** of ₹100 each at par, redeemable at par after 10 years. Issue expenses = ₹2 per debenture. Tax rate = 30%.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **cost of each** source. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Advise the company on the **most economical** source of finance. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Solution A(a): Cost of Each Source (7 Marks)

**Source 1: Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Using Gordon's Model (with Flotation Cost)**
- $D_0 = ₹12$, $g = 6\%$, Issue price = ₹100, Issue expense = ₹5
- $D_1 = 12 \times 1.06 = ₹12.72$
- $N_P = 100 - 5 = ₹95$

$$K_e = \frac{D_1}{N_P} + g = \frac{12.72}{95} + 0.06 = 0.1339 + 0.06 = 19.39\%$$

**Source 2: Cost of 10% Redeemable Preference Shares ($K_p$)**
- $D_p = 10\% \text{ of } 100 = ₹10$
- $F = ₹100$, Redemption value $RV = 110$, $N_P = 100 - 3 = ₹97$, $n = 10$

$$K_p = \frac{D_p + \frac{RV - N_P}{n}}{\frac{RV + N_P}{2}} = \frac{10 + \frac{110 - 97}{10}}{\frac{110 + 97}{2}} = \frac{10 + 1.3}{103.5} = \frac{11.3}{103.5} = 10.92\%$$

**Source 3: Cost of 12% Redeemable Debentures ($K_d$) — Post Tax**
- $I = 12\% \text{ of } 100 = ₹12$
- $F = ₹100$, $RV = ₹100$, $N_P = 100 - 2 = ₹98$, $n = 10$, $t = 30\%$

$$K_d = \frac{I(1-t) + \frac{RV - N_P}{n}}{\frac{RV + N_P}{2}} = \frac{12(0.70) + \frac{100 - 98}{10}}{\frac{100 + 98}{2}} = \frac{8.4 + 0.2}{99} = \frac{8.6}{99} = 8.69\%$$

### ✅ Solution A(b): Advice on Most Economical Source (7 Marks)

**Comparative Cost Summary Table:**

| Source | Cost of Capital |
| :--- | :--- |
| Equity Shares ($K_e$) | **19.39%** |
| Preference Shares ($K_p$) | **10.92%** |
| Debentures ($K_d$) | **8.69%** ✓ Lowest |

**Recommendation:** The company should **issue 12% Debentures** because it has the **lowest cost of capital (8.69%)**, primarily due to the **tax shield** on interest. However, the finance manager must consider:
- The firm's **debt-servicing capacity** (interest coverage ratio).
- The **industry leverage norms**.
- The impact on **financial risk** and **credit rating**.

A balanced approach may use a **mix of all three sources** to optimize WACC.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / May 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **characteristics of Equity Shares** as a long-term source of finance. Why is it considered the **most expensive** source? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** ABC Ltd. issues **₹10,00,000 worth of 14% Debentures** redeemable at par after **8 years**. The debentures are issued at a discount of **5%** with issue expenses of **₹2 per ₹100 debenture**. The tax rate is **35%**. Compute the **cost of debenture capital**. Compare it with the cost of an alternative issue of **15% Debentures** issued at par with the same terms. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Solution B(a): Characteristics of Equity Shares (7 Marks)

**1. Ownership:** Equity shareholders are the **real owners** of the company and bear the ultimate business risk. *(1 mark)*

**2. Permanent Capital:** It has **no maturity date** and need not be repaid during the life of the company. *(1 mark)*

**3. Residual Claim:** Equity shareholders are the **last to receive** dividends and the **last to be repaid** during liquidation. *(1 mark)*

**4. Variable Dividend:** Dividends are **not legally compulsory** and fluctuate with profits. The board declares dividends based on earnings. *(1 mark)*

**5. Voting Rights & Control:** Equity shareholders enjoy **voting rights** in proportion to their holdings, giving them the power to influence management. *(1 mark)*

**6. Right of Pre-emption & Bonus:** Existing shareholders have the **first right** to buy new shares and are entitled to **bonus issues**. *(1 mark)*

**Why Most Expensive?**
- No tax shield on dividends.
- High investor risk (residual claim) → high required return.
- High flotation & administrative costs.
- No obligation to pay → higher expected returns to compensate for uncertainty. *(1 mark)*

---

### ✅ Solution B(b): Cost of Debenture Capital (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Issue size = ₹10,00,000 (10000 debentures × ₹100)
- Coupon rate = 14% → $I = ₹14$ per debenture
- Discount on issue = 5% → ₹5
- Issue expenses = ₹2 per debenture
- $N_P = 100 - 5 - 2 = ₹93$
- $F = ₹100$ (redemption at par)
- $n = 8$ years
- $t = 35\%$

**Step 1: After-tax Interest**
$$I(1-t) = 14 \times 0.65 = ₹9.10$$

**Step 2: Annual Amortization of Discount**
$$\frac{F - N_P}{n} = \frac{100 - 93}{8} = \frac{7}{8} = ₹0.875$$

**Step 3: Average Investment**
$$\frac{F + N_P}{2} = \frac{100 + 93}{2} = ₹96.50$$

**Step 4: Cost of 14% Debenture**
$$K_{d,14\%} = \frac{9.10 + 0.875}{96.50} = \frac{9.975}{96.50} = 10.34\%$$

**Step 5: Cost of 15% Alternative Debenture (issued at par)**
- $I = ₹15$, $N_P = 100 - 2 = ₹98$ (only issue expenses)
- $F = 100$, $n = 8$, $t = 35\%$

$$K_{d,15\%} = \frac{15(0.65) + \frac{100-98}{8}}{\frac{100+98}{2}} = \frac{9.75 + 0.25}{99} = \frac{10}{99} = 10.10\%$$

**Conclusion:**

| Debenture Issue | Cost |
| :--- | :--- |
| 14% (Discount) | **10.34%** |
| 15% (at Par) | **10.10%** ✓ |

The **15% debenture issued at par is cheaper** (10.10% < 10.34%) because the higher coupon is offset by the **lack of issue discount** and lower flotation cost burden per unit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (14-Mark Qs):**
> 1. **Forgetting the tax shield** on debenture interest — results in over-stating $K_d$ by 30–35%. Always multiply interest by $(1-t)$.
> 2. **Using $P_0$ instead of $N_P$** in Gordon's model when flotation costs exist. $N_P$ = Issue Price − Issue Expenses.
> 3. **Confusing "discount on issue" with "discount on redemption"** — these are two different amounts and affect the formula differently.
> 4. **Not amortizing** the premium/discount in the cost of redeemable debenture formula — this causes a **2-3 mark deduction**.
> 5. **Writing qualitative answers** for numerical questions — KTU strictly expects **formula + substitution + final numerical answer** with units.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (e.g., "using the approximation method") — costs 0.5 mark but signals conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Long-term finance** = funds required for > 1 year; three primary external sources = **Equity, Preference, Debenture**.
- ✅ **Equity Share** = ownership, voting rights, variable dividend, last in priority, no tax shield, **most expensive** source.
- ✅ **Preference Share** = hybrid, fixed dividend, priority over equity, usually no voting, no tax shield.
- ✅ **Debenture** = debt, fixed compulsory interest, first priority, **tax-deductible interest**, cheapest source.
- ✅ **Cost Order:** $K_d < K_p < K_e$ (always).
- ✅ **Gordon's Model:** $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{N_P} + g$ — use when dividend grows at constant rate.
- ✅ **CAPM:** $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ — use when growth is unstable.
- ✅ **Cost of Debt (Irredeemable):** $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{N_P}$.
- ✅ **Cost of Debt (Redeemable — Approximation):** $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \frac{F - N_P}{n}}{\frac{F + N_P}{2}}$ — this is the **KTU-prescribed** formula.
- ✅ **Cost of Preferred Stock (Irredeemable):** $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{N_P}$.
- ✅ **Cost of Preferred Stock (Redeemable):** $K_p = \dfrac{D_p + \frac{F - N_P}{n}}{\frac{F + N_P}{2}}$.
- ✅ **Flotation costs always reduce $N_P$** and therefore **increase the cost of capital**.
- ✅ **Tax shield on interest = $I \times t$** — the single biggest reason debentures are cheap.
- ✅ **Cumulative preference shares** accumulate unpaid dividends — relevant for valuation.
- ✅ **Convertible debentures** offer flexibility to investors and reduce effective cost.
- ✅ **Retained earnings**, though internal, also have an opportunity cost equal to $K_e$.
- ✅ Always express final answers as **percentages (e.g., 9.66%)** — KTU specifically penalizes decimal-only answers.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions, always present a **comparative table** of all computed costs before recommending the optimal source.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Term Sources: Bank Sources, Trade Credit, Overdrafts, Commercial Papers, Money Markets and Mutual Funds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Short Term Sources: Bank Sources, Trade Credit, Overdrafts, Commercial Papers, Money Markets and Mutual Funds ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Introduction to Short-Term Sources of Finance **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > Short-term sources of finance refer to those funds that are raised by a firm for a period not exceeding **one year** (≤ 365 days) to finance its **current assets** such as inventory, receivables, and cash, fulfilling the **operating cycle** needs of the business. These instruments are also termed **spontaneous, temporary, or working capital financing sources**. They appear on the **liability side of the balance sheet** under *current liabilities*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > The six core short-term sources expected for the KTU Board Exam are: > 1. **Bank Sources** (Cash Credit, Overdraft, Short-term Loans, Bill Discounting) > 2. **Trade Credit** > 3. **Overdrafts** (treated as a distinct instrument) > 4. **Commercial Papers (CP)** > 5. **Money Market Instruments** (T-Bills, CDs, CBLO, Repo) > 6. **Mutual Funds (Liquid / Money Market Mutual Funds)** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Salary Cycle" Model Imagine you are a salaried professional. Your **monthly income** is a long-term source (salary). But between two paydays, you might: - Use a **credit card** (Trade Credit) - Take a small **personal loan** from the bank (Bank Loan) - Use an **overdraft** facility on your account (Overdraft) - Park surplus cash in a **liquid mutual fund** (Money Market Fund) Similarly, a company has a continuous flow of expenses (wages, raw materials, utilities) before its receivables come in. It must plug this **timing gap** with **short-term financing**. > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** A firm should match the **maturity of the source with the purpose**. Short-term assets (inventory, receivables) should ideally be financed by short-term sources. This is the **Matching Principle (Hedging Approach)** of working capital. --- ### 1.3 Classification Snapshot | Category | Examples | Typical Tenure | Risk to Lender | |---|---|---|---| | **Spontaneous Sources** | Trade Credit, Accrued Expenses | 30–90 days | Very Low | | **Bank Sources** | CC, OD, ST Loan, Bill Discounting | 90 days–1 year | Low–Medium | | **Market Instruments** | Commercial Paper, T-Bills, CDs | 15–365 days | Low | | **Institutional Pool** | Mutual Funds (Liquid Funds) | 1 day–91 days | Medium | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 A. BANK SOURCES OF SHORT-TERM FINANCE Banks are the **largest and most traditional** providers of short-term funds to Indian corporates. The principal bank-based instruments are: #### (i) Cash Credit (CC) - A **running account** with a pre-sanctioned **drawing limit (drawing power)**. - Interest is charged only on the **utilized amount**, not on the sanctioned limit. - The firm can **withdraw and repay repeatedly**, making it highly **flexible**. - Secured against **stock-in-trade, receivables, or other current assets**. - Interest rate is usually linked to the **bank's MCLR/Base Rate**. #### (ii) Overdraft (OD) - An **extension of credit** allowing the borrower to withdraw more than the available balance in the current account, up to an agreed limit. - It is essentially a **temporary negative balance** permitted by the bank. - Interest is charged **daily** on the overdrawn amount. > [!NOTE] > **Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):** > - **Cash Credit** is given against a **specific security** (hypothecation/pledge of stock). > - **Overdraft** is given generally against the **overall creditworthiness** and sometimes personal security of the proprietor/partners. #### (iii) Short-Term Bank Loan - A **lump-sum advance** for a **specific tenure** (≤ 1 year). - Repayment schedule may be bullet (single payment at maturity) or staggered. - Lower flexibility than CC/OD but **cheaper** in many cases. #### (iv) Bill Discounting - The firm **sells its trade receivables (bills/invoices)** to the bank at a discount. - The bank charges a **discount rate** (bank rate minus rebate). - The firm receives immediate cash; the bank collects the full amount from the debtor at maturity. --- ### 2.2 B. TRADE CREDIT **Definition:** > Trade credit is the credit extended by one business firm to another for the purchase of goods and services. It is a **spontaneous source** of finance that arises automatically with each purchase and does not require any formal negotiation. **Key Terms (T&P - Terms of Purchase):** - **Credit Period** – Maximum time allowed for payment (e.g., "net 60" = 60 days). - **Discount Period** – Window within which a cash discount is offered (e.g., "2/10 net 60" = 2% discount if paid within 10 days; otherwise, full payment within 60 days). #### Cost of Trade Credit – The KTU Favourite! For terms *k/t net n* (k% discount if paid within t days, otherwise net n days): > **Nominal Cost of Forgoing the Discount:** > $$\text{Nominal Cost} = \frac{k}{100 - k} \times \frac{360}{n - t}$$ > **Effective Annual Cost (Annualized):** > $$\text{Effective Cost} = \left(1 + \frac{k}{100-k}\right)^{\frac{360}{n-t}} - 1$$ The firm's **decision rule**: - If the firm's **cost of bank borrowing** < **cost of trade credit** → **Take the discount**. - If the firm's **cost of bank borrowing** > **cost of trade credit** → **Forgo the discount** and use the credit. --- ### 2.3 C. OVERDRAFTS (Detailed) An overdraft is technically a **fluctuating debit balance** in a current account, but in financial management we analyze it as: - A **bridge loan** to cover timing mismatches. - It is **demand-based**, meaning the bank can recall it anytime. - The cost is **floating-rate interest** charged on **daily outstanding**. - **Effective Cost of OD** (if interest is charged quarterly but compounded annually): > $$\text{Effective Rate} = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$ > where *r* = nominal annual rate, *m* = number of compounding periods. --- ### 2.4 D. COMMERCIAL PAPERS (CP) **Definition:** > A Commercial Paper is an **unsecured, short-term promissory note** issued by large, highly creditworthy corporates directly in the money market to finance their immediate working capital needs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Regulatory Guidelines (RBI / SEBI – must memorize for KTU):** > - **Minimum Credit Rating:** A1+ (by a SEBI-registered CRA). > - **Minimum Issue Size:** ₹5 Lakh (face value). > - **Maximum Tenure:** 15 days to **1 year** (currently capped at less than 1 year). > - **Who can issue?** Listed companies, NBFCs, unlisted companies with net worth ≥ ₹4 crore, etc. > - **Who can invest?** Individuals, banks, mutual funds, NRIs (with repatriation clause), etc. > - **Issue Mode:** At a **discount to face value**; redeemed at par on maturity. #### Cost of Commercial Paper For a CP issued at discount on face value: > $$\text{Discount (Interest)} = F - P$$ > $$\text{Period Rate} = \frac{F - P}{P}$$ > $$\text{Annualized Cost} = \frac{F - P}{P} \times \frac{365}{t}$$ Where: - *F* = Face Value (maturity proceeds) - *P* = Issue Price (net proceeds to the company) - *t* = Tenure in days --- ### 2.5 E. MONEY MARKET **Definition:** > The money market is a segment of the financial market where **short-term funds (≤ 1 year)** are borrowed and lent through instruments of **high liquidity, low risk, and near-money characteristics**. #### Major Money Market Instruments (India) | Instrument | Issuer | Tenure | Yield Type | |---|---|---|---| | **Treasury Bills (T-Bills)** | Central Government (RBI) | 91, 182, 364 days | Discount-based | | **Certificate of Deposit (CD)** | Scheduled Banks | 7 days – 1 year | Discount-based | | **Commercial Paper (CP)** | Corporates / NBFCs | 15 – 365 days | Discount-based | | **CBLO (Collateralized Borrowing & Lending Obligation)** | CCIL | 1 day – 1 year | Repo-like | | **Repurchase Agreement (Repo)** | Banks / RBI | Overnight – 14 days | Collateralized | | **Call Money** | Banks (interbank) | 1 day (overnight) | Variable | **Yield on Treasury Bill (T-Bill):** > $$\text{Discount Yield} = \frac{F - P}{F} \times \frac{360}{t}$$ > $$\text{Bond Equivalent Yield} = \frac{F - P}{P} \times \frac{365}{t}$$ --- ### 2.6 F. MUTUAL FUNDS (Liquid / Money Market Funds) **Definition:** > A mutual fund pools money from investors and invests it in short-term money market instruments. **Liquid funds / Money Market Mutual Funds** are specialized MF schemes that invest in debt instruments maturing in ≤ 91 days, making them a **safe parking lot for surplus corporate cash**. **Why corporates park surplus cash here:** - **Daily Dividend** option — money earns till the last day. - **High liquidity** — redemption possible within 24 hours. - **Better returns** than a savings bank account. - **Professional management** with credit risk diversification. > [!NOTE] > **SEBI Classification of Debt MF Schemes (must know for KTU):** > - **Overnight Fund** – instruments ≤ 1 day > - **Liquid Fund** – ≤ 91 days > - **Ultra Short Duration Fund** – Macaulay duration 3–6 months > - **Low Duration Fund** – 6–12 months --- ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet – High-Yield Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | |---|---|---| | 1 | Nominal Cost of Trade Credit | $\dfrac{k}{100-k} \times \dfrac{360}{n-t}$ | | 2 | Effective Annual Cost of Trade Credit | $\left(1 + \dfrac{k}{100-k}\right)^{\frac{360}{n-t}} - 1$ | | 3 | Cost of Commercial Paper (Annualized) | $\dfrac{F-P}{P} \times \dfrac{365}{t}$ | | 4 | T-Bill Discount Yield | $\dfrac{F-P}{F} \times \dfrac{360}{t}$ | | 5 | T-Bill Bond Equivalent Yield | $\dfrac{F-P}{P} \times \dfrac{365}{t}$ | | 6 | Effective Cost with Quarterly Compounding | $\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{4}\right)^4 - 1$ | | 7 | Effective Cost of OD (Monthly compounding) | $\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{12}\right)^{12} - 1$ | | 8 | Drawing Power in Cash Credit | $\% \times \text{Stock} + \% \times \text{Receivables} - \text{Other Secured Loans}$ | | 9 | Bill Discounting Discount | $\dfrac{F \times r \times t}{360}$ | | 10 | Net Proceeds of Bill Discounting | $F - \text{Bank's Discount - Commission}$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Common Mistake:** > For **T-Bill yield**, RBI uses **360 days** in the denominator (money market convention), but for **CP**, SEBI convention uses **365 days**. Many students mix this up — *always check the denominator!* --- ### 2.8 Real-World Utility - **Manufacturing Firms:** Use CC and OD for raw-material financing; CPs for seasonal inventory build-up. - **Retail Chains:** Rely heavily on trade credit from FMCG suppliers. - **IT/Service Firms:** Park daily surplus cash in Liquid Mutual Funds; issue CPs for payroll cycles. - **PSUs:** Use T-Bills for parking temporary Treasury surpluses. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Cost of Trade Credit Let us derive the most-asked formula in the KTU board exam — the **Effective Cost of Trade Credit**. **Setup:** The supplier offers terms: *k/t net n*. - If the firm pays within *t* days, it pays $P \times (1 - \frac{k}{100})$. - If it pays at *n* days, it pays the full $P$. - The firm effectively **borrows** the *discounted amount* for the extra $(n - t)$ days. **Step 1:** The benefit forgone (interest on the discount) = $\frac{k}{100} \times P$. **Step 2:** The actual amount that gets "carried" by the supplier for the extra period = $P \times \left(1 - \frac{k}{100}\right)$. **Step 3:** Hence, the rate for the extra period of $(n - t)$ days is: $$ r_{(n-t)} = \frac{\text{Interest foregone}}{\text{Funds actually used}} = \frac{\frac{k}{100} \times P}{P \times \left(1 - \frac{k}{100}\right)} $$ $$ r_{(n-t)} = \frac{k}{100 - k} $$ **Step 4:** Annualizing this to a 360-day year (assuming each period is funded similarly across the year): $$ \text{Nominal Annual Cost} = \frac{k}{100 - k} \times \frac{360}{n - t} $$ **Step 5:** To get the **effective annual** cost using compound interest (because the period repeats throughout the year): $$ \text{Effective Cost} = \left(1 + \frac{k}{100 - k}\right)^{\frac{360}{n-t}} - 1 $$ > **Verification with a numerical example:** > Let terms be **2/10 net 60**. > Then $k = 2$, $t = 10$, $n = 60$. > > Nominal Cost = $\dfrac{2}{98} \times \dfrac{360}{50}$ = $\dfrac{2 \times 360}{98 \times 50}$ = $\dfrac{720}{4900}$ ≈ **0.1469 or 14.69%**. > > Effective Cost = $\left(1 + \dfrac{2}{98}\right)^{\frac{360}{50}} - 1$ = $(1.0204)^{7.2} - 1$ ≈ **0.1572 or 15.72%**. --- ### 3.2 Cost of Commercial Paper – Full Derivation **Setup:** A firm issues a CP with face value $F$, issue price $P$, maturity $t$ days. **Step 1:** The firm's inflow on Day 0 = $P$. **Step 2:** The firm's outflow on Day *t* = $F$ (face value paid to investor). **Step 3:** Holding period return for the investor: $$ \text{HPR} = \frac{F - P}{P} $$ **Step 4:** Since the firm holds this "loan" for *t* days, the periodic (per-day) rate is $\frac{HPR}{t}$. **Step 5:** Annualizing (simple interest convention in money market): $$ \text{Annualized Cost of CP} = \frac{F - P}{P} \times \frac{365}{t} $$ **Worked Example:** A CP has Face Value = ₹1,00,000, Issue Price = ₹99,000, Tenure = 90 days. Annualized Cost = $\dfrac{1,00,000 - 99,000}{99,000} \times \dfrac{365}{90}$ = $\dfrac{1,000}{99,000} \times 4.0556$ = $0.01010 \times 4.0556$ ≈ **0.0410 or 4.10% per annum**. --- ### 3.3 Cost of Overdraft – Worked Demonstration A firm uses an OD facility at a stated rate of **12% p.a.**, but interest is **debited quarterly** and added to the principal. **Step 1:** Identify compounding frequency — *m = 4* (quarterly). **Step 2:** Apply the EAR formula: $$ EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1 = \left(1 + \frac{0.12}{4}\right)^4 - 1 $$ $$ EAR = (1.03)^4 - 1 $$ **Step 3:** Expand: $$ (1.03)^2 = 1.0609 $$ $$ (1.03)^4 = (1.0609)^2 = 1.0609 \times 1.0609 $$ Computing: $$ 1.0609 \times 1.0609 = 1.12550881 $$ **Step 4:** Subtract 1: $$ EAR = 1.12550881 - 1 = 0.1255 \approx \mathbf{12.55\%} $$ So, although the **nominal** rate is 12%, the **effective** cost is **12.55%**. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Which Short-Term Source to Use? | Decision Criterion | Cheapest Source | Costliest Source | |---|---|---| | Pure cost (effective %) | Bank Loan (high credit) | Trade Credit forgone (often 30%+) | | Flexibility | Cash Credit / OD | Bullet Short-term Loan | | Speed of Access | Trade Credit (instant) | CP (3-7 days setup) | | Tenure flexibility | OD (on-demand) | T-Bills (fixed) | | Best for surplus parking | Liquid Mutual Fund | Saving Bank (lowest return) | | Best for raising funds | CP (large cos) / Bank Loan | Trade Credit (no negotiation) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Decision Rule (frequently asked):** > Choose the source with the **lowest effective cost AFTER adjusting for risk, flexibility, and impact on credit rating**. For a single, large, one-time need, prefer a **Bank Loan**; for **continuous fluctuating needs**, prefer **CC/OD**; for **ultra-short idle cash**, prefer **Liquid MF / T-Bills**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – Universe of Short-Term Sources ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SPON["SPONTANEOUS SOURCES"] TC["Trade Credit
Supplier to Firm"] ACC["Accrued Expenses
Wages Taxes"] end subgraph BANK["BANK SOURCES"] CC["Cash Credit
Running Account"] OD1["Overdraft
Temporary Limit"] STL["Short Term Loan
Lump Sum"] BD["Bill Discounting
Receivables Sale"] end subgraph MKT["MONEY MARKET INSTRUMENTS"] CP1["Commercial Paper
Corporate Unsecured"] TB["Treasury Bills
Government"] CD["Certificate of Deposit
Bank Issued"] CBLO["CBLO Repo
Collateralized"] end subgraph MF["MUTUAL FUNDS INSTITUTIONAL"] LMF["Liquid Mutual Fund
Park Surplus Cash"] UMF["Ultra Short Duration MF"] end FIRM["CORPORATE FIRM"] WC["WORKING CAPITAL NEEDS"] FIRM --> SPON FIRM --> BANK FIRM --> MKT FIRM --> MF TC --> WC CC --> WC OD1 --> WC STL --> WC BD --> WC CP1 --> WC TB --> WC CD --> WC CBLO --> WC LMF --> WC UMF --> WC FIRM -.Surplus Cash.-> LMF LMF -.Daily Dividend.-> FIRM ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Trade Credit Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Receive Terms k/t net n
Example: 2/10 net 60"] --> B{"Firm has
liquidity?"} B -- "Yes, can pay early" --> C["Pay within t days
Take Discount"] B -- "Short on Cash" --> D{"Cost of bank
borrowing?"} D -- "Lower than trade credit cost" --> E["Borrow from Bank
Pay within t days
Take Discount"] D -- "Higher than trade credit cost" --> F["Forgo Discount
Pay at n days
Use Trade Credit"] C --> G["Effective Cost:
k/100-k x 360/n-t"] F --> H["No Cash Outflow Now
But Pay Full Price Later"] E --> I["Smart Hybrid Choice
Minimize Total Cost"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – CP Issuance Process Flow ```mermaid graph LR A["Issuer Company
Net Worth above 4 Cr"] --> B["Get Credit Rating
A1+ from CRA"] B --> C["Appoint Issuing
and Paying Agent"] C --> D["Decide Tenure
15 to 365 days"] D --> E["Set Face Value
Min 5 Lakh"] E --> F["Issue at Discount
Investors Subscribe"] F --> G["Net Proceeds
to Issuer"] G --> H["Wait t days"] H --> I["Pay Face Value
at Maturity"] ``` ### 4.4 Block Architecture – Cost Comparison Framework ```mermaid graph TD SRC["Short Term Source"] --> AN["Annualize the Cost"] AN --> EF["Compute EAR if Compound"] EF --> Q1{"Cost below
Firm's WACC?"} Q1 -- Yes --> USE["USE THIS SOURCE"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Strategic
Benefit?"} Q2 -- Yes --> USE2["Use but document
strategic rationale"] Q2 -- No --> REJECT["REJECT / Negotiate"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between Cash Credit and Overdraft as short-term bank sources of finance.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Cash Credit | Overdraft | |---|---|---| | Security | Specific charge on stock/receivables | General creditworthiness / personal security | | Drawing Limit | Based on stock value (drawing power) | Pre-fixed limit | | Purpose | Working capital / inventory | Temporary mismatch in receipts and payments | | Tenure | Usually 1 year (renewable) | On-demand | | Interest Charge | On daily utilized amount | On daily overdrawn amount | Both are **flexible bank sources** but CC is **purpose-driven and secured**, whereas OD is **need-based and often unsecured**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is a Commercial Paper? Mention any four eligibility conditions for issuing CP in India.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Commercial Paper (CP)** is an **unsecured, short-term, negotiable promissory note** issued by large corporates in the money market to finance immediate working capital needs. **Four Eligibility Conditions:** 1. The issuer must have a **minimum credit rating of A1+** from a SEBI-registered Credit Rating Agency. 2. The issuer must be a **listed company, NBFC, or unlisted company with net tangible worth of at least ₹4 crore**. 3. The minimum **face value per issue** is **₹5 lakh**, with subsequent tranches in multiples of ₹5 lakh. 4. The CP can have a maturity between **15 days and 365 days** and must be issued at a **discount to face value**. --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 questions include an internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below. --- #### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** A supplier offers credit terms of **2/10 net 60**. Explain the meaning of these terms and compute both the **nominal** and **effective** annual cost of forgoing the cash discount. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **five key features of Trade Credit** as a short-term source of finance. Under what conditions should a firm *forgo* the discount? *(7 marks)* --- **MODEL SOLUTION** **(a) Meaning of Terms (3 marks):** The terms *2/10 net 60* mean: - The buyer gets a **2% discount** on the invoice price if payment is made within **10 days** (the discount period). - Otherwise, the **full (net) amount** is due within **60 days** (the credit period). If the buyer does not pay within 10 days, they essentially forgo 2% of the price to retain the cash for an additional 50 days (60 − 10). **Computation of Nominal Cost (2 marks):** $$ \text{Nominal Cost} = \frac{k}{100 - k} \times \frac{360}{n - t} = \frac{2}{98} \times \frac{360}{50} $$ $$ = \frac{2 \times 360}{98 \times 50} = \frac{720}{4900} = 0.14694 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Nominal Cost} \approx 14.69\%} $$ **Computation of Effective Annual Cost (2 marks):** $$ \text{Effective Cost} = \left(1 + \frac{2}{98}\right)^{\frac{360}{50}} - 1 = (1.02041)^{7.2} - 1 $$ $$ = 1.15718 - 1 = 0.15718 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Effective Cost} \approx 15.72\%} $$ --- **(b) Five Key Features of Trade Credit (4 marks):** 1. **Spontaneous Source** — arises automatically with each purchase; no separate negotiation needed. 2. **Flexibility** — the firm can decide when to pay (within the credit period). 3. **No Explicit Interest** — the cost is hidden in the discount terms. 4. **Costly if Discount Forfeited** — effective cost can exceed 30–40% p.a. 5. **Affects Credit Rating** — consistent late payments may reduce future credit availability. **Conditions to Forgo the Discount (3 marks):** A firm should *forgo* the cash discount and use the trade credit only when: 1. The **firm's effective borrowing cost from banks/markets is greater** than the cost of trade credit. 2. The firm has a **proven, long-standing relationship** with the supplier and forfeiting the discount will **not jeopardize future supplies**. 3. The firm has **insufficient working capital** and cannot afford to pay early, even with bank borrowing. 4. The opportunity cost of using internal funds is **lower** than the cost of trade credit. 5. There is an **inflationary environment** where future rupee payment is less painful than present cash outflow. --- #### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** What is a **Commercial Paper (CP)**? Explain the **step-by-step process** of issuing a CP in India, along with its key **features and limitations**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A company issues a CP with a **face value of ₹5,00,000**, an **issue price of ₹4,95,000**, and a **maturity of 90 days**. Compute the annualized cost of the CP to the issuing company. Compare this with the cost of a bank loan at 11.5% p.a. Which option is preferable? *(7 marks)* --- **MODEL SOLUTION** **(a) Commercial Paper – Definition, Process, Features, Limitations (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** A Commercial Paper is an **unsecured short-term negotiable instrument** issued by highly rated corporates directly in the money market to raise working capital funds. **Step-by-Step Process (3 marks):** 1. **Eligibility Check** — The company must have a minimum credit rating of **A1+** and net tangible worth ≥ ₹4 crore. 2. **Appointment of Agents** — The issuer appoints an *Issuing and Paying Agent* (IPA), usually a scheduled bank. 3. **Decide Issuance Details** — Face value (min ₹5 lakh), tenure (15–365 days), and number of tranches. 4. **Pricing** — Issued at a *discount* to face value. Yield is determined by demand-supply. 5. **Subscription** — Investors (banks, MFs, corporates, FIIs, NRIs) subscribe at the issue price. 6. **Redemption** — At maturity, the IPA pays the face value to investors on behalf of the issuer. **Features (1.5 marks):** - Unsecured, no collateral required. - Issued at a discount, redeemed at par. - Highly liquid (can be traded in secondary market). - Issued only by financially strong companies (A1+ rated). - Minimum investment ₹5 lakh (face value). **Limitations (1.5 marks):** - Only large, listed, highly rated firms can issue. - Issue size may not be feasible for small firms. - Requires stamp duty and SEBI registration fees. - Cannot be issued for more than 1 year. - Default risk — though small, exists. --- **(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):** **Given:** - Face Value $F$ = ₹5,00,000 - Issue Price $P$ = ₹4,95,000 - Tenure $t$ = 90 days **Step 1:** Discount (Interest for the company) = $F - P$ = ₹5,000. **Step 2:** Periodic rate (for 90 days): $$ r_{90} = \frac{F - P}{P} = \frac{5000}{4,95,000} = 0.01010 \text{ or } 1.010\% $$ **Step 3:** Annualizing using 365-day convention (SEBI / CP convention): $$ \text{Cost of CP (annualized)} = \frac{5000}{4,95,000} \times \frac{365}{90} $$ $$ = 0.01010 \times 4.0556 = 0.04096 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Cost of CP} \approx 4.10\% \text{ p.a.}} $$ **Comparison with Bank Loan (2 marks):** | Option | Effective Cost | |---|---| | Commercial Paper | **4.10%** | | Bank Loan | **11.50%** | **Decision (1 mark):** Since the **CP cost (4.10%) is significantly lower** than the bank loan cost (11.50%), and the company is eligible (assumed A1+ rated), the **CP option is preferable**. However, the firm must consider stamp duty, arrangement fees, and the credit-rating renewal cost before a final decision. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Day Convention Confusion:** Students often use *360 days* for CP cost computation. In India, **SEBI convention is 365 days** for CP. Using 360 will yield an incorrect annualized rate. > > 2. **Discount vs Yield Confusion:** T-Bill yield uses *face value* in denominator (discount yield), but CP annualized yield uses *issue price* (Bond Equivalent Yield). Mixing these will lead to wrong answers. > > 3. **Trade Credit Cost Period:** For annualization, the exponent should be $\frac{360}{n-t}$ where $(n-t)$ is the **additional days of credit obtained**. Students often wrongly use *n* instead of *(n-t)*. > > 4. **No Step-Marking:** KTU awards **step marks only if each formula is written before substitution**. Always write the formula, substitute, and then compute. > > 5. **CP Eligibility:** Many students write "CP is for all companies" — this is **wrong**. CP requires **A1+ rating** and a **minimum net worth of ₹4 crore** (for unlisted cos). > > 6. **Cash Credit vs OD:** Many students use the terms interchangeably in the exam — this **costs 1 full mark**. Always specify that CC is **stock-linked** and OD is **general creditworthiness**. --- ### ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Short-term sources** finance current assets with maturity ≤ 1 year. - **Bank sources** include CC (drawing power based), OD (on-demand), STL (lump-sum), and Bill Discounting (receivables). - **Trade credit** is spontaneous, with cost hidden in discount terms; cost can be **nominal** or **effective (annualized)**. - **Overdraft** is a flexible facility with floating-rate interest; the effective cost is **higher than nominal** due to compounding. - **Commercial Paper** is a money-market instrument, unsecured, A1+ rated, issued at discount, redeemed at par. - **Money Market** provides liquidity through T-Bills, CDs, CBLO, and Repo — all discount-based. - **Mutual Fund Liquid Schemes** are used to park surplus cash for better returns than savings accounts. - **Decision rule:** Always pick the source with the **lowest effective cost**, considering flexibility, credit impact, and convenience. - **KTU favourite numericals:** Trade credit cost (2/10 net 30 type) and CP annualized cost. - **Day convention:** 360 days for bank discount, **365 days** for CP and Bond Equivalent Yield. - **Eligibility mantra:** CP = A1+ + ₹4 Cr net worth + ₹5 L minimum face value. --- > 🎓 **End of Module 2 Notes — Short-Term Sources of Finance** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PEIET743 — Financial Management*

topics.title: Financial Statement and Analysis: Introduction, Concepts of Financial Accounting, Financial Statements, and Financial Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Financial Statement and Analysis: Introduction, Concepts of Financial Accounting, Financial Statements, and Financial Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Financial Statement Analysis (FSA)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Financial Statement Analysis (FSA)** is the systematic process of examining, reviewing, and interpreting the financial statements of an organization to make better economic decisions. It involves evaluating the financial health, performance, and prospects of a business entity through the analysis of its Balance Sheet, Income Statement, Cash Flow Statement, and Statement of Changes in Equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Financial Statement Analysis is the process of reviewing and evaluating a company's financial statements (such as the balance sheet, income statement, and cash flow statement) to gain an understanding of its financial health, performance, and prospects. It helps stakeholders like investors, creditors, and management make informed economic decisions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a doctor examining a patient. Before prescribing any treatment, you review multiple medical reports — blood test results, X-rays, ECG reports — to understand the patient's overall health. **Financial Statement Analysis works exactly the same way for a business.**

- The **Balance Sheet** is like an X-ray showing the structural health of the company.
- The **Income Statement** is like a blood test showing performance and vitality.
- The **Cash Flow Statement** is like an ECG showing the live "pulse" of money moving in and out.

Just as a doctor derives a diagnosis by **comparing** today's reports with previous ones and with healthy benchmarks, a financial analyst derives insights by **comparing** financial data across time, across firms, and against industry standards.

---

## 1.2 Objectives of Financial Statement Analysis

The primary objectives of FSA are:

1. **Assessing Profitability** — Determining whether the firm is earning sufficient returns on its investments.
2. **Evaluating Solvency** — Assessing the long-term debt repayment capability of the business.
3. **Judging Operational Efficiency** — Understanding how effectively management utilizes resources.
4. **Determining Liquidity Position** — Evaluating the ability to meet short-term obligations.
5. **Forecasting Future Performance** — Using historical trends to predict future financial outcomes.
6. **Comparing Performance** — Benchmarking against competitors and industry standards.
7. **Identifying Trends** — Spotting patterns in revenue, expenses, and asset utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Stakeholders of FSA:** Shareholders, Creditors, Lenders, Management, Government, Tax Authorities, Employees, Suppliers, Customers, and the General Public.

---

## 1.3 Concepts of Financial Accounting

**Financial Accounting** is the branch of accounting that involves recording, summarizing, and reporting the myriad transactions arising from business operations over a period of time. It is the **language of business**, providing standardized financial information to external users.

### 1.3.1 Core Concepts (The Foundation Pillars)

| Concept | Definition | Intuitive Meaning |
|---------|------------|-------------------|
| **Business Entity Concept** | Business is treated as a separate entity from its owners. | Your business has its own identity — its money is NOT your money. |
| **Money Measurement Concept** | Only monetary transactions are recorded. | The canteen's pleasant smell cannot appear in the books — only its revenue can! |
| **Going Concern Concept** | Business is assumed to continue indefinitely. | We value assets at cost, not at fire-sale price, because we assume long life. |
| **Accounting Period Concept** | Life of business is divided into time periods (usually 1 year). | We slice the infinite life into reportable yearly pieces. |
| **Cost Concept** | Assets are recorded at their acquisition cost. | The land bought in 1990 for ₹1 lakh still shows ₹1 lakh, even if it's worth ₹1 crore now. |
| **Dual Aspect (Duality) Concept** | Every transaction has two aspects — Debit and Credit. | "You can't take from one hand without giving to the other." Total Assets = Total Liabilities. |
| **Revenue Recognition (Realization) Concept** | Revenue is recognized when it is earned, not when cash is received. | A sale on credit counts as revenue the moment it happens. |
| **Matching Concept** | Expenses are matched with revenues of the same period. | The salary of a salesperson who sold this year's goods goes to THIS year's P&L. |
| **Full Disclosure Concept** | All material information must be disclosed. | No hiding bad news — disclose everything relevant in notes to accounts. |
| **Consistency Concept** | Same accounting methods are used year after year. | Don't change your scoring rules mid-game. |
| **Conservatism (Prudence) Concept** | Anticipate no profits but provide for all losses. | Don't count your chickens before they hatch, but count the predators! |
| **Materiality Concept** | Only material/significant events are reported separately. | A ₹5 pen for a ₹1000 crore company is irrelevant detail. |
