| **Accrual Concept** | Income & expenses are recorded as they occur, regardless of cash flow. | Bookings today for next month = today's revenue. |

### 1.3.2 Systems of Accounting

There are three main systems of recording financial transactions:

1. **Cash System of Accounting** — Transactions are recorded only when cash is received or paid.
2. **Accrual/Mercantile System of Accounting** — Transactions are recorded when they occur, irrespective of cash flow. *(Most commonly used and KTU-preferred.)*
3. **Mixed/Hybrid System** — Combines features of both cash and accrual systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Indian companies are legally required to follow the **Accrual System** under the Companies Act, 2013, and Accounting Standards issued by ICAI.

### 1.3.3 Accounting Standards (IND AS / IFRS)

**Accounting Standards** are the **rulebook** ensuring that financial statements are prepared in a consistent, comparable, and transparent manner.

- **IND AS** (Indian Accounting Standards) — applicable in India, largely converged with **IFRS** (International Financial Reporting Standards).
- Issued by **ICAI** (Institute of Chartered Accountants of India).
- The **Accounting Standards Board (ASB)** of ICAI formulates these standards.

---

## 1.4 Users of Financial Statements

Different stakeholders use financial statements for different purposes:

- **Internal Users (Management)** — Planning, controlling, and decision-making.
- **External Users**:
  - **Investors** — Investment decisions.
  - **Creditors/Lenders** — Credit and loan decisions.
  - **Government** — Tax assessment, policy-making.
  - **Employees** — Job security, bonus calculations.
  - **Public** — Social and economic impact.
  - **Researchers** — Academic and industry analysis.

---

## 1.5 Limitations of Financial Statement Analysis

Despite its utility, FSA has certain inherent limitations:

1. **Based on Historical Data** — Past may not predict future.
2. **Ignores Price Level Changes** — Inflation distorts real comparability.
3. **Window Dressing** — Companies may manipulate statements near year-end.
4. **Personal Bias of Analyst** — Different analysts may draw different conclusions.
5. **Qualitative Factors Ignored** — Cannot quantify employee morale, brand value, etc.
6. **Accounting Policies Vary** — Different depreciation methods affect comparability.
7. **Affected by One-time Items** — Extraordinary items may distort trends.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Financial Statements — The Three Pillars

Financial statements are the **formal records** of the financial activities and position of a business. The three primary financial statements (as per KTU 2024 syllabus and Companies Act 2013) are:

### 2.1.1 The Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position)

**Definition:** A balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time. It is the **photograph** of the business's financial position.

**Fundamental Equation (The Foundation of Accounting):**

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity (Net Worth)}$$

**Structure of a Balance Sheet (Vertical Format — KTU Preferred):**

| **Liabilities Side** | **Assets Side** |
|----------------------|------------------|
| **Shareholders' Funds** | **Non-Current Assets** |
| Equity Share Capital | Fixed Assets (Tangible + Intangible) |
| Preference Share Capital | Capital Work-in-Progress |
| Reserves & Surplus | Long-term Investments |
| **Non-Current Liabilities** | **Current Assets** |
| Long-term Borrowings | Inventories |
| Deferred Tax Liabilities | Trade Receivables (Debtors) |
| Long-term Provisions | Cash & Cash Equivalents |
| **Current Liabilities** | Short-term Loans & Advances |
| Short-term Borrowings | Other Current Assets |
| Trade Payables (Creditors) | |
| Other Current Liabilities | |
| Short-term Provisions | |

**Real-World Utility:** Used by banks to decide loan eligibility, by investors to assess capital structure, and by management for asset-liability management.

---

### 2.1.2 The Income Statement (Statement of Profit & Loss)

**Definition:** The Income Statement shows the company's **revenues, expenses, and profits/losses** over a specific period (usually one financial year). It is the **movie** of business performance.

**Format (Vertical — KTU Standard):**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|-------------|------------|
| Revenue from Operations (Net Sales) | XXX |
| Less: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | (XXX) |
| **Gross Profit** | **XXX** |
| Less: Operating Expenses (Admin, Selling, etc.) | (XXX) |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | **XXX** |
| Add: Non-Operating Income | XXX |
| Less: Non-Operating Expenses | (XXX) |
| **Profit Before Interest & Tax (PBIT)** | **XXX** |
| Less: Interest | (XXX) |
| **Profit Before Tax (PBT)** | **XXX** |
| Less: Tax | (XXX) |
| **Profit After Tax (PAT) / Net Profit** | **XXX** |

**Real-World Utility:** Used to evaluate profitability, operational efficiency, and tax planning.

---

### 2.1.3 The Cash Flow Statement (CFS)

**Definition:** A Cash Flow Statement provides a detailed picture of all cash inflows and outflows during a financial period, categorized into **Operating, Investing, and Financing** activities.

**The Three Sections of Cash Flow:**

1. **Cash Flow from Operating Activities (CFO)** — Cash generated from core business operations.
2. **Cash Flow from Investing Activities (CFI)** — Cash used in or generated from purchase/sale of long-term assets.
3. **Cash Flow from Financing Activities (CFF)** — Cash flow related to borrowing, issuing shares, paying dividends.

**Reconciliation Formula:**

$$\text{Net Increase/Decrease in Cash} = \text{CFO} + \text{CFI} + \text{CFF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Note:** A company can be profitable on paper (positive PAT) but cash-starved (negative CFO) — this is why Cash Flow Analysis is critical.

---

## 2.2 Types / Methods of Financial Analysis

Financial Analysis can be classified in **four primary ways**, all of which are KTU syllabus mandates:

### 2.2.1 Comparative Financial Statements

**Definition:** Financial statements prepared in a way that allows for comparison of figures across multiple periods (usually 2 or 3 years).

**Tools Used:**
1. **Comparative Income Statement** — Compares P&L items year-on-year.
2. **Comparative Balance Sheet** — Compares assets, liabilities, equity year-on-year.

**Technique:** Absolute change and Percentage change are calculated for each item.

$$\text{Absolute Change} = \text{Current Year Amount} - \text{Base Year Amount}$$

$$\text{Percentage Change} = \left( \frac{\text{Absolute Change}}{\text{Base Year Amount}} \right) \times 100$$

**Use Case:** Identifies growth rates, rising expenses, and shifting capital structures.

---

### 2.2.2 Common-Size Financial Statements

**Definition:** Each item in the financial statement is expressed as a **percentage of a common base** — for the Income Statement, the base is **Net Sales**; for the Balance Sheet, the base is **Total Assets** (or Total Liabilities).

**Formulas:**

$$\text{Common-Size \% (Income Statement)} = \left( \frac{\text{Each Item}}{\text{Net Sales}} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Common-Size \% (Balance Sheet)} = \left( \frac{\text{Each Item}}{\text{Total Assets}} \right) \times 100$$

**Use Case:** Eliminates size differences between firms, enabling **inter-firm comparison** and revealing structural patterns.

---

### 2.2.3 Trend Analysis (Time Series Analysis)

**Definition:** Trend analysis studies the **movement of financial data over a series of years** (typically 5–10 years) to identify patterns and forecast future values.

**Formula:**

$$\text{Trend Percentage} = \left( \frac{\text{Current Year Value}}{\text{Base Year Value}} \right) \times 100$$

**Base Year** = The year chosen as the reference point (Trend Percentage = 100%).

**Use Case:** Long-term forecasting, strategic planning, and seasonality detection.

---

### 2.2.4 Ratio Analysis (The Heart of FSA)

**Definition:** Ratio analysis expresses the **mathematical relationship** between two financial figures to evaluate various aspects of a company's performance.

**Classification of Ratios (KTU Standard — 4 Categories):**

| Category | Purpose | Key Ratios |
|----------|---------|------------|
| **Liquidity Ratios** | Short-term solvency | Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, Cash Ratio |
| **Profitability Ratios** | Earnings capacity | GP Ratio, NP Ratio, ROA, ROE, ROCE |
| **Solvency Ratios** | Long-term solvency | Debt-Equity, Interest Coverage, Debt-Asset |
| **Activity/Turnover Ratios** | Efficiency | Stock Turnover, Debtors Turnover, Asset Turnover |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all critical formulas** for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these thoroughly.

| # | Ratio | Formula | Standard Benchmark |
|---|-------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Current Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **2 : 1** (Ideal) |
| 2 | **Quick/Liquid Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Quick Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} = \dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Exp.}}{\text{CL}}$ | **1 : 1** (Ideal) |
| 3 | **Super Quick / Cash Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **0.5 : 1** |
| 4 | **Absolute Liquid Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Absolute Liquid Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | **0.2 : 1** |
| 5 | **Gross Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry Specific |
| 6 | **Net Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (PAT)}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry Specific |
| 7 | **Operating Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry Specific |
| 8 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit before Interest \& Tax}}{\text{Total Investment (LT Funds)}} \times 100$ | Higher is better |
| 9 | **Return on Equity (ROE)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$ | Higher is better |
| 10 | **Return on Assets (ROA)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit after Tax}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times 100$ | Higher is better |
| 11 | **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit after Tax} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{Number of Equity Shares}}$ | Higher is better |
| 12 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ | **2 : 1** (Max) |
| 13 | **Debt-Asset Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | < 0.5 preferred |
| 14 | **Proprietary Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Higher is better |
| 15 | **Interest Coverage Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | **2 to 3** (Min) |
| 16 | **Stock/Inventory Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Higher is better |
| 17 | **Debtors/Receivables Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Debtors}}$ | Higher is better |
| 18 | **Average Collection Period** | $\dfrac{365 \text{ days}}{\text{Debtors Turnover Ratio}}$ | Lower is better |
| 19 | **Creditors/Payables Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Credit Purchases}}{\text{Average Creditors}}$ | Higher is better |
| 20 | **Average Payment Period** | $\dfrac{365}{\text{Creditors Turnover}}$ | Lower is better |
| 21 | **Fixed Asset Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Net Fixed Assets}}$ | Higher is better |
| 22 | **Total Asset Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Higher is better |
| 23 | **Working Capital Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Net Working Capital}}$ | Higher is better |
| 24 | **Capital Gearing Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Interest Bearing Capital}}{\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds}}$ | Lower is better |
| 25 | **Dividend Payout Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Dividend per Share}}{\text{Earnings per Share}} \times 100$ | Variable |
| 26 | **Dividend Yield Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Dividend per Share}}{\text{Market Value per Share}} \times 100$ | Variable |
| 27 | **Price-Earnings (P/E) Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Market Value per Share}}{\text{Earnings per Share}}$ | Variable |
| 28 | **Net Working Capital** | $\text{Current Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities}$ | Positive ideal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** When "Average Inventory" is not specified, use **Closing Inventory**. The KTU 2024 Scheme often tests with both options — read the question carefully!

---

## 2.4 DuPont Analysis (Advanced — High-Value Topic)

The **DuPont Identity** breaks down ROE into its components to identify the true driver of returns.

**Three-Step DuPont Formula:**

$$\text{ROE} = \text{Net Profit Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover} \times \text{Equity Multiplier}$$

Where:
- **Net Profit Margin** = $\dfrac{\text{Net Income}}{\text{Net Sales}}$
- **Asset Turnover** = $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}}$
- **Equity Multiplier** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$

**Five-Step DuPont (Extended):**

$$\text{ROE} = \left( \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Sales}} \right) \times \left( \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}} \right) \times \left( \frac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}} \right) \times \left( \frac{\text{EBT}}{\text{EBIT}} \right) \times \left( \frac{\text{NI}}{\text{EBT}} \right)$$

**Use Case:** Diagnoses whether ROE is driven by profitability, efficiency, or leverage.

---

## 2.5 The Operating Cycle Concept

The **Operating Cycle** is the average time between acquiring inventory and collecting cash from sales.

$$\text{Operating Cycle} = \text{Inventory Period} + \text{Receivables Period}$$

$$\text{Operating Cycle} = \frac{365}{\text{Stock Turnover}} + \frac{365}{\text{Debtors Turnover}}$$

$$\text{Cash Cycle} = \text{Operating Cycle} - \text{Creditors Period}$$

**Use Case:** A shorter operating cycle indicates efficient working capital management.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Comprehensive Ratio Analysis from a Balance Sheet & P&L

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU ESE standard question format (14 marks).** We will solve this exhaustively, showing every single arithmetic step.

### Given Data (Hypothetical Company — XYZ Ltd.)

**Balance Sheet as on 31st March 2024:**

| Liabilities | Amount (₹) | Assets | Amount (₹) |
|-------------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Equity Share Capital (1,00,000 shares of ₹10 each) | 10,00,000 | Land & Building | 8,00,000 |
| 10% Preference Share Capital | 2,00,000 | Plant & Machinery | 5,00,000 |
| General Reserve | 3,00,000 | Furniture | 1,00,000 |
| P&L Account (Balance) | 1,00,000 | Stock | 4,00,000 |
| 12% Debentures | 6,00,000 | Debtors | 3,00,000 |
| Trade Creditors | 2,00,000 | Bills Receivable | 1,00,000 |
| Bills Payable | 1,00,000 | Cash in Hand | 50,000 |
| Bank Overdraft | 1,00,000 | Cash at Bank | 1,50,000 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 50,000 | Prepaid Expenses | 1,00,000 |
| Provision for Tax | 1,00,000 | | |
| **Total** | **27,50,000** | **Total** | **27,50,000** |

**Income Statement for the year ended 31st March 2024:**

| Particulars | Amount (₹) |
|-------------|-----------|
| Net Sales (Revenue from Operations) | 20,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) | 12,00,000 |
| Gross Profit | 8,00,000 |
| Office & Administrative Expenses | 2,00,000 |
| Selling & Distribution Expenses | 1,50,000 |
| Operating Profit (EBIT) | 4,50,000 |
| Interest on Debentures (12% of 6,00,000) | 72,000 |
| Profit Before Tax (PBT) | 3,78,000 |
| Provision for Tax (25%) | 94,500 |
| Profit After Tax (PAT) | 2,83,500 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step Calculation of All Ratios

#### Step 1: Pre-requisite — Identify Components

**Current Assets (CA) Calculation:**

$$\text{CA} = \text{Stock} + \text{Debtors} + \text{Bills Receivable} + \text{Cash in Hand} + \text{Cash at Bank} + \text{Prepaid Expenses}$$

$$\text{CA} = 4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{CA} = 11{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Current Liabilities (CL) Calculation:**

$$\text{CL} = \text{Trade Creditors} + \text{Bills Payable} + \text{Bank Overdraft} + \text{Outstanding Expenses} + \text{Provision for Tax}$$

$$\text{CL} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{CL} = 5{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Quick Assets (QA) Calculation:**

$$\text{QA} = \text{CA} - \text{Stock} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}$$

$$\text{QA} = 11{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Total Assets (TA) Calculation:**

$$\text{TA} = \text{Land \& Building} + \text{Plant \& Machinery} + \text{Furniture} + \text{CA}$$

$$\text{TA} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Shareholders' Equity (Net Worth) Calculation:**

$$\text{Equity} = \text{Equity Share Capital} + \text{Preference Share Capital} + \text{General Reserve} + \text{P\&L Balance}$$

$$\text{Equity} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Long-term Debt (LTD) Calculation:**

$$\text{LTD} = 12\% \text{ Debentures} = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

#### Step 2: Liquidity Ratios

**(a) Current Ratio:**

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{11{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} = 2 : 1$$

*Interpretation:* **Ideal** — Company has ₹2 of current assets for every ₹1 of current liabilities.

**(b) Quick Ratio:**

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{\text{QA}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} = 1.09 : 1$$

*Interpretation:* **Slightly above ideal** (1:1) — Healthy immediate liquidity.

**(c) Cash Ratio:**

$$\text{Cash Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cash in Hand} + \text{Cash at Bank}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000}{5{,}50{,}000} = 0.36 : 1$$

*Interpretation:* Below standard benchmark (0.5:1) — acceptable but limited pure cash buffer.

#### Step 3: Profitability Ratios

**(a) Gross Profit Ratio:**

$$\text{GP Ratio} = \frac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**(b) Net Profit Ratio:**

$$\text{NP Ratio} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}83{,}500}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.18\%$$

**(c) Operating Profit Ratio:**

$$\text{OP Ratio} = \frac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 22.5\%$$

**(d) Return on Equity (ROE):**

$$\text{ROE} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}83{,}500}{16{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 17.72\%$$

**(e) Return on Investment (ROI):**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{PBIT}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{3{,}78{,}000 + 72{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20.45\%$$

**(f) Earnings Per Share (EPS):**

$$\text{EPS} = \frac{\text{PAT} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$$

$$\text{Preference Dividend} = 10\% \text{ of } 2{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}000$$

$$\text{EPS} = \frac{2{,}83{,}500 - 20{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = \frac{2{,}63{,}500}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹2.635 \text{ per share}$$

**(g) Return on Assets (ROA):**

$$\text{ROA} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Total Assets}} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}83{,}500}{25{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 11.34\%$$

#### Step 4: Solvency Ratios

**(a) Debt-Equity Ratio:**

$$\text{D/E} = \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Equity}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.375 : 1$$

*Interpretation:* Well within the 2:1 benchmark — **low financial risk**.

**(b) Debt-Asset Ratio:**

$$\text{D/A} = \frac{\text{LTD}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.24 \text{ or } 24\%$$

**(c) Interest Coverage Ratio:**

$$\text{ICR} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest}} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{72{,}000} = 6.25 \text{ times}$$

*Interpretation:* Earnings cover interest **6.25 times** — Excellent debt-servicing capacity (benchmark: 2-3 times).

**(d) Proprietary Ratio:**

$$\text{Proprietary Ratio} = \frac{\text{Equity}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{16{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.64 \text{ or } 64\%$$

#### Step 5: Activity / Turnover Ratios

**(a) Stock/Inventory Turnover Ratio:**

$$\text{Stock Turnover} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Stock}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 3 \text{ times}$$

**(b) Debtors Turnover Ratio:**

$$\text{Debtors Turnover} = \frac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Debtors}}$$

$$\text{Assuming Net Credit Sales} = \text{Net Sales} = 20{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Debtors Turnover} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 6.67 \text{ times}$$

**(c) Average Collection Period:**

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{365}{\text{Debtors Turnover}} = \frac{365}{6.67} = 54.7 \text{ days}$$

**(d) Total Asset Turnover Ratio:**

$$\text{TAT} = \frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.8 \text{ times}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Common-Size Statement Preparation

### Given: ABC Ltd. Income Statement

| Particulars | 2023 (₹) | 2024 (₹) |
|-------------|----------|----------|
| Net Sales | 10,00,000 | 15,00,000 |
| COGS | 6,00,000 | 8,50,000 |
| Operating Expenses | 1,50,000 | 2,00,000 |
| Interest | 50,000 | 60,000 |
| Tax (25%) | 75,000 | 97,500 |
| **PAT** | **1,25,000** | **2,92,500** |

### Solution: Common-Size Statement (Base = Net Sales)

| Particulars | 2023 (₹) | 2023 % | 2024 (₹) | 2024 % |
|-------------|----------|--------|----------|--------|
| Net Sales | 10,00,000 | **100.00%** | 15,00,000 | **100.00%** |
| COGS | 6,00,000 | 60.00% | 8,50,000 | 56.67% |
| Gross Profit | 4,00,000 | 40.00% | 6,50,000 | 43.33% |
| Operating Expenses | 1,50,000 | 15.00% | 2,00,000 | 13.33% |
| Operating Profit | 2,50,000 | 25.00% | 4,50,000 | 30.00% |
| Interest | 50,000 | 5.00% | 60,000 | 4.00% |
| PBT | 2,00,000 | 20.00% | 3,90,000 | 26.00% |
| Tax | 75,000 | 7.50% | 97,500 | 6.50% |
| PAT | 1,25,000 | 12.50% | 2,92,500 | 19.50% |

**Calculation for COGS % (2024):**

$$\text{COGS \%} = \frac{8{,}50{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 56.67\%$$

**Calculation for PAT % (2024):**

$$\text{PAT \%} = \frac{2{,}92{,}500}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 19.50\%$$

### Interpretation
- **COGS** decreased from **60% → 56.67%** → Cost efficiency improved.
- **Operating Expenses** decreased from **15% → 13.33%** → Better control.
- **PAT %** jumped from **12.50% → 19.50%** → Significant improvement in bottom-line profitability.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DuPont Analysis

### Given Data

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Net Sales | 50,00,000 |
| PAT | 5,00,000 |
| Total Assets | 25,00,000 |
| Shareholders' Equity | 10,00,000 |

### Step 1: Calculate Each Component

**Net Profit Margin (NPM):**

$$\text{NPM} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} = 0.10 \text{ or } 10\%$$

**Asset Turnover (AT):**

$$\text{AT} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 2.0$$

**Equity Multiplier (EM):**

$$\text{EM} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 2.5$$

### Step 2: Apply DuPont Formula

$$\text{ROE} = \text{NPM} \times \text{AT} \times \text{EM} = 0.10 \times 2.0 \times 2.5 = 0.50 \text{ or } 50\%$$

### Verification (Direct Calculation)

$$\text{ROE} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Equity}} \times 100 = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 50\% \quad \checkmark$$

### Interpretation
Out of the 50% ROE:
- **10%** comes from profit margin (operational profitability).
- **2x leverage from asset turnover** (efficiency).
- **2.5x leverage from equity multiplier** (use of debt).

> [!NOTE]
> A high Equity Multiplier (>2) signals aggressive use of debt to amplify shareholder returns.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Trend Analysis

### Given: Sales Data Over 5 Years (Base Year: 2020)

| Year | Sales (₹ Lakhs) |
|------|-----------------|
| 2020 | 50 (Base) |
| 2021 | 60 |
| 2022 | 75 |
| 2023 | 90 |
| 2024 | 110 |

### Trend Percentage Calculation

| Year | Sales (₹ L) | Trend % = (Sales / Base Sales) × 100 |
|------|-------------|---------------------------------------|
| 2020 | 50 | $(50/50) \times 100 = 100\%$ |
| 2021 | 60 | $(60/50) \times 100 = 120\%$ |
| 2022 | 75 | $(75/50) \times 100 = 150\%$ |
| 2023 | 90 | $(90/50) \times 100 = 180\%$ |
| 2024 | 110 | $(110/50) \times 100 = 220\%$ |

### Trend Equation (Linear Forecast)

For 2025 (next year):

$$\text{Growth Rate (CAGR)} = \left( \frac{110}{50} \right)^{1/4} - 1 = (2.2)^{0.25} - 1 = 1.2179 - 1 = 0.2179 = 21.79\%$$

$$\text{Forecast for 2025} = 110 \times 1.2179 = 133.97 \text{ Lakhs} \approx ₹134 \text{ Lakhs}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define Financial Statement Analysis. List its objectives.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Financial Statement Analysis (FSA) is the process of examining, reviewing, and interpreting a company's financial statements (Balance Sheet, Income Statement, Cash Flow Statement) to assess its financial health, performance, and prospects.

**Objectives (Any 4 to be stated):**

1. **Assessing Profitability** — To evaluate the firm's earning capacity and return on investments.
2. **Judging Liquidity** — To determine the firm's ability to meet short-term obligations.
3. **Evaluating Solvency** — To assess long-term financial stability and debt-servicing capacity.
4. **Determining Operational Efficiency** — To analyze how effectively management uses resources.
5. **Forecasting Future Performance** — To project future trends based on historical data.
6. **Inter-firm Comparison** — To compare financial performance with peer companies.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` Differentiate between Accrual System and Cash System of Accounting.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Accrual System | Cash System |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| **Basis of Recording** | Records transactions when they occur (i.e., when revenue is earned or expense is incurred) | Records transactions only when cash is received or paid |
| **Income Recognition** | Based on accrual concept (irrespective of cash inflow) | Based on actual cash receipt |
| **Expense Recognition** | Recorded in the period to which it relates | Recorded only on cash payment |
| **Accuracy** | More accurate matching of revenue & expenses | May distort profit figures |
| **Legal Status** | Mandatory under Companies Act, 2013 | Used by small professionals/not-for-profits |
| **Example** | Credit sale booked in books on sale date | Credit sale booked only when cash is collected |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must attempt **ONE full question (with both sub-parts)** from a Module Internal Choice.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the various **tools/methods of Financial Statement Analysis** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Financial Statement Analysis employs the following systematic tools:

**1. Comparative Financial Statements (2 Marks)**
- These are statements showing financial data for **two or more periods side by side**.
- Two types: Comparative Income Statement & Comparative Balance Sheet.
- **Absolute Change** = Current Year − Base Year.
- **Percentage Change** = (Absolute Change / Base Year) × 100.
- **Utility:** Identifies growth/decline trends in revenue, expenses, assets, and liabilities.

**2. Common-Size Financial Statements (1.5 Marks)**
- Each item is expressed as a **percentage of a common base**.
- For P&L: Base = Net Sales. For Balance Sheet: Base = Total Assets.
- **Utility:** Enables **inter-firm comparison** by neutralizing size differences.

**3. Trend Analysis (1.5 Marks)**
- Multi-year (usually 5–10 years) movement of financial data.
- A base year is selected (Trend % = 100).
- **Trend %** = (Current Year / Base Year) × 100.
- **Utility:** Long-term forecasting and pattern recognition.

**4. Ratio Analysis (2 Marks)**
- The most widely used tool, expressing mathematical relationships between two financial items.
- **Four categories:** Liquidity, Profitability, Solvency, Activity.
- **Utility:** Simplifies complex data into meaningful, comparable metrics.

---

**Q. (b)** From the following data, prepare a **Common-Size Income Statement** and comment. **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | 2022-23 (₹) | 2023-24 (₹) |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Sales | 8,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Cost of Sales | 4,80,000 | 5,50,000 |
| Office Expenses | 80,000 | 90,000 |
| Selling Expenses | 40,000 | 50,000 |
| Interest | 20,000 | 30,000 |
| Tax (25%) | 47,500 | 70,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute Absolute Profit Figures.

| Particulars | 2022-23 (₹) | 2023-24 (₹) |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Sales | 8,00,000 | 10,00,000 |
| Less: Cost of Sales | (4,80,000) | (5,50,000) |
| **Gross Profit** | **3,20,000** | **4,50,000** |
| Less: Office Expenses | (80,000) | (90,000) |
| Less: Selling Expenses | (40,000) | (50,000) |
| **Operating Profit** | **2,00,000** | **3,10,000** |
| Less: Interest | (20,000) | (30,000) |
| **PBT** | **1,80,000** | **2,80,000** |
| Less: Tax (25%) | (47,500) | (70,000) |
| **PAT** | **1,32,500** | **2,10,000** |

**Step 2:** Convert Each Item to % of Sales.

| Item | 2022-23 (₹) | 2022-23 (%) | 2023-24 (₹) | 2023-24 (%) |
|------|--------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| Sales | 8,00,000 | **100.00%** | 10,00,000 | **100.00%** |
| Cost of Sales | 4,80,000 | 60.00% | 5,50,000 | 55.00% |
| Gross Profit | 3,20,000 | 40.00% | 4,50,000 | 45.00% |
| Office Expenses | 80,000 | 10.00% | 90,000 | 9.00% |
| Selling Expenses | 40,000 | 5.00% | 50,000 | 5.00% |
| Operating Profit | 2,00,000 | 25.00% | 3,10,000 | 31.00% |
| Interest | 20,000 | 2.50% | 30,000 | 3.00% |
| PBT | 1,80,000 | 22.50% | 2,80,000 | 28.00% |
| Tax | 47,500 | 5.94% | 70,000 | 7.00% |
| PAT | 1,32,500 | 16.56% | 2,10,000 | 21.00% |

**Step 3: Sample Computation (Verify)**

$$\text{COGS \% (2023-24)} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 55.00\%$$

$$\text{PAT \% (2023-24)} = \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 21.00\%$$

**Comments / Interpretation:**

1. **COGS declined** from 60% → 55% → Improved cost efficiency.
2. **Operating Profit % rose** from 25% → 31% → Strong operational performance.
3. **PAT % improved** from 16.56% → 21% → Significant growth in bottom-line profitability.
4. **Overall:** The company demonstrated **healthy financial improvement** in 2023-24.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q. (a)** What is a **Balance Sheet**? Explain the various **concepts of financial accounting** that govern its preparation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Balance Sheet — Definition (1 Mark):**
A Balance Sheet is a financial statement that presents a company's financial position at a specific point in time, listing its **Assets, Liabilities, and Shareholders' Equity** as of a particular date.

**Fundamental Equation (0.5 Mark):**

$$\text{Total Assets} = \text{Total Liabilities} + \text{Shareholders' Equity}$$

**Key Accounting Concepts Governing Balance Sheet (5.5 Marks):**

1. **Business Entity Concept (0.5 M):** Business is distinct from owner; assets belong to the company, not the proprietor.
2. **Going Concern Concept (0.5 M):** Assumes infinite life; assets valued at cost, not liquidation value.
3. **Money Measurement Concept (0.5 M):** Only monetary items are recorded; qualitative facts excluded.
4. **Cost Concept (0.5 M):** Assets recorded at acquisition cost; ignores market value fluctuations.
5. **Dual Aspect Concept (0.5 M):** Every transaction has dual effect; total debits = total credits, ensuring the balance sheet always balances.
6. **Accounting Period Concept (0.5 M):** Business life is split into equal periods (typically 1 year) for reporting.
7. **Consistency Concept (0.5 M):** Same accounting methods used across periods for comparability.
8. **Conservatism Concept (0.5 M):** Anticipate losses, not profits; assets are never overstated.
9. **Full Disclosure Concept (0.5 M):** All material information is disclosed in notes to accounts.
10. **Materiality Concept (0.5 M):** Only material items are reported separately; immaterial items are clubbed.
11. **Accrual Concept (0.5 M):** Transactions recorded when they occur, not when cash is exchanged.

---

**Q. (b)** From the data given below, calculate: **(i) Current Ratio, (ii) Quick Ratio, (iii) Debt-Equity Ratio, (iv) Return on Equity, (v) Inventory Turnover Ratio, (vi) Debtors Turnover Ratio, (vii) Average Collection Period. (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Current Assets | 4,00,000 |
| Inventory | 1,50,000 |
| Current Liabilities | 2,00,000 |
| Long-term Debts | 3,00,000 |
| Shareholders' Equity | 5,00,000 |
| Net Sales | 12,00,000 |
| COGS | 8,00,000 |
| PAT | 1,00,000 |
| Debtors | 2,00,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Current Ratio:**

$$\text{Current Ratio} = \frac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{2 : 1}$$

*Comment:* Ideal — ₹2 of current assets per ₹1 of current liabilities.

**(ii) Quick Ratio:**

$$\text{Quick Ratio} = \frac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{CL}} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{1.25 : 1}$$

*Comment:* Above ideal (1:1) — Strong immediate liquidity.

**(iii) Debt-Equity Ratio:**

$$\text{D/E} = \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Equity}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{0.6 : 1}$$

*Comment:* Low — Conservative capital structure.

**(iv) Return on Equity (ROE):**

$$\text{ROE} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Equity}} \times 100 = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = \mathbf{20\%}$$

*Comment:* Excellent return to shareholders.

**(v) Inventory Turnover Ratio:**

$$\text{Stock Turnover} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Inventory}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = \mathbf{5.33 \text{ times}}$$

*Comment:* Inventory is turned over ~5 times a year.

**(vi) Debtors Turnover Ratio:**

$$\text{Debtors Turnover} = \frac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Debtors}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \mathbf{6 \text{ times}}$$

**(vii) Average Collection Period:**

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{365}{\text{Debtors Turnover}} = \frac{365}{6} = \mathbf{60.83 \text{ days}}$$

*Comment:* ~61 days to collect receivables.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 2-3 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Preference Dividend from PAT** before calculating EPS. Always do: $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{PAT} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$.
> 2. **Confusing "Net Sales" with "Total Sales"** — Always use **Net Sales** (after returns and discounts) in ratio formulas.
> 3. **Treating "Stock" as both Opening & Closing** — When "Average Inventory" is not specified, KTU allows use of Closing Inventory. Read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Wrong Current Asset identification** — Prepaid expenses are current assets; Provisions for Tax (if short-term) are current liabilities.
> 5. **Calculating ROI vs. ROE confusion** — ROI uses PBIT over Total Investment; ROE uses PAT over Equity only.
> 6. **Mixing up Cash Ratio formula** — Some students include Debtors in Cash Ratio. **No, Cash Ratio = (Cash + Marketable Securities) / Current Liabilities only.**
> 7. **Forgetting units in final answer** — Always write "**times**" for turnover ratios and "**years**" for periods.
> 8. **Skipping working notes** — KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for properly labeled working steps.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 2**

### 🔹 Core Concepts to Memorize
- ☐ **Dual Aspect Concept** → Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Equity (The golden equation)
- ☐ **13 Accounting Concepts** — Business Entity, Money Measurement, Going Concern, Accounting Period, Cost, Dual Aspect, Revenue Recognition, Matching, Full Disclosure, Consistency, Conservatism, Materiality, Accrual.
- ☐ **Accrual vs Cash** — Accrual is the standard in India.

### 🔹 Financial Statements — 3 Types
- ☐ **Balance Sheet** → Position at a point in time
- ☐ **Income Statement** → Performance over a period
- ☐ **Cash Flow Statement** → Cash movement (Operating, Investing, Financing)

### 🔹 Methods of Analysis
- ☐ **Comparative** → Absolute & % change (intra-firm, time-based)
- ☐ **Common-Size** → % of common base (inter-firm)
- ☐ **Trend** → % of base year (forecasting)
- ☐ **Ratio** → 4 categories, 25+ ratios

### 🔹 Critical Ratio Benchmarks
- ☐ **Current Ratio** → 2:1
- ☐ **Quick Ratio** → 1:1
- ☐ **Debt-Equity** → 2:1 (Max)
- ☐ **Interest Coverage** → 2-3 times (Min)
- ☐ **Quick Ratio Formula Trap** → (CA − Inventory − Prepaid) / CL

### 🔹 DuPont Identity (5 Components)
- ☐ $\text{ROE} = \text{NPM} \times \text{AT} \times \text{EM}$

### 🔹 Operating Cycle
- ☐ $\text{OC} = \text{Inventory Period} + \text{Receivables Period}$
- ☐ $\text{Cash Cycle} = \text{OC} - \text{Creditors Period}$

### 🔹 Examiner's Red Flags (Don't Do This!)
- ☐ Don't confuse **Net Profit** with **Operating Profit** in ratio formulas
- ☐ Don't forget to deduct **Preference Dividend** for EPS
- ☐ Don't include **Bank Overdraft** in Long-term Debt for D/E ratio
- ☐ Don't write ratios without "**: 1**" or "**times**" — units matter

### 🔹 High-Priority 14-Mark Topics for KTU 2024
- ☐ Ratio Analysis Computation (Most repeated)
- ☐ Common-Size Statement Preparation & Interpretation
- ☐ Comparative Statement with % Change
- ☐ DuPont 3-step / 5-step analysis
- ☐ Accounting Concepts with examples
- ☐ Balance Sheet equation & format
- ☐ Cash Flow Statement classification

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ratio Analysis: Balance Sheet Ratios, Income Statement Ratios, Cash Flow Statement Ratios, Leverage Ratios and Composite Ratios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Ratio Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ratio Analysis (KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition):**
> Ratio Analysis is the **systematic process of computing, interpreting, and evaluating meaningful mathematical relationships (ratios) between selected financial variables** drawn from the Balance Sheet, Income Statement, and Cash Flow Statement, with the objective of assessing the **liquidity, solvency, profitability, operational efficiency, and overall financial health** of a business enterprise.

It is the **backbone of financial statement analysis**, transforming raw numerical data into **comparable, standardised performance metrics**. Ratios are **relative measures** — pure numbers, percentages, or multiples — that allow an analyst to:

1. Compare performance **across time** (intra-firm comparison)
2. Compare performance **across firms** (inter-firm benchmarking)
3. Identify **trends, anomalies, and areas of concern**

## 1.2 Classification of Ratios (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!NOTE]
> For PEIET743 — Financial Management, the KTU 2024 syllabus groups ratios into **five canonical families**, each measuring a distinct dimension of corporate financial performance.

| **#** | **Ratio Family** | **Source Statement** | **Primary Question Answered** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Balance Sheet Ratios** | Balance Sheet | Can the firm meet short-term & long-term obligations? |
| 2 | **Income Statement Ratios** | P&L Statement | How efficiently is the firm generating profits? |
| 3 | **Cash Flow Statement Ratios** | Cash Flow Statement | Is the firm generating sufficient real cash? |
| 4 | **Leverage Ratios** | Both BS & P&L | How risky is the firm's capital structure? |
| 5 | **Composite Ratios** | Both BS & P&L | How effectively is capital employed? |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Body Check-Up"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a company as a **human body**.
> - **Balance Sheet Ratios** = **Vital signs** (pulse, blood pressure) — immediate health
> - **Income Statement Ratios** = **Stamina & Energy** — how much work the body can do
> - **Cash Flow Ratios** = **Actual oxygen in the blood** — real, usable capacity
> - **Leverage Ratios** = **Joint flexibility vs. rigidity** — risk of injury
> - **Composite Ratios** = **Overall fitness score** — integrating all metrics
>
> A doctor (financial analyst) doesn't just look at one indicator; they look at a **panel of ratios** to give a complete diagnosis.

## 1.4 Why Ratio Analysis Matters — Stakeholder Perspective

- **Equity Investors** → assess return potential (ROE, EPS)
- **Lenders / Banks** → assess repayment capacity (Debt-Equity, Interest Coverage)
- **Management** → identify operational gaps (Inventory Turnover, Gross Margin)
- **Creditors** → assess liquidity (Current Ratio, Quick Ratio)
- **Regulators** → ensure financial stability (Capital Adequacy variants)

## 1.5 Limitations (Honest Disclosure)

> [!WARNING]
> Ratios are **powerful but imperfect**. They suffer from:
> 1. **Historical bias** — based on past data, not future
> 2. **Inflation distortion** — across-year comparisons get skewed
> 3. **Window-dressing** — companies can manipulate year-end numbers
> 4. **Industry variability** — no universal "ideal" ratio
> 5. **Qualitative blind spots** — ignore management quality, brand value

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section presents the **complete, exam-ready formula set** for Module 2. Each formula is mathematically rigorous and accompanied by the **standard acceptable benchmark**.

## 2.1 Balance Sheet Ratios (Structural Ratios)

### 2.1.1 Liquidity Ratios

These measure the firm's ability to meet **short-term obligations** (current liabilities) from short-term resources (current assets).

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **Ideal Benchmark** | **Meaning** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Current Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Current Assets (CA)}}{\text{Current Liabilities (CL)}}$ | **2 : 1** | Short-term solvency |
| **Quick / Acid-Test Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses}}{\text{CL}}$ | **1 : 1** | Strictest liquidity |
| **Absolute Liquid Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Receivables}}{\text{CL}}$ | **0.5 : 1** | Most conservative |
| **Cash Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Cash Equivalents}}{\text{CL}}$ | 0.2 : 1 | Pure cash cushion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Step:** As we move from Current → Quick → Absolute Liquid, we are progressively **stripping away less-liquid current assets** (Inventory, Prepaid) to reach the **most defensible liquidity measure**.

### 2.1.2 Solvency / Capital Structure Ratios

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **Ideal Benchmark** | **Meaning** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}$ | **1 : 1 or 2 : 1** | Long-term risk |
| **Proprietary Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | 0.5 : 1 (or **higher**) | Owner's stake |
| **Capital Gearing Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Preference Share Capital} + \text{Long-Term Debt}}{\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds}}$ | Depends on industry | Debt vs. equity mix |
| **Debt to Total Assets** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | Below 0.5 | Asset funding via debt |
| **Fixed Assets to Net Worth** | $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Assets}}{\text{Net Worth}}$ | < 1 | Tangible coverage |

## 2.2 Income Statement / Profitability Ratios

### 2.2.1 Margin Ratios (Profit vs. Sales)

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **Healthy Range** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Gross Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **25 – 40 %** |
| **Operating Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **15 – 25 %** |
| **Net Profit Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit (After Tax)}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | **5 – 15 %** |
| **Operating Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold} + \text{Operating Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | < 75 % |
| **Expense Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Expenses}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Industry-dependent |

### 2.2.2 Activity / Turnover Ratios (Efficiency)

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **Interpretation** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Inventory Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Stock rotation speed |
| **Debtors Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Credit Sales}}{\text{Average Debtors}}$ | Collection efficiency |
| **Creditors Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Credit Purchases}}{\text{Average Creditors}}$ | Payment policy |
| **Fixed Asset Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Average Fixed Assets}}$ | Asset productivity |
| **Total Asset Turnover** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Average Total Assets}}$ | Overall efficiency |

> [!TIP]
> **Derived Metric — Collection Period (Days):**
> $$\text{Collection Period} = \dfrac{365}{\text{Debtors Turnover Ratio}}$$
> Similarly, **Inventory Holding Period** $= 365 / \text{Inventory Turnover}$.

## 2.3 Cash Flow Statement Ratios

These ratios are **increasingly emphasized in KTU 2024** because they expose **quality of earnings** — a firm can be profitable on paper but cash-starved in reality.

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **What It Reveals** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Operating Cash Flow Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Cash Flow from Operations (CFO)}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ | Cash-based short-term solvency |
| **Free Cash Flow Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{CFO} - \text{Capital Expenditure}}{\text{Sales}}$ | Discretionary cash per rupee of sales |
| **Cash Flow to Debt** | $\dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Total Debt}}$ | Debt-servicing capacity |
| **Cash Flow per Share (CFPS)** | $\dfrac{\text{CFO} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}}$ | Per-share cash generation |
| **Cash Flow Margin** | $\dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Operational cash efficiency |
| **Dividend Payout Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{Dividend per Share (DPS)}}{\text{Earnings per Share (EPS)}}$ | % of earnings distributed |
| **Dividend Yield Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{DPS}}{\text{Market Price per Share}} \times 100$ | Cash return to investors |

## 2.4 Leverage Ratios (Capital Structure Risk)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Leverage ratios quantify **how sensitive the firm's earnings and risk profile are to fixed costs** — both operating (fixed) and financial (interest).

### 2.4.1 Operating Leverage (DOL)

**Degree of Operating Leverage** measures the **% change in EBIT** for a 1% change in Sales.

$$\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \dfrac{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} - \text{Fixed Cost (Operating)}}$$

### 2.4.2 Financial Leverage (DFL)

**Degree of Financial Leverage** measures the **% change in EPS** for a 1% change in EBIT.

$$\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBIT} - \text{Interest} - \dfrac{\text{Preference Dividend}}{(1-t)}}$$

### 2.4.3 Combined Leverage (DCL)

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$$

### 2.4.4 Coverage Ratios

| **Ratio** | **Formula** | **Benchmark** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Interest Coverage Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest}}$ | **3 to 4 times** |
| **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Interest} + \text{Principal Repayment}}$ | > 1.5 |
| **Preference Dividend Coverage** | $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Preference Dividend}}$ | > 2 |

## 2.5 Composite Ratios (The Du Pont Family)

These ratios **integrate multiple statements** and are the **pinnacle of ratio analysis** — they tell the **complete story of return on investment**.

### 2.5.1 Return on Investment (ROI)

$$\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit Before Interest \& Tax (NPBIT)}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = \text{Net Profit Margin} \times \text{Investment (Asset) Turnover}$$

### 2.5.2 Return on Equity (ROE)

$$\text{ROE} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit After Tax (PAT)}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}} \times 100$$

### 2.5.3 Du Pont Identity (Three-Factor Decomposition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Du Pont Identity is a KTU-favorite topic.** It decomposes ROE into three multiplicative drivers:

$$\boxed{\text{ROE} = \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Sales}}}_{\text{Net Profit Margin}} \times \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}}}_{\text{Asset Turnover}} \times \underbrace{\dfrac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Equity}}}_{\text{Equity Multiplier (Leverage)}}}$$

### 2.5.4 Earnings Per Share (EPS)

$$\text{EPS} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit Available to Equity Shareholders}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares Outstanding}}$$

Where:
$$\text{Net Profit Available} = \text{PAT} - \text{Preference Dividend} - \text{Tax on Preference Dividend}$$

## 2.6 The KTU "Big Picture" — Integrated Ratio Logic

> [!TIP]
> **Master Formula Chain (commit to memory):**
> $$\text{ROE} = \text{Net Margin} \times \text{Turnover} \times \text{Leverage}$$
> $$\text{ROE} = \text{ROA} \times \text{Equity Multiplier}$$
> $$\text{ROA} = \text{Operating Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover}$$

These formulas are **the examiners' shortcut to full marks** — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 2 question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **fully worked numerical demonstrations** of every ratio family. The data is consistent across examples so that you can verify your understanding holistically.

## 3.1 Master Dataset (Used in All Examples Below)

**ABC Ltd. — Summarised Financial Statements for the Year Ended 31-03-2024**

### 3.1.1 Balance Sheet (₹ in Lakhs)

| **Liabilities** | **₹** | **Assets** | **₹** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Equity Share Capital (10,000 shares @ ₹100) | 10.00 | Land & Building | 8.00 |
| 8% Preference Share Capital | 2.00 | Plant & Machinery | 12.00 |
| Reserves & Surplus | 6.00 | Furniture | 2.00 |
| 12% Debentures | 8.00 | Inventory | 10.00 |
| Trade Payables | 4.00 | Trade Receivables | 6.00 |
| Short-term Loan | 2.00 | Cash in Hand | 1.00 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 2.00 | Prepaid Expenses | 1.00 |
| **Total** | **34.00** | **Total** | **40.00** |

### 3.1.2 Income Statement (₹ in Lakhs)

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|:---|:---:|
| Net Sales | 50.00 |
| (-) Cost of Goods Sold | 30.00 |
| **Gross Profit** | **20.00** |
| (-) Operating Expenses | 10.00 |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | **10.00** |
| (-) Interest on Debentures (12% of 8) | 0.96 |
| **Profit Before Tax (EBT)** | **9.04** |
| (-) Tax (30%) | 2.71 |
| **Net Profit After Tax (PAT)** | **6.33** |

### 3.1.3 Cash Flow Extract (₹ in Lakhs)

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|:---|:---:|
| Cash Flow from Operations (CFO) | 9.50 |
| Capital Expenditure (CapEx) | 4.00 |
| Dividend Paid | 2.00 |
| Total Debt | 8.00 (LT) + 2.00 (ST) = 10.00 |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Balance Sheet Ratios (Liquidity)

### Example 1.1: Current Ratio

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
Current Assets = Inventory + Receivables + Cash + Prepaid Expenses

**Step 2 — Compute Current Assets.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Assets} &= 10.00 + 6.00 + 1.00 + 1.00 \\
&= 18.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Current Liabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Liabilities} &= \text{Trade Payables} + \text{ST Loan} + \text{Outstanding Expenses} \\
&= 4.00 + 2.00 + 2.00 \\
&= 8.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\
&= \dfrac{18.00}{8.00} = 2.25 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Ideal is **2 : 1**; the firm has **₹2.25 of current assets for every ₹1 of current liabilities** → **Healthy liquidity**.

### Example 1.2: Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Assets} &= \text{CA} - \text{Inventory} - \text{Prepaid Expenses} \\
&= 18.00 - 10.00 - 1.00 \\
&= 7.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \dfrac{7.00}{8.00} = 0.875 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below the **1 : 1** benchmark → firm is **heavily dependent on inventory** to meet obligations. This is a **warning signal**.

### Example 1.3: Absolute Liquid Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absolute Liquid Assets} &= \text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities} + \text{Receivables} \\
&= 1.00 + 0 + 6.00 = 7.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Absolute Liquid Ratio} &= \dfrac{7.00}{8.00} = 0.875 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Solvency Ratios

### Example 2.1: Debt-Equity Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Long-Term Debt} &= 12\% \text{ Debentures} = 8.00 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{Shareholders' Funds} &= \text{Equity} + \text{Preference} + \text{Reserves} \\
&= 10.00 + 2.00 + 6.00 = 18.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{D/E Ratio} &= \dfrac{8.00}{18.00} = 0.44 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below **1 : 1** → **conservatively financed**, low financial risk.

### Example 2.2: Proprietary Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Proprietary Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Funds}}{\text{Total Assets}} \\
&= \dfrac{18.00}{40.00} = 0.45 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Shareholders fund **45% of assets**; **55% is financed by outsiders** — moderate.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Income Statement (Profitability) Ratios

### Example 3.1: Gross Profit Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GP Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{20.00}{50.00} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** ₹40 retained as gross profit from every ₹100 of sales — **very healthy**.

### Example 3.2: Net Profit Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NP Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{6.33}{50.00} \times 100 = 12.66\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 3.3: Operating Profit Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OP Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{10.00}{50.00} \times 100 = 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 3.4: Operating Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Cost} &= \text{COGS} + \text{Operating Expenses} \\
&= 30.00 + 10.00 = 40.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Ratio} &= \dfrac{40.00}{50.00} \times 100 = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Cash Flow Ratios

### Example 4.1: Operating Cash Flow Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OCFR} &= \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} \\
&= \dfrac{9.50}{8.00} = 1.19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 of current liability, the firm generates **₹1.19 from operations** → strong cash position.

### Example 4.2: Free Cash Flow Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} &= \text{CFO} - \text{CapEx} \\
&= 9.50 - 4.00 = 5.50
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF Ratio} &= \dfrac{5.50}{50.00} = 0.11 \text{ (or 11%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 4.3: Cash Flow to Debt

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CF/Debt} &= \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Total Debt}} = \dfrac{9.50}{10.00} = 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 4.4: Cash Flow Per Share (CFPS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFPS} &= \dfrac{\text{CFO} - \text{Preference Dividend}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}} \\
&= \dfrac{9.50 - 0.16}{10{,}000 \text{ shares}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where 0.16 = 8% of ₹2 lakh preference capital.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CFPS} &= \dfrac{9.34}{10{,}000} = \text{₹}0.0000934 \text{ lakhs} = ₹9.34 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Leverage Ratios

> [!NOTE]
> Assume fixed operating costs are embedded in the operating expenses of ₹10 lakhs, and variable cost = COGS = ₹30 lakhs (in this simplified dataset). Variable cost actually includes direct material/labour; we re-classify as: Variable Cost = ₹30, Fixed Operating Cost = ₹10.

### Example 5.1: Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution} &= \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{EBIT} &= \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Cost} = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOL} &= \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \dfrac{20}{10} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A **1% change in sales** produces a **2% change in EBIT** → **moderate operating risk**.

### Example 5.2: Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DFL} &= \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \dfrac{10.00}{9.04} = 1.106
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A **1% change in EBIT** → **1.106% change in EPS** → **low financial risk** (consistent with low debt).

### Example 5.3: Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DCL} &= \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = 2.0 \times 1.106 = 2.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A **1% change in sales** → **2.21% change in EPS** → **overall moderate risk**.

### Example 5.4: Interest Coverage Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ICR} &= \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest}} = \dfrac{10.00}{0.96} = 10.42 \text{ times}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** EBIT can cover interest **10.42 times** — **extremely safe**.

## 3.7 Worked Example 6 — Composite Ratios (Du Pont)

### Example 6.1: Return on Investment (ROI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital Employed} &= \text{Total Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities} \\
&= 40.00 - 8.00 = 32.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{\text{NPBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{10.00}{32.00} \times 100 = 31.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Example 6.2: Return on Equity (ROE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROE} &= \dfrac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{6.33 - 0.16}{10.00 + 6.00} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{6.17}{16.00} \times 100 = 38.56\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** ₹0.16 = preference dividend (8% of 2 lakhs). We deduct it to get **earnings available to equity shareholders**.

### Example 6.3: Du Pont Decomposition (Verification)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROE} &= \text{Net Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover} \times \text{Equity Multiplier} \\
&= \left(\dfrac{6.17}{50}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{50}{40}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{40}{16}\right) \\
&= 0.1234 \times 1.25 \times 2.5 \\
&= 0.3856 = 38.56\% \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The three factors multiply back exactly to ROE — **mathematical proof** of the Du Pont identity.

### Example 6.4: Earnings Per Share (EPS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPS} &= \dfrac{\text{Profit Available to Equity}}{\text{No. of Equity Shares}} \\
&= \dfrac{6.17 \text{ lakhs}}{10{,}000} \\
&= \text{₹}61.7 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Consolidated Analytical Matrix (Full Picture)

| **Category** | **Ratio** | **Computed Value** | **Benchmark** | **Verdict** |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Liquidity | Current Ratio | 2.25 : 1 | 2 : 1 | ✅ Healthy |
| Liquidity | Quick Ratio | 0.875 : 1 | 1 : 1 | ⚠️ Below par |
| Solvency | Debt-Equity | 0.44 : 1 | ≤ 1 : 1 | ✅ Low risk |
| Profitability | Gross Margin | 40 % | 25–40 % | ✅ Strong |
| Profitability | Net Margin | 12.66 % | 5–15 % | ✅ Strong |
| Cash Flow | OCFR | 1.19 | > 1 | ✅ Excellent |
| Leverage | DOL | 2.0 | < 3 | ✅ Moderate |
| Leverage | DFL | 1.106 | < 1.5 | ✅ Low |
| Leverage | ICR | 10.42× | > 3× | ✅ Safe |
| Composite | ROE | 38.56 % | > 15 % | ✅ Excellent |

> [!TIP]
> **Master Takeaway:** ABC Ltd. is a **financially healthy, conservatively financed, cash-rich firm** with one weakness — **inventory dependence** (quick ratio).
