# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section contains **KTU-aligned practice questions** with model solutions and a **rapid-revision recap**.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**Q: Distinguish between Operating Leverage and Financial Leverage. Why are they combined into "Total Leverage"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Basis** | **Operating Leverage (DOL)** | **Financial Leverage (DFL)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Source of risk | **Fixed Operating Costs** (rent, depreciation, salaries) | **Fixed Financial Charges** (interest, preference dividend) |
| Measures | Effect of sales change on **EBIT** | Effect of EBIT change on **EPS** |
| Formula | Contribution ÷ EBIT | EBIT ÷ EBT |
| Focus | **Operating / Business Risk** | **Financial Risk** |

**Why Combined?**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Operating and Financial Leverage are **multiplicative** in their effect on shareholder earnings.
> $$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$$
> The combined leverage measures the **total business risk** faced by equity shareholders — a 1% change in sales produces a DCL% change in EPS. **Investors and managers need this composite view** because either type of risk alone understates the total exposure.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Q: What are Cash Flow Statement Ratios? Why are they considered more reliable than Income Statement Ratios?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cash Flow Statement Ratios are ratios computed using **Cash Flow from Operations (CFO), Free Cash Flow, and Cash Flow per Share** to assess the **actual cash-generating ability** of a firm.

**Reasons for Superior Reliability:**

1. **Cash is King** — Profit can be manipulated through **accruals, depreciation choices, and revenue recognition policies**, but **cash inflow is harder to fake**.
2. **Liquidity Insight** — A firm may show high net profit but be **technically insolvent** if cash is locked in receivables.
3. **Quality of Earnings** — CF ratios reveal whether reported profits are backed by **actual cash collections**.
4. **Creditor Preference** — Lenders care about **cash availability** to service debt, not just reported earnings.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners specifically award **bonus marks** for mentioning the **"accrual vs. cash" distinction** — a classic KTU buzz phrase.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every 14-mark question offers an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Both are provided below with **complete, step-by-step model solutions**.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`

**From the following Balance Sheet and Income Statement of M/s PQR Industries Ltd. for the year ended 31-03-2024, compute:**
**(a)** Current Ratio, Quick Ratio, and Debt-Equity Ratio. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Gross Profit Ratio, Net Profit Ratio, and Operating Ratio. (7 Marks)

**Balance Sheet as on 31-03-2024 (₹ in Lakhs)**

| **Liabilities** | **₹** | **Assets** | **₹** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Equity Share Capital | 20.00 | Land & Building | 15.00 |
| 10% Preference Capital | 5.00 | Plant & Machinery | 25.00 |
| General Reserve | 8.00 | Stock (Inventory) | 18.00 |
| 9% Debentures | 12.00 | Debtors | 10.00 |
| Creditors | 7.00 | Cash & Bank | 3.00 |
| Bills Payable | 3.00 | Prepaid Expenses | 2.00 |
| Outstanding Expenses | 3.00 | — | — |
| **Total** | **58.00** | **Total** | **73.00** |

**Income Statement for the Year Ended 31-03-2024 (₹ in Lakhs)**

| **Particulars** | **₹** |
|:---|:---:|
| Net Sales | 100.00 |
| (-) Cost of Goods Sold | 60.00 |
| **Gross Profit** | **40.00** |
| (-) Administrative Expenses | 8.00 |
| (-) Selling Expenses | 6.00 |
| **Operating Profit (EBIT)** | **26.00** |
| (-) Interest on Debentures | 1.08 |
| **Profit Before Tax** | **24.92** |
| (-) Tax (40%) | 9.97 |
| **Net Profit After Tax** | **14.95** |

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Balance Sheet Ratios [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute Current Assets.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Assets} &= \text{Stock} + \text{Debtors} + \text{Cash} + \text{Prepaid} \\
&= 18.00 + 10.00 + 3.00 + 2.00 \\
&= 33.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Current Liabilities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Liabilities} &= \text{Creditors} + \text{Bills Payable} + \text{Outstanding Expenses} \\
&= 7.00 + 3.00 + 3.00 \\
&= 13.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Current Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current Ratio} &= \dfrac{33.00}{13.00} = 2.54 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict (1 mark):** Above 2:1 benchmark → **Strong short-term solvency**. ✅ [1M]

**Step 4 — Quick Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Quick Assets} &= 33.00 - 18.00 - 2.00 = 13.00 \\
\text{Quick Ratio} &= \dfrac{13.00}{13.00} = 1.00 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict (1 mark):** Meets 1:1 benchmark → **Adequate** but **no cushion** if inventory becomes obsolete. ⚠️ [1M]

**Step 5 — Debt-Equity Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Long-Term Debt} &= 9\% \text{ Debentures} = 12.00 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{Shareholders' Funds} &= 20.00 + 5.00 + 8.00 = 33.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{D/E Ratio} &= \dfrac{12.00}{33.00} = 0.36 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict (1 mark):** Well below 1:1 → **Conservative capital structure, low financial risk**. ✅ [1M]

**Subtotal for Part (a): 7 Marks** (3 computations × 2 marks each + 1 mark for final interpretation)

---

#### Part (b) — Income Statement Ratios [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Gross Profit Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GP Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{40.00}{100.00} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** Very strong — ₹40 gross margin per ₹100 sales. ✅ [1M]

**Step 2 — Net Profit Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NP Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{PAT}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100 = \dfrac{14.95}{100.00} \times 100 = 14.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** Healthy 14.95% — firm converts nearly 15% of sales into bottom-line profit. ✅ [1M]

**Step 3 — Operating Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operating Cost} &= \text{COGS} + \text{Administrative} + \text{Selling} \\
&= 60.00 + 8.00 + 6.00 = 74.00 \\
\text{Operating Ratio} &= \dfrac{74.00}{100.00} \times 100 = 74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** Below 75% benchmark → **Efficient cost control**, leaving 26% as operating margin. ✅ [1M]

**Subtotal for Part (b): 7 Marks** (3 computations × 2 marks each + 1 mark for final interpretation)

**TOTAL: 14 MARKS** ✅

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**From the data of PQR Industries Ltd. (above), and additional information:**

> Additional Data: Cash Flow from Operations = ₹18.00 lakhs; Capital Expenditure = ₹6.00 lakhs; Total Debt = ₹12.00 lakhs (LT) + ₹0 (ST); 10,000 equity shares outstanding; Preference Dividend Paid = ₹0.50 lakhs.

**Compute:**
**(a)** Operating Cash Flow Ratio, Free Cash Flow, Cash Flow to Debt, and Cash Flow per Share. (7 Marks)
**(b)** Return on Investment (ROI), Return on Equity (ROE), and the Du Pont decomposition of ROE. (7 Marks)

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Cash Flow Ratios [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Operating Cash Flow Ratio (OCFR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OCFR} &= \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Current Liabilities}} = \dfrac{18.00}{13.00} = 1.38
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** ₹1.38 of operating cash per ₹1 of current liability — **excellent cash-based liquidity**. ✅ [1M]

**Step 2 — Free Cash Flow (FCF).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FCF} &= \text{CFO} - \text{CapEx} = 18.00 - 6.00 = 12.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** ₹12 lakhs available for dividends, debt repayment, and expansion. ✅ [1M]

**Step 3 — Cash Flow to Debt.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CF/Debt} &= \dfrac{\text{CFO}}{\text{Total Debt}} = \dfrac{18.00}{12.00} = 1.50
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Operating cash covers total debt 1.5 times → **strong debt-servicing capacity**. ✅ [1M]

**Step 4 — Cash Flow per Share (CFPS).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cash Available} &= \text{CFO} - \text{Preference Dividend} \\
&= 18.00 - 0.50 = 17.50 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{CFPS} &= \dfrac{17.50}{10{,}000} = \text{₹}175 \text{ per share}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Each share generates ₹175 of operating cash — **strong cash-backed value**. ✅ [1M]

**Subtotal for Part (a): 7 Marks** (4 computations × ~1.75 marks each, plus 0 marks for interpretation if ratio answers are clean)

---

#### Part (b) — Composite Ratios & Du Pont [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Capital Employed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capital Employed} &= \text{Total Assets} - \text{Current Liabilities} \\
&= 73.00 - 13.00 = 60.00 \text{ lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Return on Investment (ROI).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{26.00}{60.00} \times 100 = 43.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Exceptional — every ₹100 of capital earns ₹43.33. ✅ [1M]

**Step 3 — Return on Equity (ROE).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit Available to Equity} &= \text{PAT} - \text{Preference Dividend} \\
&= 14.95 - 0.50 = 14.45 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds} &= 20.00 + 8.00 = 28.00 \text{ lakhs} \\
\text{ROE} &= \dfrac{14.45}{28.00} \times 100 = 51.61\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Equity shareholders earn over 51% — **outstanding return**. ✅ [1M]

**Step 4 — Du Pont Three-Factor Decomposition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Profit Margin} &= \dfrac{14.45}{100.00} = 0.1445 \; (14.45\%) \\
\text{Asset Turnover} &= \dfrac{100.00}{73.00} = 1.3699 \\
\text{Equity Multiplier} &= \dfrac{73.00}{28.00} = 2.6071
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROE} &= 0.1445 \times 1.3699 \times 2.6071 \\
&= 0.5161 = 51.61\% \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification (1 mark):** The three drivers multiply exactly to ROE, confirming the Du Pont identity. ✅

**Subtotal for Part (b): 7 Marks**

**TOTAL: 14 MARKS** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct Preference Dividend** from PAT before computing ROE / EPS. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Using Total Assets instead of Capital Employed** in ROI — KTU strictly expects the latter. (Loss: 0.5 mark)
> 3. **Confusing Average vs. Closing** Inventory/Debtors — if "Average" is not given, use the closing balance; if it is given, use average. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up Current Assets** — *Prepaid Expenses* are technically a current asset but should be **excluded from Quick Ratio**. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Omitting units** — write "₹ in lakhs" or "₹ in crores" in the final answer table. (Loss: 0.5 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation** — KTU awards **at least 1 mark per sub-question** for a clear verdict. **Never end with just numbers.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this section as a final-glance revision sheet before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ 1. The 5 Families — One-Liner Each
- **Balance Sheet Ratios** = static snapshot of financial position
- **Income Statement Ratios** = flow-based profitability measures
- **Cash Flow Ratios** = real-cash quality of earnings
- **Leverage Ratios** = magnifying effect on shareholder returns
- **Composite Ratios** = integrated cross-statement metrics

### ✅ 2. Critical Benchmarks (Memorize)
- Current Ratio: **2 : 1** | Quick Ratio: **1 : 1** | Absolute Liquid: **0.5 : 1**
- Debt-Equity: **≤ 1 : 1** | Interest Coverage: **≥ 3 times**
- Gross Margin: **25 – 40 %** | Net Margin: **5 – 15 %**
- ROE: **> 15 %** is healthy | ROI: **> 20 %** is strong

### ✅ 3. The Du Pont Trifecta
> $$\text{ROE} = \text{Net Margin} \times \text{Asset Turnover} \times \text{Equity Multiplier}$$
> This is **the most-tested formula in KTU Module 2** — practice 5 numerical problems.

### ✅ 4. Leverage Ladder
> $$\text{Sales} \xrightarrow{-\text{VC}} \text{Contribution} \xrightarrow{-\text{FC}} \text{EBIT} \xrightarrow{-\text{Int}} \text{EBT} \xrightarrow{-\text{Tax}} \text{PAT}$$
> $$\text{DOL} = \text{Cont}/\text{EBIT}, \;\; \text{DFL} = \text{EBIT}/\text{EBT}, \;\; \text{DCL} = \text{Cont}/\text{EBT}$$

### ✅ 5. Cash Flow Hierarchy
> $$\text{CFO} > \text{Net Profit} \implies \text{Healthy Earnings Quality}$$
> $$\text{FCF} = \text{CFO} - \text{CapEx} \implies \text{Discretionary Cash}$$

### ✅ 6. EPS & CFPS
> Always deduct **Preference Dividend + Tax on Pref. Dividend** from PAT.
> Tax on Pref. Dividend = `Pref. Dividend × Tax Rate / (1 − Tax Rate)`.

### ✅ 7. Common Confusions to Avoid
| **Confusion** | **Clarity** |
|:---|:---|
| "Quick Ratio excludes Inventory" ✅ | Also excludes **Prepaid Expenses** |
| "DOL uses fixed cost" | Uses **Total Fixed Operating Cost**, not depreciation alone |
| "ROI uses Total Assets" | Uses **Capital Employed** (Assets − Current Liabilities) |
| "Debt-Equity uses all debt" | KTU uses **Long-Term Debt only**, unless specified otherwise |
| "ROA = ROI" | Sometimes yes; ROI in KTU = **NPBIT / Capital Employed** |

### ✅ 8. The "If Asked Only One Formula" Picks
- For **profitability** → **Du Pont ROE**
- For **liquidity** → **Quick Ratio** (most rigorous)
- For **risk** → **DCL** (comprehensive)
- For **cash quality** → **OCFR**
- For **shareholder value** → **EPS + ROE**

### ✅ 9. Presentation Tips for KTU Exam
- Use **tables** for ratio computations — saves time, looks professional
- **Bold** your final numerical answer
- Always end with a **one-line verdict** (✅ healthy, ⚠️ watch, ❌ weak)
- Carry **2 decimal places** for ratios (e.g., 2.54:1, not 2.5:1)

### ✅ 10. The "Never Forget" Checklist Before Submission
- [ ] Did I show **all working steps** with ₹ values?
- [ ] Did I provide a **clear interpretation** for every ratio?
- [ ] Did I deduct **preference dividend** from PAT for equity-based ratios?
- [ ] Did I label all units (₹ lakhs, ₹ crores, times, %)?
- [ ] Did I verify the **Du Pont multiplication** matches ROE?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Ratios without interpretation are like speedometers without a driver — they show numbers, but they don't tell you where to go."* Always interpret. Always justify. Always conclude.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Structure: Meaning, Scope and Patterns of Capital Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital Structure: Meaning, Scope and Patterns of Capital Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Structure** refers to the specific combination or **mix of long-term sources of funds** employed by a firm to finance its assets and operations. It is the permanent financing arrangement represented by the relative proportions of **long-term debt, preference share capital, equity share capital, and retained earnings** on the right-hand side of the balance sheet.

According to the standard KTU 2024 financial management framework, capital structure represents only the **long-term funds** of a business — those obligations whose repayment extends beyond one operating cycle (typically more than 12 months). The decision regarding the proportions of these long-term sources constitutes one of the most critical financing decisions faced by a financial manager.

### 1.2 Capital Structure vs Financial Structure

This is a **high-frequency KTU exam distinction**. Students must not confuse the two:

| Aspect | Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Only long-term sources of finance | All sources of finance (both short-term and long-term) |
| **Components** | Equity, Preference Share, Debentures, Long-term Loans, Retained Earnings | All Capital Structure components + Current Liabilities + Short-term Borrowings + Trade Payables |
| **Nature** | Permanent capital | Permanent + Temporary capital |
| **Decision-making** | Long-term financing decisions | Entire financing mix including working capital |
| **Focus Area** | Risk-return trade-off over the long term | Liquidity and solvency considerations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memory**: Capital Structure is a **subset** of Financial Structure. Think of Financial Structure as a "big umbrella" under which Capital Structure sits as a "smaller, specialized compartment."

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The House-Building Analogy** 🏗️
>
> Imagine you are building your dream house. You have three funding options:
> - **Bank Loan (Debt)** — You pay back with interest, but it's not "your" money. The bank has a claim on your house if you default.
> - **Parent's Contribution (Equity / Owner's Capital)** — This is permanent, no fixed obligation to repay, but they share ownership and future profits.
> - **Savings Retained from Past Earnings (Retained Earnings)** — Your own previously earned money reinvested.
>
> The **Capital Structure** is the **recipe** that decides how much of your house will be funded by bank loans versus your own resources versus your savings. Too much loan → high risk of foreclosure. Too little loan → you lose out on the **tax shield** and **leverage benefits**. A balanced mix is the goal.

### 1.4 Components of Capital Structure

The long-term sources of finance that constitute the capital structure include:

1. **Equity Share Capital** — Owned funds representing ownership stake
2. **Preference Share Capital** — Hybrid security with fixed dividend and priority claim
3. **Debentures / Bonds** — Long-term debt instruments with fixed interest
4. **Term Loans from Financial Institutions** — Bank borrowings and institutional debt
5. **Retained Earnings / Reserves & Surplus** — Internal accruals reinvested
6. **Convertible Securities** — Hybrid instruments (convertible debentures, warrants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for Capital Structure Analysis**: The **Debt-Equity Ratio** is the most widely used metric in KTU examinations.
>
> $$D/E = \frac{\text{Total Long-term Debt}}{\text{Total Shareholders' Funds}}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scope of Capital Structure Decision

The capital structure decision is **multi-dimensional** and is not merely about choosing between debt and equity. Its scope encompasses the following key dimensions:

#### A. Determining the Debt-Equity Mix
- What proportion of total long-term funds should come from debt?
- What proportion should come from equity?
- This is the **central** capital structure decision.

#### B. Choosing the Type of Debt Instrument
- Secured vs Unsecured debentures
- Convertible vs Non-convertible debentures
- Fixed rate vs Floating rate debt
- Short-term long-term vs perpetual debt

#### C. Timing of the Capital Issue
- **Right timing** to tap the market (bull vs bear phase)
- Window of opportunity in interest rate cycles
- Coordination with project completion schedules

#### D. Form of Capital
- Public issue vs Private placement
- Domestic vs Foreign currency denominated debt (ECBs)
- Mezzanine financing options

#### E. Maturity Profile
- Matching asset life with liability maturity
- Avoiding asset-liability mismatch (critical in banking finance)

#### F. Cost Optimization
- Minimizing the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**
- Maximizing firm valuation

#### G. Risk Management
- **Financial Risk** — Risk arising from fixed financial charges (interest + preference dividend)
- **Business Risk** — Operating risk from the firm's industry
- Total Risk = Business Risk + Financial Risk

### 2.2 Important Theoretical Concepts

#### 1. Trading on Equity (Leverage)
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**: The practice of using debt (fixed-cost funds) in the capital structure to amplify the returns to equity shareholders, especially when the **Return on Investment (ROI) exceeds the Cost of Debt (Kd)**.

**Mechanism**: When a firm earns more on borrowed money than what it pays as interest, the surplus accrues to equity shareholders, thereby magnifying their earnings per share (EPS).

#### 2. Capital Gearing
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: The ratio between **fixed-interest bearing capital (debt + preference share capital)** and **equity share capital** in the total capital structure. It describes the proportion of fixed-return capital to variable-return capital.

**Three Categories**:
- **Highly Geared Company** — Debt >> Equity (High financial risk, high potential return)
- **Moderately Geared Company** — Balanced mix
- **Low Geared Company** — Debt << Equity (Conservative, low risk)

#### 3. Financial Leverage
**Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** measures the sensitivity of EPS to changes in EBIT.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Debt-Equity Ratio** | $D/E = \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Equity Shareholders' Funds}}$ | D = Debt, E = Equity | Ideal range varies by industry; $< 1$ considered conservative |
| 2 | **Debt-Total Funds Ratio** | $D/TA = \frac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ | TA = Total Assets | Measures asset coverage by debt |
| 3 | **Equity Multiplier** | $EM = \frac{\text{Total Assets}}{\text{Net Worth}}$ | Net Worth = Equity | $EM = 1 + D/E$ |
| 4 | **Interest Coverage Ratio (Times Interest Earned)** | $ICR = \frac{EBIT}{\text{Interest Expense}}$ | EBIT = Earnings Before Interest & Tax | Higher is better; $ICR < 1.5$ indicates distress |
| 5 | **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** | $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - PD}$ | I = Interest, PD = Preference Dividend | Measures EPS sensitivity to EBIT changes |
| 6 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = W_d K_d (1-t) + W_e K_e + W_p K_p$ | W = weights, K = costs, t = tax rate | $W_d + W_e + W_p = 1$ |
| 7 | **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate, $\beta$ = Beta, $R_m$ = Market return | Used in equity cost computation |
| 8 | **Cost of Debt (After-tax)** | $K_d(1-t)$ | $K_d$ = pre-tax cost, $t$ = tax rate | Interest is tax-deductible |
| 9 | **EPS Formula** | $EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD}{\text{Number of Equity Shares}}$ | — | Critical for financial planning |
| 10 | **Financial Break-Even EBIT** | $EBIT_{BE} = \frac{(EPS \times N) + PD}{1-t} + I$ | N = number of shares | EBIT level at which EPS = 0 |
| 11 | **Gearing Ratio** | $GR = \frac{\text{Fixed Interest Capital}}{\text{Equity Capital}}$ | — | Measures financial leverage intensity |
| 12 | **Capitalisation Patterns Ratio** | $CR = \frac{\text{Equity}}{\text{Debt} + \text{Preference Share}}$ | — | Used in pattern classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note**: In formula tables, always specify whether the debt component is **book value or market value**. The two give substantially different ratios. KTU typically uses **book value** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Capital Structure Analysis

Capital structure analysis is foundational to:
- **Corporate Finance Valuation** (DCF, WACC discounting)
- **Credit Rating Assessment** by agencies (CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's)
- **M&A Transactions** — determining fair value of target's capital mix
- **Bank Lending Decisions** — Debt Service Coverage Ratio analysis
- **Working Capital Management** — Long-term solvency assessment
- **Investor Decision-making** — Risk-return profile of equity investments

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)

The DFL measures the percentage change in EPS for a given percentage change in EBIT.

**Step 1: Define EPS**

$$EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD}{N}$$

Where:
- $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Taxes
- $I$ = Interest on debt
- $t$ = Tax rate
- $PD$ = Preference Dividend
- $N$ = Number of equity shares

**Step 2: Define Earnings Before Tax (EBT)**

$$EBT = EBIT - I$$

**Step 3: Express Earnings After Tax (EAT) before preference dividend**

$$EAT_{pre} = (EBIT - I)(1-t)$$

**Step 4: Express EPS after preference dividend**

$$EPS = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD}{N}$$

**Step 5: Take the differential of EPS with respect to EBIT**

$$\frac{d(EPS)}{d(EBIT)} = \frac{(1-t)}{N}$$

**Step 6: Apply the definition of DFL (ratio of % change in EPS to % change in EBIT)**

$$DFL = \frac{\frac{d(EPS)}{EPS}}{\frac{d(EBIT)}{EBIT}}$$

**Step 7: Substitute the differential**

$$DFL = \frac{\frac{(1-t)}{N}}{\frac{EBIT}{\frac{(EBIT-I)(1-t) - PD}{N}}}$$

**Step 8: Simplify the N's and 1-t terms**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD} \times (1-t)$$

**Step 9: Cancel (1-t) carefully and arrive at the standard form**

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT - \frac{PD}{(1-t)}}$$

**Step 10: Standard practical form (most used in KTU)**

$$\boxed{DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - PD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: A DFL of 2 means a 10% change in EBIT will cause a 20% change in EPS. Higher DFL = Higher financial risk = Higher potential reward.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Trading on Equity — Conditions and Outcomes

| Scenario | ROI (Return on Investment) | Kd (Cost of Debt) | Trading on Equity Effect | Impact on EPS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scenario 1: Favourable Leverage** | $ROI > K_d$ | Lower | **Positive** (Beneficial) | EPS increases with debt |
| **Scenario 2: Neutral Point** | $ROI = K_d$ | Equal | **Nil** (No effect) | EPS unchanged |
| **Scenario 3: Unfavourable Leverage** | $ROI < K_d$ | Higher | **Negative** (Adverse) | EPS decreases with debt |

**Mathematical Representation of the Trading on Equity Effect**:

$$\Delta EPS \propto (ROI - K_d) \times D$$

Where $D$ is the amount of debt raised. If the expression is **positive**, debt is favourable; if **negative**, debt is eroding shareholder value.

### 3.3 Numerical Illustration: Pattern of Capital Structure

**Problem**: A firm has the following capital structure. Calculate the gearing ratio and classify the firm.

| Source of Capital | Amount (₹ in lakhs) |
|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital (10,000 shares of ₹100 each) | 1,000 |
| 12% Preference Share Capital | 200 |
| 14% Debentures | 800 |
| **Total Capital** | **2,000** |

**Solution**:

**Step 1: Identify Fixed-Return Capital**

$$\text{Fixed-Return Capital} = \text{Preference Share Capital} + \text{Debentures} = 200 + 800 = 1{,}000 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 2: Identify Variable-Return Capital (Equity)**

$$\text{Variable-Return Capital} = \text{Equity Share Capital} = 1{,}000 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Gearing Ratio**

$$GR = \frac{\text{Fixed-Return Capital}}{\text{Equity Capital}} = \frac{1{,}000}{1{,}000} = 1.0$$

**Step 4: Classification of the Firm**

- If $GR > 1$ → **Highly Geared**
- If $GR = 1$ → **Moderately Geared (Balanced)**
- If $GR < 1$ → **Low Geared (Conservative)**

Since $GR = 1.0$, the firm is **Moderately Geared / Normally Geared**.

**Step 5: Interpretation**

The firm has an equal proportion of fixed-return capital and equity capital. This represents a **balanced capital structure** where financial risk and return potential are evenly distributed. The firm is neither over-relying on debt nor being excessively conservative with equity.

### 3.4 Patterns of Capital Structure — Detailed Classification

#### Pattern 1: **Conservative (Low-Geared) Pattern**
- Heavy reliance on **equity capital**
- Minimal debt component
- **Advantages**: Low financial risk, high solvency, flexibility in downturns
- **Disadvantages**: High cost of capital (equity is costlier than debt), missed tax shield

#### Pattern 2: **Aggressive (High-Geared) Pattern**
- Heavy reliance on **debt and preference share capital**
- Minimal equity proportion
- **Advantages**: Tax shield, magnified EPS, lower WACC
- **Disadvantages**: High financial risk, solvency concerns, distress in low EBIT

#### Pattern 3: **Balanced (Moderately Geared) Pattern**
- Optimal mix of debt and equity
- Aligned with industry norms
- **Advantages**: Trade-off between risk and return
- **Disadvantages**: Requires continuous monitoring and re-balancing

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Style Identification Tip**: When given a capital structure, students should immediately compute (a) D/E ratio, (b) ICR, and (c) Gearing Ratio to classify the pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Capital Structure Classification Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Capital Structure Decision] --> node2[Long-term Sources of Funds]
    node2 --> node3[Owned Funds]
    node2 --> node4[Borrowed Funds]

    node3 --> node3b[Preference Share Capital]

    node3 --> node3d[Reserves and Surplus]

    node4 --> node4c[Convertible Securities]
    node4 --> node4d[Foreign Currency Bonds]

    node5 --> node5a[Conservative Pattern]
    node5 --> node5b[Balanced Pattern]
    node5 --> node5c[Aggressive Pattern]
    node5a --> node5a1[Low Debt to Equity Ratio]
    node5a --> node5a2[Low Financial Risk]
    node5a --> node5a3[Higher Cost of Capital]
    node5b --> node5b1[Optimal Debt Equity Mix]
    node5b --> node5b2[Balanced Risk and Return]
    node5c --> node5c1[High Debt to Equity Ratio]

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Trading on Equity Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Investment Decision: Raise Debt] --> nodeB{Is ROI greater than Kd?}
    nodeB -->|Yes, ROI greater than Kd| nodeC[Positive Trading on Equity]
    nodeB -->|No, ROI less than Kd| nodeD[Negative Trading on Equity]
    nodeB -->|Equal, ROI equals Kd| nodeE[Neutral Position]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[EPS Magnified]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Shareholder Wealth Maximized]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[EPS Diluted]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Shareholder Wealth Eroded]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[No Change in EPS]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Capital Structure Decision-Making Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputA[Market Conditions] --> proc1[Capital Structure Analysis Engine]
    inputB[Industry Benchmarks] --> proc1
    inputC[Tax Environment] --> proc1
    inputD[Cost of Capital Components] --> proc1
    inputE[Risk Appetite of Management] --> proc1
    proc1 --> out1[Optimal Debt Equity Mix]
    proc1 --> out2[Pattern Classification Output]

    proc1 --> out4[Firm Valuation Impact]

    out2 --> act2[Risk Profiling]

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Capital Gearing Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Capital Gearing Analysis] --> nodeQ1{Fixed Capital compared to Equity}
    nodeQ1 -->|Fixed greater than Equity| nodeHG[Highly Geared Company]
    nodeQ1 -->|Fixed approximately equal to Equity| nodeMG[Moderately Geared Company]
    nodeQ1 -->|Fixed less than Equity| nodeLG[Low Geared Company]
    nodeHG --> nodeHG1[High Financial Leverage]
    nodeHG --> nodeHG2[High EPS Volatility]
    nodeMG --> nodeMG1[Balanced Financial Profile]
    nodeLG --> nodeLG1[Conservative Profile]
    nodeLG --> nodeLG2[Stable EPS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define Capital Structure. How is it different from Financial Structure?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Definition (2 marks)**: Capital Structure refers to the composition of long-term sources of funds employed by a firm. It includes the relative proportions of equity share capital, preference share capital, debentures, long-term loans, and retained earnings used to finance the firm's permanent capital requirements.

**Differentiation (1 mark)**:

| Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|---|---|
| Considers only long-term sources | Considers both long-term and short-term sources |
| Excludes current liabilities | Includes current liabilities and trade payables |
| Subset of Financial Structure | Superset of Capital Structure |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Explain the concept of "Trading on Equity" with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Concept (2 marks)**: Trading on Equity refers to the practice of using debt (fixed-cost funds) in the capital structure to increase the earnings per share of equity shareholders. This works favourably when the return on investment exceeds the cost of debt, allowing the surplus to enhance shareholder returns.

**Example (1 mark)**: Suppose a firm earns a return of 15% on its investments and the cost of debt is 10%. By raising an additional ₹10 lakh through debt at 10% interest, the firm earns ₹1.5 lakh from the investment but pays only ₹1 lakh as interest, leaving a surplus of ₹50,000 to enhance equity shareholders' earnings. This is favourable trading on equity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Capital Structure decisions are central to value maximization in any firm." Discuss the meaning, scope, and patterns of capital structure in detail.**

**Model Solution**:

#### Part (a) — Meaning and Scope of Capital Structure (7 marks)

**Meaning (3 marks)**:
Capital Structure is the permanent financing of a firm represented by the specific mix of long-term debt, preference share capital, equity share capital, and retained earnings. It excludes short-term obligations like trade payables and short-term bank borrowings.

The financial manager's capital structure decision determines the firm's long-term financial risk and cost of capital, directly impacting the firm's market value.

**Scope (4 marks)**:

1. **Determining Debt-Equity Proportions**: The most fundamental decision — the right balance between fixed-cost capital (debt + preference) and variable-cost capital (equity).

2. **Selection of Debt Instruments**: Choosing between secured/unsecured debentures, convertible/non-convertible bonds, fixed/floating rate debt, and matching maturities with asset life.

3. **Timing of Capital Raising**: Identifying favourable market windows, interest rate cycles, and economic conditions for tapping the capital market.

4. **Form of Capital Issuance**: Public issue, rights issue, private placement, or qualified institutional placement.

5. **Cost Optimization**: Minimizing WACC to maximize firm value — the central tenet of MM approach and modern capital structure theory.

6. **Risk Management**: Balancing financial risk (from fixed charges) against the benefits of leverage.

#### Part (b) — Patterns of Capital Structure (7 marks)

**Pattern 1: Conservative (Low-Geared) Pattern (2.5 marks)**:
- **Debt-Equity Ratio**: Low (typically $< 0.5$)
- **Composition**: Predominantly equity, minimal debt
- **Advantages**: Low financial risk, high solvency, flexibility in downturns
- **Disadvantages**: High cost of capital, missed tax shield, lower EPS amplification
- **Example**: Infrastructure companies in early stages, IT service firms

**Pattern 2: Balanced (Moderately Geared) Pattern (2 marks)**:
- **Debt-Equity Ratio**: Moderate (typically $0.5$ to $1.0$)
- **Composition**: Equilibrium between debt and equity
- **Advantages**: Optimal risk-return trade-off
- **Disadvantages**: Requires continuous monitoring
- **Example**: Established manufacturing companies

**Pattern 3: Aggressive (High-Geared) Pattern (2.5 marks)**:
- **Debt-Equity Ratio**: High (typically $> 1.0$)
- **Composition**: Heavy reliance on debt
- **Advantages**: Tax shield, magnified EPS, lower WACC
- **Disadvantages**: High financial risk, bankruptcy risk in low EBIT periods
- **Example**: Utility companies, real estate firms, infrastructure with stable cash flows

**Conclusion**: The choice of pattern depends on the firm's business risk, industry life cycle, growth stage, and management's risk appetite. An optimal capital structure minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative]

**Explain the concept of Capital Gearing. Discuss its types and the significance of gearing ratio in financial decision-making.**

**Model Solution**:

#### Part (a) — Concept and Types of Capital Gearing (7 marks)

**Concept (3 marks)**:
Capital Gearing refers to the relationship between **fixed-interest bearing capital** (debentures + preference share capital) and **equity share capital** in the total capital structure of a firm. It is a measure of the proportion of fixed-return capital relative to variable-return capital.

**Mathematical Expression**:

$$Gearing\ Ratio = \frac{\text{Fixed-Interest Capital}}{\text{Equity Capital}}$$

**Types (4 marks)**:

1. **Highly Geared Company** (2 marks):
   - When Fixed-Interest Capital $\gg$ Equity Capital
   - Gearing Ratio typically $> 1.0$
   - High financial leverage, high EPS volatility
   - High financial risk, but high reward potential

2. **Moderately / Normally Geared Company** (1 mark):
   - When Fixed-Interest Capital $\approx$ Equity Capital
   - Gearing Ratio $\approx 1.0$
   - Balanced approach, moderate risk-return profile

3. **Low Geared Company** (1 mark):
   - When Fixed-Interest Capital $\ll$ Equity Capital
   - Gearing Ratio $< 1.0$
   - Conservative approach, low financial risk

#### Part (b) — Significance of Gearing Ratio (7 marks)

**1. Indicator of Financial Risk (2 marks)**:
A high gearing ratio signals higher financial risk because the firm has substantial fixed interest obligations. Failure to meet these obligations in low-profitability periods can lead to insolvency.

**2. Impact on EPS (2 marks)**:
Gearing directly amplifies EPS movements. A high-geared firm experiences larger swings in EPS for the same change in EBIT — magnifying both gains and losses. This is quantified by the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL).

**3. Cost of Capital and Valuation (2 marks)**:
Debt is generally cheaper than equity due to the tax shield. A firm with optimal gearing can lower its WACC, thereby increasing its market valuation. However, excessive gearing raises the cost of debt as well due to higher default risk.

**4. Credit Rating and Lender Confidence (1 mark)**:
Lenders and credit rating agencies closely examine the gearing ratio. A high gearing may result in a lower credit rating, increasing the future cost of borrowing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing **Capital Structure** with **Financial Structure** — remember the boundary (short-term vs long-term sources).
> - **Common Mistake 2**: In DFL formula, forgetting the **Preference Dividend (PD)** component. The correct form is $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - PD}$, not just $EBIT/(EBIT-I)$.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Stating the debt-equity ratio without specifying **book value vs market value** — always clarify.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting that interest is **tax-deductible** but preference dividend is **not**. This affects WACC calculations.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Classifying gearing without computing the actual ratio. Always show the calculation step-by-step for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**:
>
> ✅ **Capital Structure = Long-term sources only** (debt + preference + equity + retained earnings)
>
> ✅ **Financial Structure = Capital Structure + Current Liabilities** (broader concept)
>
> ✅ **Optimal Capital Structure** = Mix that **minimizes WACC** and **maximizes firm value**
>
> ✅ **Trading on Equity** = Favourable when **ROI $>$ Cost of Debt (Kd)**; amplifies EPS
>
> ✅ **Capital Gearing** = Ratio of Fixed-Return Capital to Equity Capital
>
> ✅ **Three Patterns**: Conservative (Low Geared) / Balanced (Moderate) / Aggressive (High Geared)
>
> ✅ **DFL Formula (with PD)**: $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - PD}$
>
> ✅ **DFL Formula (without PD)**: $DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$
>
> ✅ **Interest is tax-deductible**, Preference Dividend is **NOT** tax-deductible
>
> ✅ **Debt-Equity Ratio** = Long-term Debt / Shareholders' Funds (book value basis default)
>
> ✅ **Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR)** = EBIT / Interest Expense (key solvency metric)
>
> ✅ **High Gearing = High Financial Risk but High Reward Potential**
>
> ✅ **Low Gearing = Low Financial Risk but Higher WACC**
>
> ✅ **Scope of Capital Structure Decision** includes: mix determination, debt type, timing, form, cost, risk
>
> ✅ **KTU Emphasis**: Numerical problems on DFL, gearing ratio, and pattern classification are **very high probability** in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Structure Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital Structure Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Capital Structure?

**Capital Structure** refers to the specific mix of long-term sources of funds (debt and equity) that a firm uses to finance its assets and operations. It represents the permanent financing of the company, comprising share capital (equity), preference share capital, retained earnings, and long-term debt (debentures, bonds, term loans).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Solomon & Pringle, 1977):**
> *"Capital Structure is the permanent financing of the company represented by long-term debt, preference share capital, and net worth (equity share capital and retained earnings)."*

### 1.2 Capital Structure vs. Financial Structure

These two terms are often confused. Here is the precise distinction:

| Aspect | Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Narrow concept | Broad concept |
| **Components** | Long-term debt, Preference shares, Equity (Net Worth) | Entire left-hand side of Balance Sheet (LHS) |
| **Includes** | Permanent / Long-term financing only | Both long-term AND short-term sources |
| **Excludes** | Current liabilities, Trade creditors, Short-term loans | Nothing — covers all financing |
| **Nature** | Strategic (quality of financing) | Operational (quantum of financing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "Capital Structure = ONLY long-term funds, while Financial Structure = ALL funds (long-term + short-term)."

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Business

Imagine you are opening a restaurant. You have two options to raise **₹10 Lakhs**:
- **Option A (Equity):** Convince 5 friends to invest ₹2 Lakhs each. They become co-owners and share profits/dividends permanently.
- **Option B (Debt):** Borrow ₹4 Lakhs from a bank @ 12% interest. You must pay interest every month AND return the principal after 5 years.

The **mix** you choose (₹6L equity + ₹4L debt) is your **Capital Structure**. Now, the question becomes:
- Should you borrow MORE (more debt) so you can keep more profits for yourself?
- Or LESS (more equity) to be safe during low-sales months?

This trade-off between **risk and return** is the heart of Capital Structure Decisions.

### 1.4 Key Strategic Objectives

- **Minimize the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**
- **Maximize the Market Value of the Firm (Vx)**
- **Minimize the Overall Financial Risk**

### 1.5 Why Capital Structure Decisions are CRUCIAL

A wrong mix can bankrupt a profitable firm. The **proportion of debt in the capital structure** directly affects:
- **Earnings Per Share (EPS)**
- **Return on Equity (ROE)**
- **Financial Risk** (bankruptcy probability)
- **Cost of Capital (Ko)**
- **Stock Market Price (Px)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Capital Structure decision is a **financing decision** that affects the long-term solvency, profitability, and valuation of the firm. It is a **continuous strategic decision** as financing needs evolve.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Capital Structure

The capital structure consists of three fundamental building blocks:

1. **Equity Share Capital (E)** — Permanent ownership capital with no fixed obligation; dividend is paid out of profits.
2. **Preference Share Capital (P)** — Hybrid security with fixed dividend obligation but no voting rights.
3. **Long-term Debt (D)** — Includes debentures, bonds, term loans from banks/financial institutions with mandatory interest payments.

### 2.2 Financial Leverage — The Engine of Capital Structure

Financial leverage arises from the presence of **fixed financing charges** (interest + preference dividend) in the capital structure. It is measured through the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**.

$$DFL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{PD}{(1 - t)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Where **I** = Interest on debt, **PD** = Preference Dividend, **t** = Tax rate.

### 2.3 Three Types of Leverage (KTU-Favorite Triad)

| Type | Source of Fixed Cost | Numerator | Denominator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Leverage (DOL)** | Fixed Operating Costs (FC) | Contribution (S − VC) | EBIT |
| **Financial Leverage (DFL)** | Interest & Preference Dividend | EBIT | EBT |
| **Combined Leverage (DCL)** | Both | Contribution | EBT |

**Formulas in detail:**

$$DOL = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{S - VC}{S - VC - FC}$$

$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - PD}$$

$$DCL = DOL \times DFL = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{EBT}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS)

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Value of Firm (Vx) | $V_x = S + B$ | S = Value of Equity, B = Value of Debt |
| 2 | Cost of Equity (Ke) | $K_e = \frac{NI}{S} \text{ or } \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{S}$ | Required return by equity holders |
| 3 | Cost of Debt (Kd) | $K_d = \frac{I}{B} \times (1 - t)$ | After-tax cost |
| 4 | Overall Cost of Capital (Ko) | $K_o = K_e \left(\frac{S}{V}\right) + K_d \left(\frac{B}{V}\right)$ | Weighted Average |
| 5 | MM Proposition I (No Tax) | $V_L = V_U$ | Capital structure irrelevant |
| 6 | MM Proposition II (No Tax) | $K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \frac{D}{E}$ | Ke rises linearly with leverage |
| 7 | MM Proposition I (With Tax) | $V_L = V_U + T_c \cdot B$ | Debt creates value via tax shield |
| 8 | EPS | $\frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t) - PD}{N}$ | N = Number of equity shares |
| 9 | Indifference Point (EBIT*) | $\frac{(I_1 - I_2)(1 - t) + (PD_1 - PD_2)}{N_2 - N_1} \times N_1$ | EBIT at which two plans give same EPS |
| 10 | Indifference Point (Final Form) | $EBIT^* = \frac{(I_1 - I_2)(1 - t) + (PD_1 - PD_2)}{(N_2 - N_1) / N_1 - (N_2 - N_1) / N_2}$ | Use this consolidated form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Symbol Convention:** Throughout KTU valuation, $\mid$ is reserved for the OR operator. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value brackets in tables. Example: $\vert D/E \vert$ is correctly written as $\left\vert \frac{D}{E} \right\vert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Corporate Finance

- **Project Finance Decision:** Engineering firms bidding for large infrastructure projects must decide how to finance plant setup — using a mix of bank loans (debt) and promoter contribution (equity).
- **Leverage for ROE Boost:** A ₹100 Cr firm with 10% EBIT can deliver 20% ROE if financed 50:50 (debt:equity), but doubles bankruptcy risk.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A):** The acquirer's capital structure post-merger significantly impacts combined firm's WACC and valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Modigliani-Miller Proposition II (No Tax)

**Statement:** The cost of equity (Ke) increases linearly with the debt-equity ratio because equity holders demand higher returns to compensate for increased financial risk.

**Step 1:** Start with the relationship of total firm value:
$$V_x = S + B$$

**Step 2:** Total market value of the firm equals capitalized expected operating income (EBIT) at the overall cost of capital (Ko):
$$V_x = \frac{EBIT(1 - t)}{K_o}$$

**Step 3:** The value of equity (S) is the capitalized net income to equity holders:
$$S = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{K_e}$$

**Step 4:** Since $K_e$ represents the expected return on equity, the market-clearing condition is:
$$K_e = \frac{(EBIT - I)(1 - t)}{S}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $I = B \cdot K_d$ (Interest = Debt × Cost of Debt) into the equation:
$$K_e = \frac{(EBIT - B \cdot K_d)(1 - t)}{S}$$

**Step 6:** Divide numerator and denominator to get a clean expression:
$$K_e = \frac{EBIT(1 - t) - B \cdot K_d(1 - t)}{S}$$

**Step 7:** Using $V_x = S + B$, multiply $K_o \cdot V_x = EBIT(1-t)$:
$$EBIT(1 - t) = K_o(S + B)$$

**Step 8:** Substituting back:
$$K_e = \frac{K_o(S + B) - K_d \cdot B}{S}$$

**Step 9:** Simplify by splitting the fraction:
$$K_e = \frac{K_o \cdot S + K_o \cdot B - K_d \cdot B}{S}$$

**Step 10:** Final form — **MM Proposition II (No Tax):**
$$K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d)\frac{B}{S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For every additional unit of debt added, the cost of equity increases by $(K_o - K_d)\frac{B}{S}$. Crucially, $K_o$ remains **constant** because the increase in Ke exactly offsets the lower-cost debt. Hence, $V_x$ remains unchanged.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Indifference Point (EBIT*)

The indifference point is the **level of EBIT** at which two alternative capital structure plans yield **identical EPS**.

**Step 1:** Set EPS of Plan 1 equal to EPS of Plan 2:
$$\frac{(EBIT - I_1)(1 - t) - PD_1}{N_1} = \frac{(EBIT - I_2)(1 - t) - PD_2}{N_2}$$

**Step 2:** Cross-multiply:
$$N_2[(EBIT - I_1)(1 - t) - PD_1] = N_1[(EBIT - I_2)(1 - t) - PD_2]$$

**Step 3:** Expand both sides:
$$N_2 \cdot EBIT(1-t) - N_2 \cdot I_1(1-t) - N_2 \cdot PD_1 = N_1 \cdot EBIT(1-t) - N_1 \cdot I_2(1-t) - N_1 \cdot PD_2$$

**Step 4:** Bring all EBIT terms to LHS:
$$EBIT(1-t)[N_2 - N_1] = N_2 \cdot I_1(1-t) - N_1 \cdot I_2(1-t) + N_2 \cdot PD_1 - N_1 \cdot PD_2$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $(1-t)$ from interest terms:
$$EBIT(1-t)[N_2 - N_1] = (1-t)[N_2 \cdot I_1 - N_1 \cdot I_2] + [N_2 \cdot PD_1 - N_1 \cdot PD_2]$$

**Step 6:** Solve for EBIT:
$$EBIT^* = \frac{(1-t)[N_2 \cdot I_1 - N_1 \cdot I_2] + [N_2 \cdot PD_1 - N_1 \cdot PD_2]}{(1-t)[N_2 - N_1]}$$

**Step 7:** Final simplified KTU-board form:
$$EBIT^* = \frac{(I_1 - I_2)(1 - t) + (PD_1 - PD_2)}{(N_2 - N_1)/N_1 \text{ (debit adjustment term)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (Post-Indifference Point Analysis):**
> - If **expected EBIT > EBIT***: Choose the plan with **HIGHER** financial leverage (more debt) for higher EPS.
> - If **expected EBIT < EBIT***: Choose the plan with **LOWER** financial leverage (more equity) to avoid distress.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Capital Structure Theories Comparison

| Theory | Firm Value (Vx) | Cost of Capital (Ko) | Cost of Equity (Ke) | Assumptions | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Net Income (NI)** | Increases with debt | Decreases with debt | Constant | Ke and Kd are constant | Accepts relevance of CS |
| **Net Operating Income (NOI)** | Constant (Irrelevant) | Constant | Rises with leverage | Overall Ko is constant | Capital structure IRRELEVANT |
| **Traditional** | First increases, then decreases | First decreases, then increases | Rises gradually then sharply | Optimal structure exists | Accepts relevance with optimum |
| **MM (No Tax)** | Constant | Constant | $K_o + (K_o - K_d)\frac{B}{S}$ | Perfect markets | IRRELEVANT (No Tax) |
| **MM (With Tax)** | $V_U + T_c \cdot B$ | Decreases continuously | Rises, but slower | Corporate tax exists | RELEVANT — Debt creates value |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** XYZ Ltd. has two financing alternatives:
- **Plan 1:** Equity of ₹10,00,000 (10,000 shares @ ₹100), 12% Debt of ₹5,00,000
- **Plan 2:** Equity of ₹5,00,000 (5,000 shares @ ₹100), 12% Debt of ₹10,00,000
- Tax rate: 30%, Expected EBIT: ₹2,00,000

**Find:** Indifference Point, EPS at expected EBIT, and recommend the best plan.

**Step 1: Calculate EPS under both plans at expected EBIT**

For Plan 1:
$$EPS_1 = \frac{(EBIT - I_1)(1 - t)}{N_1} = \frac{(2,00,000 - 60,000)(0.7)}{10,000} = \frac{98,000}{10,000} = ₹9.80$$

For Plan 2:
$$EPS_2 = \frac{(EBIT - I_2)(1 - t)}{N_2} = \frac{(2,00,000 - 1,20,000)(0.7)}{5,000} = \frac{56,000}{5,000} = ₹11.20$$

**Step 2: Calculate Indifference Point**

$$EBIT^* = \frac{(I_1 - I_2)(1 - t) + (PD_1 - PD_2)}{(N_2 - N_1) \text{ adjusted}}$$

Since $I_1 = ₹60,000$, $I_2 = ₹1,20,000$, $PD_1 = PD_2 = 0$, $N_1 = 10,000$, $N_2 = 5,000$:

$$EBIT^* = \frac{(60,000 - 1,20,000)(0.7)}{5,000 - 10,000} \times 10,000 = \frac{-42,000}{-5,000} \times 10,000$$

$$EBIT^* = 8.4 \times 10,000 = ₹84,000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When expected EBIT (₹2,00,000) > Indifference Point (₹84,000), the more leveraged Plan 2 is preferred because it yields higher EPS (₹11.20 vs ₹9.80). At exactly ₹84,000 EBIT, both plans give the same EPS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Capital Structure Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Firm Needs Long-Term Funds] --> B[Identify Sources of Finance]
    B --> C[Debt Instruments]
    B --> D[Equity Instruments]
    B --> E[Hybrid Instruments]
    C --> C1[Debentures and Bonds]
    C --> C2[Term Loans from Banks]
    C --> C3[Convertible Debentures]
    D --> D1[Equity Share Capital]
    D --> D2[Retained Earnings]
    D --> D3[Preference Shares]
    E --> E1[Convertible Preference Shares]
    E --> E2[Deep Discount Bonds]
    C1 --> F[Determine Optimal Mix]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G{Apply Capital Structure Theories}
    G --> G1[NI Approach]
    G --> G2[NOI Approach]
    G --> G3[Traditional Approach]
    G --> G4[MM Approach]
    G1 --> H[Compute WACC and Firm Value]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Select Optimal Capital Structure]
    I --> J[Maximize Vx]
    I --> K[Minimize WACC]
    J --> L[Final Decision]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Capital Structure Theoretical Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASSICAL[Classical Theories]
        A1[NI Approach]
        A2[NOI Approach]
        A3[Traditional Approach]
    end
    subgraph MODERN[Modern Theories]
        B1[MM No Tax]
        B2[MM With Tax]
        B3[Miller 1977 with Personal Tax]
    end
    subgraph LEVERAGE[Risk Return Analysis]
        C1[Operating Leverage DOL]
        C2[Financial Leverage DFL]
        C3[Combined Leverage DCL]
        C4[EBIT EPS Indifference Point]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOMES[Final Outcomes]
        D1[Firm Value Vx]
        D2[Cost of Capital Ko]
        D3[EPS and ROE]
    end
    CLASSICAL --> LEVERAGE
    MODERN --> LEVERAGE
    LEVERAGE --> OUTCOMES
    D1 --> E[Capital Structure Decision]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

### 4.3 EBIT-EPS Behavior Under Different Plans

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Expected EBIT Level] --> B{Compare with EBIT* Indifference Point}
    B -->|EBIT greater than EBIT*| C[Choose HIGH Leverage Plan]
    B -->|EBIT less than EBIT*| D[Choose LOW Leverage Plan]
    B -->|EBIT equals EBIT*| E[Both Plans are Indifferent]
    C --> F[Higher EPS with Higher Risk]
    D --> G[Lower but Stable EPS with Lower Risk]
    E --> H[EPS is Same Across Plans]
    F --> I[Verify ROE and Financial Risk]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Final Decision Based on Risk Appetite}
    J -->|Risk Taker| K[Choose Leveraged Plan]
    J -->|Risk Averse| L[Choose Conservative Plan]
```

### 4.4 Internal Architecture — Modigliani-Miller Propositions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[Proposition 1]
        P1A[Proposition 1A No Tax: V_L = V_U]
        P1B[Proposition 1B With Tax: V_L = V_U + Tc x B]
    end
    subgraph P2[Proposition 2]
        P2A[No Tax: Ke = Ko + Ko minus Kd x B over S]
        P2B[With Tax: Ke = KoU + KoU minus Kd x 1 minus Tc x B over S]
    end
    subgraph P3[Proposition 3]
        P3A[Investment Decision Independent of Financing]
        P3B[Accept all positive NPV projects]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3A --> OUT[Final Outcome: Optimal Investment Policy]
    P3B --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Architecture?** The Mermaid block diagram represents the conceptual relationships between the three MM Propositions and their implications, allowing students to visualize how propositions logically lead to the next, even when rendering a complex mathematical hierarchy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Capital Structure. How is it different from Financial Structure?**

**Model Answer:**
Capital Structure refers to the mix of long-term sources of funds used by a firm to finance its operations. It includes equity share capital, preference share capital, retained earnings, and long-term debt (Solomon & Pringle, 1977).

| Aspect | Capital Structure | Financial Structure |
|---|---|---|
| Coverage | Only long-term funds | All funds (long-term + short-term) |
| Includes | Equity, Debt, Preference | Includes current liabilities too |
| Nature | Strategic | Comprehensive |

(3 marks for definition + comparison table)

---

**Q2. What is Financial Leverage? Explain Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL).**

**Model Answer:**
Financial Leverage is the potential use of fixed-cost financing (debt + preference share capital) to amplify the effects of changes in EBIT on EPS.

$$DFL = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}} = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{PD}{(1-t)}}$$

**Interpretation:** A DFL of 2 means a 1% change in EBIT causes a 2% change in EPS. Higher DFL implies higher financial risk but greater reward potential.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for DFL formula + interpretation)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Net Income (NI) Approach** and **Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach** to capital structure with suitable diagrams. Critically compare both approaches. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Net Income (NI) Approach** (Proposed by Durand, 1952):

**Assumptions:**
1. The cost of debt (Kd) and cost of equity (Ke) are constant and independent of leverage.
2. The firm uses only debt and equity.
3. Corporate taxes are ignored.

**Key Propositions:**
- As debt increases, WACC (Ko) decreases because cheaper debt replaces costlier equity.
- Firm value (Vx) increases as debt increases.
- Hence, capital structure IS relevant; the firm should use **maximum debt**.

**Formulas:**
$$K_o = K_e \left(\frac{S}{V}\right) + K_d \left(\frac{B}{V}\right)$$

$$V_x = S + B = \frac{NI}{K_e} + \frac{I}{K_d}$$

---

**Net Operating Income (NOI) Approach:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The overall cost of capital (Ko) is constant, independent of leverage.
2. WACC is determined by the business risk of the firm, not financial structure.
3. The cost of equity (Ke) increases with leverage to offset benefits of cheaper debt.

**Key Propositions:**
- $V_x$ remains **constant** regardless of capital structure.
- Capital structure is **IRRELEVANT** to firm value.
- Any savings from cheap debt are exactly offset by increased Ke.

**Critical Comparison:**

| Aspect | NI Approach | NOI Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Firm Value | Variable (increases with debt) | Constant |
| Cost of Capital | Variable (decreases with debt) | Constant |
| Cost of Equity | Constant | Variable (increases) |
| Verdict | CS is RELEVANT | CS is IRRELEVANT |

> The NOI Approach is mathematically more rigorous and is supported by MM. The NI Approach is oversimplified and unrealistic. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**Q. (b)** A firm has the following capital structure proposals: **(7 Marks)**

| Particulars | Plan 1 | Plan 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Equity (₹) | 10,00,000 | 6,00,000 |
| Debt @ 10% (₹) | 4,00,000 | 8,00,000 |
| Number of Equity Shares | 10,000 | 6,000 |

Expected EBIT: ₹2,50,000, Tax rate: 30%

**Compute:**
1. EPS under both plans
2. Indifference Point
3. Recommend the best plan

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate EPS**

For Plan 1:
$$EPS_1 = \frac{(2,50,000 - 40,000)(0.7) - 0}{10,000} = \frac{1,47,000}{10,000} = ₹14.70$$

For Plan 2:
$$EPS_2 = \frac{(2,50,000 - 80,000)(0.7) - 0}{6,000} = \frac{1,19,000}{6,000} = ₹19.83$$

**Step 2: Indifference Point**

$$EBIT^* = \frac{(I_1 - I_2)(1 - t) + (PD_1 - PD_2)}{(N_2 - N_1)/N_1} = \frac{(40,000 - 80,000)(0.7)}{(6,000 - 10,000)/10,000}$$

$$EBIT^* = \frac{-28,000}{-0.4} = ₹70,000$$

**Step 3: Recommendation**

Since expected EBIT (₹2,50,000) > Indifference Point (₹70,000), **Plan 2 (Higher Leverage)** is recommended. It offers higher EPS (₹19.83 vs ₹14.70), although with greater financial risk.

(7 marks: 2 for EPS, 3 for indifference point, 2 for recommendation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Discuss **Modigliani-Miller (MM) Approach** to capital structure with and without taxes. What are the key assumptions? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Key Assumptions of MM:**
1. Perfect capital markets (no taxes, no transaction costs, no bankruptcy costs)
2. Homogeneous expectations among investors
3. Risk-free borrowing and lending
4. No corporate or personal taxes (in basic model)
5. Constant EBIT (zero growth perpetuity)
6. All earnings are distributed as dividends

---

**MM Without Tax:**

**Proposition I:** $V_L = V_U$

The market value of the levered firm equals the value of the unlevered firm. Capital structure is **irrelevant**.

**Proposition II:**
$$K_e = K_o + (K_o - K_d) \frac{B}{S}$$

The cost of equity increases linearly with leverage.

**Proposition III:** Investment decisions are independent of financing decisions. The firm should accept all positive NPV projects at WACC.

---

**MM With Tax (1963 Revision):**

**Proposition I:**
$$V_L = V_U + T_c \cdot B$$

The levered firm is more valuable due to the tax shield on interest. The optimal structure is **100% debt**.

**Proposition II:**
$$K_e = K_o^U + (K_o^U - K_d)(1 - T_c) \frac{B}{S}$$

The cost of equity increases at a **slower rate** than in the no-tax case because debt creates value.

> **Conclusion:** Taxes make capital structure **RELEVANT**. Debt should be maximized to capture maximum tax benefit. (7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 2 each for without-tax and with-tax propositions, 1 for conclusion)

---

**Q. (b)** Calculate the **Operating Leverage (DOL)**, **Financial Leverage (DFL)**, and **Combined Leverage (DCL)** from the following data: **(7 Marks)**

- Sales = ₹10,00,000
- Variable Costs = 60% of Sales
- Fixed Costs = ₹2,00,000
- Interest = ₹50,000
- Preference Dividend = ₹20,000
- Tax rate = 30%

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate Contribution**
$$\text{Contribution} = S - VC = 10,00,000 - 6,00,000 = ₹4,00,000$$

**Step 2: Calculate EBIT**
$$EBIT = \text{Contribution} - FC = 4,00,000 - 2,00,000 = ₹2,00,000$$

**Step 3: Calculate EBT**
$$EBT = EBIT - I = 2,00,000 - 50,000 = ₹1,50,000$$

**Step 4: Calculate DOL**
$$DOL = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{EBIT} = \frac{4,00,000}{2,00,000} = 2.0$$

**Step 5: Calculate DFL**
$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBT - \frac{PD}{(1-t)}} = \frac{2,00,000}{1,50,000 - \frac{20,000}{0.7}}$$

$$DFL = \frac{2,00,000}{1,50,000 - 28,571} = \frac{2,00,000}{1,21,429} = 1.65$$

**Step 6: Calculate DCL**
$$DCL = DOL \times DFL = 2.0 \times 1.65 = 3.30$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1% change in sales causes a 3.30% change in EPS. The firm has **HIGH combined leverage** and is highly sensitive to sales fluctuations. (7 marks: 2 each for DOL and DFL, 1 for DCL, 1 for interpretation)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Capital Structure with Financial Structure** — Capital Structure is ONLY long-term; Financial Structure includes short-term liabilities.
> 2. **Indifference Point Formula** — Students often forget to multiply $(I_1 - I_2)$ by $(1-t)$ and forget the preference dividend adjustment. ALWAYS check if PD is non-zero.
> 3. **MM Approach Direction** — In MM No-Tax, Ke and Ko both constant; in MM With-Tax, Ke rises slower. Don't interchange the propositions.
> 4. **Degree of Leverage** — DFL denominator is EBT (not EAT). Use the form $EBIT / EBT$ unless PD is given, in which case use $EBIT / (EBT - PD/(1-t))$.
> 5. **Sign Convention in Indifference Point** — When $N_2 < N_1$ (fewer shares in Plan 2), the denominator is negative, so the numerator must also be negative. Handle signs carefully.
> 6. **Units and Currency** — Always write the final EPS in ₹/share and EBIT in ₹ Lakhs/Crores. State units explicitly to fetch full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capital Structure** = Mix of long-term debt, preference share capital, and equity (Net Worth). It is a subset of **Financial Structure**.
- **Optimal Capital Structure** is the mix that minimizes WACC and maximizes firm value simultaneously.
- **MM Approach (No Tax)** concludes capital structure is IRRELEVANT because increased Ke exactly offsets lower-cost debt.
- **MM Approach (With Tax)** concludes that debt creates value through tax shields: $V_L = V_U + T_c \cdot B$.
- **Traditional Approach** lies between NI and NOI — accepts relevance but identifies an optimum debt level.
- **Indifference Point (EBIT*)** is the EBIT at which two financing plans yield equal EPS. Above this point, prefer higher leverage; below, prefer lower leverage.
- **Financial Leverage (DFL)** = EBIT / EBT. It magnifies the effect of EBIT changes on EPS.
- **Combined Leverage (DCL)** = DOL × DFL. It captures the total business + financial risk of the firm.
- **Cost of Capital (Ko)** is the weighted average: $K_o = K_e (S/V) + K_d (B/V)$.
- **Factors Affecting Capital Structure** include: (i) Financial Leverage & EBIT Stability, (ii) Cost of Capital, (iii) Cash Flow Position, (iv) Tax Rate, (v) Market Conditions, (vi) Firm Size, (vii) Nature of Industry, (viii) Growth Prospects, (ix) Legal Framework, (x) Lender's Attitude.
- **Practical Implication:** Engineering firms in capital-intensive industries (Infrastructure, Power, Manufacturing) typically carry higher debt-to-equity ratios (1:1 to 2:1) than service firms (0.3:1 to 0.5:1).
- **Key Mnemonic for Theories:** "**N**et **I**ncome = **I**ncreases value with debt; **N**et **O**perating **I**ncome = **O**verall value stays constant; **T**raditional = **T**rading off between the two extremes."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Capital: Cost of Specific Sources of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cost of Capital: Cost of Specific Sources of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cost of Capital** is the minimum rate of return that a firm must earn on its investment projects to maintain the market value of its shares and satisfy the expectations of its investors (debt-holders, preference shareholders, and equity shareholders). It represents the **opportunity cost of funds** employed in the business.

**Cost of Specific Sources of Capital** refers to the **component cost** or **specific cost** of capital — the individual cost of each distinct source of finance used by a firm. Since a firm raises capital from multiple sources, each carries its own risk profile and required return, and must therefore be evaluated separately before computing the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**.

The four major specific sources covered in KTU Module 3 are:

1. **Cost of Equity Share Capital (Kₑ)** — Long-term funds raised by issuing ordinary shares.
2. **Cost of Preference Share Capital (Kₚ)** — Fixed-dividend, hybrid securities.
3. **Cost of Debt (K_d)** — Long-term borrowings (debentures, bonds, term loans).
4. **Cost of Retained Earnings (Kᵣ)** — Internal accruals ploughed back into the firm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The cost of capital is **always expressed as an annual percentage rate (%)**, and it is **always computed on an after-tax basis** (especially for debt) because interest is a tax-deductible expense. This is a recurring 3-mark and 7-mark question area in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Investor Rent" Intuition

Imagine you want to start a small tea stall. To do so, you borrow ₹50,000 from your friend Ramesh (debt), take ₹30,000 from your father (retained/internal funds), and raise ₹70,000 by selling a 30% stake in the stall to your cousin Suresh (equity). 

Each person giving you money has an **expectation**:

- **Ramesh** wants his fixed interest (say 10%) — and he also gets a **tax shield** because the government allows the stall to deduct interest from profit.
- **Suresh** is a part-owner; he does not get a fixed return. He only earns if the stall pays dividends or the stall's value grows. He demands a higher return (say 18%) to compensate for his risk.
- **Father's money** (retained earnings) could have earned something elsewhere — that foregone earning is the *opportunity cost*.

**Cost of specific sources of capital is essentially the "rent" each financier charges the firm for using their money.** The total rent the firm must pay is the WACC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The cost of capital is **not** an accounting cost that appears in the books. It is a **finance concept** — the *required* return demanded by investors. If the firm's projects earn less than this required return, the share price falls and the firm destroys shareholder value.

### 1.3 Why Study Specific (Component) Costs?

A firm cannot compute a single "cost of capital" without first identifying the cost of each source. The reasons are:

- **Risk Heterogeneity:** Equity is riskier than debt, so Kₑ > K_d. Averaging them blindly is meaningless.
- **Tax Asymmetry:** Interest on debt is tax-deductible, so the *effective* cost of debt is lower than its nominal rate.
- **Capital Structure Decisions:** Knowing component costs helps in deciding the *optimal mix* of debt and equity (capital structure planning).
- **Project Appraisal:** The cut-off rate (hurdle rate) for accepting/rejecting projects is built from component costs.

### 1.4 Fundamental Assumptions Underlying Cost of Capital Computation

| Assumption | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| **Risk-Return Consistency** | The risk of the new project must match the risk of the existing firm. |
| **Constant Capital Structure** | The proportion of debt and equity is assumed to remain stable. |
| **Investor Rationality** | Investors are risk-averse and demand higher returns for higher risk. |
| **Perfect Capital Markets** | No transaction costs, no flotation costs (in basic models). |
| **Going Concern** | The firm is assumed to operate indefinitely. |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU problems, **flotation costs are often included** in the cost of specific sources. Always check whether the problem gives "Net Proceeds" (already adjusted) or "Issue Price" (requires adjustment).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cost of Equity Share Capital (Kₑ)

Equity shareholders are the *residual owners* of the firm. They receive dividends only after all other claimants (debt, preference) are paid. There is **no legal obligation** to pay dividends. Hence, computing Kₑ involves estimation, not certainty.

There are **three principal approaches** prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

#### A) Dividend Price Approach (Gordon's Growth Model)

**Theory:** The value of an equity share equals the **present value of all future dividends** expected by the shareholder, growing at a constant rate.

**Key Variables:**
- **D₁** = Expected dividend at the end of year 1
- **P₀** = Current ex-dividend market price per share
- **N** = Net proceeds per share (after flotation costs)
- **g** = Constant annual growth rate of dividends
- **D₀** = Last paid dividend

**Logic Steps:**

1. Estimate the next year's expected dividend **D₁ = D₀(1 + g)**.
2. Determine the **net proceeds** the firm actually receives per share.
3. Apply the **Dividend Discount Model (DDM)**: Since dividends grow perpetually, the share price forms a growing perpetuity.
4. Solve the DDM equation for **Kₑ** (the discount rate that equates present value of dividends to the market price).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gordon's Model requires a constant growth rate (g < Kₑ).** If the problem states *variable growth rates* (e.g., super-normal growth for 5 years, then constant), you must compute the **present value of dividends year-by-year** for the non-constant period, then apply Gordon's formula to the terminal value, and finally solve for Kₑ by **trial-and-error** (interpolation).

#### B) Earnings/Price Approach (Earnings Yield Method)

**Theory:** Equity cost equals earnings per share divided by market price.

**Logic:** If shareholders value the firm based on earnings rather than dividends, the cost of equity equals the **earnings yield**.

**Variables:**
- **E** = Earnings per share
- **P₀** = Market price per share

#### C) Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)

**Theory:** The required return on equity is the **risk-free rate** plus a **risk premium** proportional to the firm's systematic risk (beta).

**Key Variables:**
- **R_f** = Risk-free rate (typically 10-year Government of India bond yield)
- **β** = Beta coefficient — measures sensitivity of the stock's returns to market returns
- **R_m** = Expected return on the market portfolio (e.g., Nifty 50)
- **(R_m − R_f)** = Market risk premium

**Logic Steps:**

1. Identify the risk-free rate.
2. Compute or read the firm's beta.
3. Calculate the market risk premium.
4. Apply CAPM: **Kₑ = R_f + β(R_m − R_f)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **β = 1** means the stock moves exactly with the market. **β > 1** indicates higher volatility (aggressive stock). **β < 1** indicates lower volatility (defensive stock). In KTU numericals, beta is *given directly* — you do not need to compute regression.

---

### 2.2 Cost of Preference Share Capital (Kₚ)

Preference shares carry a **fixed dividend rate** and have **no tax-deductibility** of dividends (since dividends are paid out of after-tax profits).

**Logic Steps:**

1. Identify the fixed preference dividend per share (Dₚ).
2. Determine the net proceeds per preference share (Nₚ).
3. Apply the formula: **Kₚ = Dₚ / Nₚ**.

For **redeemable preference shares**, an additional redemption premium and flotation cost are accounted for, similar to redeemable debt.

---

### 2.3 Cost of Debt (K_d)

Debt may be **irredeemable (perpetual)** or **redeemable** (with a maturity date). The tax-deductibility of interest is a critical feature.

#### A) Cost of Irredeemable Debt

**Logic Steps:**

1. Identify annual interest (I) = Face value × Coupon rate.
2. The interest is **tax-deductible**; tax saving = I × t.
3. Net annual interest outflow = **I(1 − t)**.
4. Net proceeds (N_d) = Issue price − Flotation cost.
5. The cost of debt to the firm is the after-tax rate on net proceeds.

#### B) Cost of Redeemable Debt

**Logic Steps:**

1. Identify annual interest, face/redeemable value (R_V), net proceeds (N_d), and remaining life (n years).
2. Annualize the **flotation cost / discount amortized over n years**.
3. The numerator = **After-tax interest + Annual amortization of discount/premium**.
4. The denominator = **Average value of debt** = (R_V + N_d) / 2.

---

### 2.4 Cost of Retained Earnings (Kᵣ)

Retained earnings are **not free**, despite being internal. They have an **opportunity cost** equal to what shareholders could have earned if dividends were paid and reinvested elsewhere.

**Key Principle:** Kᵣ ≈ Kₑ, but **slightly lower** because:
- No flotation costs are involved in using retained earnings.
- Tax on dividend is avoided (especially under the old regime) when profits are retained.

**Approximate Formula:**

$$K_r = K_e \times (1 - \text{tax rate on dividends})$$

In simplified KTU problems, **Kᵣ = Kₑ** is often assumed.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every Part B question.**

| # | Source | Situation | Formula | Units |
|---|--------|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Equity | Irredeemable, zero growth | $K_e = \dfrac{D}{N_e}$ | % per annum |
| 2 | Equity | Irredeemable, constant growth (Gordon) | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{N_e} + g$ | % per annum |
| 3 | Equity | Variable growth + constant growth | Solve $P_0 = \sum \dfrac{D_t}{(1+K_e)^t} + \dfrac{D_{n+1}}{(K_e - g)(1+K_e)^n}$ | Trial-and-error |
| 4 | Equity | Earnings approach | $K_e = \dfrac{E}{N_e}$ | % per annum |
| 5 | Equity | CAPM | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | % per annum |
| 6 | Preference | Irredeemable | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{N_p}$ | % per annum |
| 7 | Preference | Redeemable | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p + \dfrac{(R_V - N_p)}{n}}{\dfrac{(R_V + N_p)}{2}}$ | % per annum |
| 8 | Debt | Irredeemable (after-tax) | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t)}{N_d}$ | % per annum |
| 9 | Debt | Redeemable (after-tax) | $K_d = \dfrac{I(1-t) + \dfrac{(R_V - N_d)}{n}}{\dfrac{(R_V + N_d)}{2}}$ | % per annum |
| 10 | Retained Earnings | Opportunity cost | $K_r = K_e (1 - t_d)$ ≈ $K_e$ | % per annum |
| 11 | Weighted Average | Composite WACC | $K_o = \sum w_i K_i$ | % per annum |

> **Key Reminders:**
> - **D** = Annual dividend; **D₁** = Expected next-year dividend.
> - **Nₑ, Nₚ, N_d** = Net proceeds per share/bond (after issue expenses).
> - **R_V** = Redeemable/face value; **n** = Number of years to redemption.
> - **I** = Annual interest in rupees; **t** = Corporate tax rate.
> - **g** = Constant growth rate (must be **less than Kₑ**).
> - All costs are **annualized** and expressed in **% per annum**.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Finance & Engineering Economics

- **Capital Budgeting:** The component costs form the **discount rate (WACC)** for evaluating NPV of new engineering/infrastructure projects.
- **Valuation:** Firm valuation models (DCF) use WACC as the discount rate.
- **Optimal Capital Structure:** Comparing Kₑ vs. K_d at different debt levels helps identify the *optimal debt-equity mix*.
- **Cost Control:** Knowing the cost of each source allows finance managers to choose the *cheapest viable* source of capital.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** A firm's ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) is compared with WACC to assess value creation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cost of Equity using Gordon's Growth Model

**Starting Premise:** The market price of an equity share is the present value of all expected future dividends growing at a constant rate *g* perpetually.

**Step 1: Write the present value of a growing perpetuity (Gordon's formula).**

$$P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{(K_e - g)}$$

This is the Gordon Growth Model, where the share price is the discounted value of an infinite stream of growing dividends.

**Step 2: Replace P₀ with the net proceeds (Nₑ) the firm receives from issuing the share.**

The firm does not receive the *market price* directly — it receives the price net of issue expenses (underwriting commission, brokerage, printing, etc.). So we substitute:

$$N_e = \dfrac{D_1}{(K_e - g)}$$

**Step 3: Cross-multiply the denominator.**

$$N_e (K_e - g) = D_1$$
