   - Eurobonds & Foreign Currency Loans

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Domestic Sources of Finance — Detailed Anatomy

### A. Owned Capital (Shareholders' Funds)

#### (i) Equity Share Capital
- Represents **ownership** in the company.
- Shareholders are the **last to be paid** in liquidation but have **voting rights**.
- Dividend is **not fixed**; it depends on profits.
- **Cost:** High, since dividends are not tax-deductible.
- **Best for:** Long-term, permanent capital needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Right Shares vs. Bonus Shares:**
> - **Right Issue:** Existing shareholders are offered new shares *in proportion* to their holding at a discounted price.
> - **Bonus Issue:** Free shares issued out of accumulated reserves/securities premium — *no cash inflow* to the company.

#### (ii) Preference Share Capital
- Carries a **fixed dividend**, paid *before* equity shareholders.
- **No voting rights** (generally), hence no dilution of control.
- Redeemable after a fixed tenure.
- **Types:** Cumulative, Non-cumulative, Participating, Convertible, Redeemable.

#### (iii) Retained Earnings
- The portion of net profit **ploughed back** into the business.
- **Cheapest source of finance** (no flotation cost, no dividend obligation).
- However, represents **opportunity cost** to shareholders (foregone dividends).
- **Cost of Retained Earnings** = Cost of Equity × (1 - Tax Rate) approximately, but adjusted for flotation and personal tax of shareholders.

### B. Borrowed Capital (Debt Funds)

#### (i) Debentures / Bonds
- A debenture is an **acknowledgement of debt** issued under the company's seal.
- Carries a fixed coupon rate, redeemable on a specified date.
- **Secured** (mortgaged against assets) or **Unsecured**.
- **Cost is tax-deductible** — interest is allowed as an expense under the Companies Act and Income Tax Act, 1961.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Note:**
> The **tax shield** benefit of debt is captured by the formula:
> $$\text{After-tax Cost of Debt} = K_d \times (1 - t)$$
> where $K_d$ is the contractual interest rate and $t$ is the corporate tax rate. This makes debt cheaper than equity in most jurisdictions, including India.

#### (ii) Term Loans from Banks & Financial Institutions
- Sourced from **Scheduled Commercial Banks**, **IDBI**, **SIDBI**, **NABARD**, **IFCI**, etc.
- Used for acquiring **fixed assets** (plant, machinery, building).
- Repayable in **instalments** (monthly/quarterly/annually).
- **Secured** by collateral — primary or secondary.

#### (iii) Working Capital Loans / Short-term Borrowings
- **Cash Credit (CC):** Operates like a running account, with interest charged on the utilised amount.
- **Overdraft (OD):** Allows withdrawal beyond the deposit balance, up to a sanctioned limit.
- **Short-term loans:** Bullet repayment within 12 months.
- **Bill Discounting:** Selling trade receivables (bills) to a bank at a discount.

#### (iv) Trade Credit
- Credit extended by **suppliers** of raw materials and finished goods.
- A form of **spontaneous, interest-free** short-term financing.
- **Cost of Trade Credit** = (Discount % / (100 - Discount %)) × (365 / (Credit Period - Discount Period))
- Best example: "2/10 Net 30" — 2% discount if paid in 10 days, else full payment in 30 days.

#### (v) Commercial Paper (CP)
- An **unsecured, short-term promissory note** issued by large, creditworthy corporates.
- Issued in **multiples of ₹5 Lakhs**, for tenures of 15 days to 1 year.
- Sold at a discount and redeemed at face value.
- Regulated by **RBI**.

#### (vi) Public Deposits
- Deposits invited from the **general public** (not relatives) by Non-Banking Financial Companies (NBFCs) and companies.
- Tenure: 6 months to 3 years.
- Regulated by the **Companies (Acceptance of Deposits) Rules, 2014**.

---

## 2.2 International Sources of Finance — Detailed Anatomy

### A. Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)
- **Direct, long-term investment** by a foreign entity in the production/business of the host country.
- Involves **management control** or **significant influence** (typically ≥10% equity in listed companies, per RBI).
- **Two routes in India:**
  - **Automatic Route:** No prior government approval required (most sectors).
  - **Government Route:** Requires approval from the relevant ministry/department.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FDI vs. FPI — KTU Favourite Distinction:**
> - **FDI:** Investor takes a **strategic, controlling interest** → aims at production, market access, long-term growth.
> - **FPI:** Investor is a **passive financial investor** → aims at capital gains, dividends, no management control.

### B. External Commercial Borrowings (ECB)
- **Commercial loans** availed from **non-resident lenders** with a minimum average maturity of **3 years** (under RBI's ECB Framework).
- Eligible borrowers: Indian corporates, NBFCs, SEZ units, etc.
- Permitted currencies: USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, AUD, CAD, CHF, etc.
- **End-use restrictions:** Cannot be used for real estate, stock markets, on-lending, investment in capital markets (with limited exceptions).

### C. Depository Receipts (ADRs / GDRs)

| Feature | ADR (American Depository Receipt) | GDR (Global Depository Receipt) |
|---|---|---|
| **Traded in** | US Stock Exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ) | Multiple countries (usually Europe) |
| **Issued by** | Indian company to US investors | Indian company to global investors |
| **Denominated in** | USD | USD, EUR, GBP |
| **Compliance** | SEC regulations | EU / UK listing rules |
| **Minimum Issue** | Generally large | Slightly flexible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism:** A *Depository Bank* in the foreign country holds the underlying Indian shares and issues the DR to foreign investors. The Indian company effectively raises rupee capital, which is converted to foreign currency.

### D. Foreign Currency Convertible Bonds (FCCBs)
- A hybrid instrument — a **bond that can be converted into equity shares** of the issuing company at a predetermined exchange ratio.
- Attractive to issuers because the **coupon rate is lower** than vanilla debt (investors get the upside of conversion).
- Attractive to investors because they get **fixed income + equity upside**.

### E. Eurobonds / Foreign Bonds
- A bond issued in a currency **different from the currency of the country** where it is issued.
- Example: An Indian company issuing a USD-denominated bond in London is issuing a Eurobond.
- The "Yankee Bond" (USD bond in the US by a non-US issuer), "Samurai Bond" (JPY bond in Japan), "Bulldog Bond" (GBP bond in UK) are all variants.

### F. Multilateral / Bilateral Loans
- Loans from international financial institutions such as:
  - **World Bank (IBRD/IDA)**
  - **International Monetary Fund (IMF)** — generally sovereign.
  - **Asian Development Bank (ADB)**
  - **New Development Bank (NDB)** — BRICS bank.
  - **Asian Infrastructure Investment Bank (AIIB)**
- Typically long-tenure, concessional, and project-specific.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 8 formulas — they cover ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cost of Equity (Dividend Model — DDM)** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = Expected dividend next year, $P_0$ = Current market price, $g$ = Growth rate |
| 2 | **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = Risk-free rate, $\beta$ = Beta of stock, $R_m$ = Market return |
| 3 | **Cost of Retained Earnings** | $K_r = K_e \times (1 - t_p)$ (approx) | $t_p$ = Personal tax rate of shareholders |
| 4 | **Cost of Preference Shares** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ (or $P_0 - F$ if flotation) | $D_p$ = Annual preference dividend, $P_0$ = Issue price |
| 5 | **After-tax Cost of Debt** | $K_d = \dfrac{I (1 - t)}{NP}$ | $I$ = Annual interest, $t$ = Tax rate, $NP$ = Net proceeds |
| 6 | **Cost of Trade Credit** | $K_{tc} = \dfrac{d}{1 - d} \times \dfrac{360}{C - D}$ | $d$ = Discount %, $C$ = Credit period, $D$ = Discount period |
| 7 | **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** | $WACC = w_e K_e + w_p K_p + w_d K_d$ | $w_e, w_p, w_d$ = Weights of equity, preference, debt |
| 8 | **Marginal Cost of Capital (MCC)** | $MCC = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Total Cost}}{\Delta \text{Total Capital}}$ | Change in total cost per unit change in capital raised |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Corporate Finance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does a B.Tech student need to know this?**
> - If you start your own **tech startup**, you will face these exact decisions: VC funding (equity) vs. bank loan (debt) vs. FDI from a US-based accelerator.
> - For a **civil/infra engineer** working with EPC companies, project finance relies heavily on **ECBs**, **infrastructure bonds**, and **multilateral loans** — you will review term sheets with these instruments.
> - For an **electrical/Mech engineer** in a manufacturing firm, working capital loans and trade credit finance the **inventory of components** — understanding CP and CC limits matters in BOM planning.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)

### Setup
Suppose a company has the following capital structure:

| Source | Amount (₹) | Specific Cost | Tax Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 60,00,000 | 15% | — |
| Preference Share Capital | 10,00,000 | 12% | — |
| Debentures (10%) | 30,00,000 | 10% (pre-tax) | 30% |

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Capital

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Capital} &= 60{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text logic:* Sum the book value (or market value, if specified) of all components of long-term capital to find the denominator base.

### Step 2 — Compute the After-Tax Cost of Debt

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_d \text{ (after tax)} &= K_d \text{ (pre-tax)} \times (1 - t) \\
&= 10\% \times (1 - 0.30) \\
&= 7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text logic:* The Indian Income Tax Act, 1961, allows interest paid on debt as a deductible expense. Therefore the effective cost to the company is the gross rate multiplied by $(1 - t)$.

### Step 3 — Determine Weights of Each Source

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_e &= \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.60 \\
w_p &= \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.10 \\
w_d &= \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.30 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text logic:* Weights are the relative proportions of each source in the total capital employed. Weights must sum to 1.0.

### Step 4 — Substitute into the WACC Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WACC} &= w_e K_e + w_p K_p + w_d K_d (1 - t) \\
&= (0.60 \times 15\%) + (0.10 \times 12\%) + (0.30 \times 7\%) \\
&= 9.0\% + 1.2\% + 2.1\% \\
&= 12.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text logic:* The WACC is the weighted sum of the specific after-tax cost of each capital component. It represents the average cost the firm pays for every rupee of finance.

### Step 5 — Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The company must earn **at least 12.3%** on every new project to avoid destroying shareholder value. If the project's IRR < WACC, the project should be rejected.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Cost of Trade Credit

### Setup
A supplier offers terms: **"2/10, Net 60"**.
- **2% discount** if paid within **10 days**.
- Otherwise, the **full amount** is due in **60 days**.

A firm makes purchases of **₹10,00,000 per year**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Variables

$$
d = 0.02, \quad C = 60 \text{ days}, \quad D = 10 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Cost of Trade Credit Formula

$$
K_{tc} = \dfrac{d}{1 - d} \times \dfrac{360}{C - D}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{tc} &= \frac{0.02}{1 - 0.02} \times \frac{360}{60 - 10} \\
&= \frac{0.02}{0.98} \times \frac{360}{50} \\
&= 0.020408 \times 7.2 \\
&= 0.14694 \\
&\approx 14.69\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> By *forgoing* a 2% discount and waiting 50 extra days, the firm is **effectively borrowing** at an annualised rate of **14.69%**. This is often more expensive than a bank loan — therefore, it is usually optimal to take the discount.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Cost of Equity (CAPM)

### Setup
- Risk-free rate $R_f = 7\%$ (10-year G-Sec yield)
- Expected market return $R_m = 14\%$
- Stock's beta $\beta = 1.20$

### Step 1 — Identify Inputs

$$
R_f = 0.07, \quad R_m = 0.14, \quad \beta = 1.20
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Market Risk Premium

$$
R_m - R_f = 0.14 - 0.07 = 0.07
$$

*Text logic:* The market risk premium is the additional return expected by the market for bearing average equity risk above the risk-free rate.

### Step 3 — Apply CAPM

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_e &= R_f + \beta \times (R_m - R_f) \\
&= 0.07 + 1.20 \times 0.07 \\
&= 0.07 + 0.084 \\
&= 0.154 \\
&= 15.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text logic:* Since the stock's beta is 1.20 (more volatile than the market), the cost of equity is 1.20 times the market premium, added to the risk-free rate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Framework for Choosing a Source

| Decision Criterion | Equity Shares | Preference Shares | Debentures | Bank Loan | Retained Earnings | FDI | ECB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost of Capital** | High | Medium | Lowest (after tax) | Low | Lowest | High | Low |
| **Voting/Control** | Dilutes | No dilution | No dilution | No dilution | No dilution | May dilute | No dilution |
| **Risk to Issuer** | Low (no fixed obligation) | Medium (fixed div) | High (fixed int) | High | Very Low | Low (long-term) | Medium |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Medium |
| **Tax Deductibility** | No | No (dividend) | Yes (interest) | Yes | No (already taxed) | No | Yes (interest) |
| **Floatation Cost** | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Zero | Very High | Medium |
| **Suitability for Short-term** | No | No | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Suitability for Long-term** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Currency Risk Exposure** | None | None | None | None | None | Yes (if INR depreciation) | **Yes (high)** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define retained earnings. Why is it considered the cheapest source of finance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Retained earnings refer to the portion of net profits that a company **ploughs back** into the business instead of distributing as dividends. It is considered the **cheapest source of finance** because:
1. **No flotation cost** — there is no issue expense, underwriting fee, or brokerage.
2. **No dividend obligation** — there is no contractual commitment to pay; it is discretionary.
3. **No dilution of control** — no new shares are issued, so ownership is preserved.
4. It also helps in **tax planning** because dividends attract DDT (now abolished but historically relevant) and personal tax in the hands of shareholders; retention defers this.

*(Award 1 mark each for definition + 2 valid reasons. Maximum 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between ADR and GDR.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | ADR | GDR |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | American Depository Receipt | Global Depository Receipt |
| **Market** | Traded only in **US** stock exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ) | Traded in **multiple international** markets (Europe, Asia) |
| **Currency** | US Dollars | US Dollars / Euros / Pounds |
| **Regulator** | Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), USA | Respective foreign regulators |
| **Accessibility** | Restricted to US investors | Accessible to global investors |

*(Award 1 mark for full form + 2 marks for the table comparison. Maximum 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern:**
> Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Most questions have two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, often combining a theoretical question with a numerical problem.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Typical Pattern]`

**(a) "The choice of source of finance is influenced by several factors." Discuss the various factors that influence the financing decision of a firm. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The financing decision of a firm is influenced by the following interrelated factors:

**1. Cost of Capital (2 marks)**
The firm should aim to minimise its cost of capital. While debt is cheaper due to the tax shield, excessive debt raises financial risk. The optimal mix balances cost and risk.

**2. Risk Consideration (1 mark)**
Higher debt means higher **financial risk** and **bankruptcy risk**. Firms with stable cash flows (utilities, FMCG) can take more debt; cyclical firms (textiles, real estate) should rely on equity.

**3. Control (1 mark)**
If promoters want to retain voting control, they prefer preference shares, debentures, or rights issues (to existing shareholders) over fresh equity issues to the public.

**4. Flexibility (1 mark)**
The capital structure should be flexible enough to absorb future changes. Too many restrictive covenants in debt agreements reduce flexibility.

**5. Floatation Costs and Issue Size (1 mark)**
For smaller requirements, equity or bank loans are economical; for massive requirements (e.g., a new power plant), ECBs or ADRs/GDRs are more viable despite higher floatation cost.

**6. Tax Position (1 mark)**
Profitable firms with high tax outflows benefit more from debt (interest is tax-deductible). Loss-making firms derive no tax benefit and should prefer equity.

*(Award 1 mark per well-explained factor. For full 7 marks, cover at least 5-6 factors with examples.)*

---

**(b) A company has the following capital structure: Equity share capital ₹8,00,000; 10% Preference share capital ₹2,00,000; 12% Debentures ₹5,00,000. The current market price of equity shares is ₹40, expected dividend next year ₹4, and growth rate 6%. The debentures are redeemable at par in 5 years. Tax rate is 30%. Find the WACC. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Capital**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Equity (Dividend Growth Model)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_e &= \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g \\
&= \frac{4}{40} + 0.06 \\
&= 0.10 + 0.06 \\
&= 0.16 = 16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Preference Shares**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_p &= \frac{D_p}{P_0} = \frac{10}{100} = 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — After-tax Cost of Debt**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_d (1 - t) &= 12\% \times (1 - 0.30) \\
&= 12\% \times 0.70 \\
&= 8.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Weights**
$$
\begin{aligned}
w_e &= \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 0.5333 \\
w_p &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 0.1333 \\
w_d &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 0.3333 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — WACC**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WACC} &= (0.5333 \times 16\%) + (0.1333 \times 10\%) + (0.3333 \times 8.4\%) \\
&= 8.533\% + 1.333\% + 2.800\% \\
&\approx 12.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The WACC of the firm is approximately **12.67%**. The firm should accept only those projects with an IRR > 12.67%.

*(Award: Step 1 = 1 mark; Steps 2-4 = 1 mark each; Step 5 = 1 mark; Step 6 = 1 mark; Conclusion = 1 mark. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Typical Pattern]`

**(a) What is meant by Foreign Direct Investment? Explain the various routes through which FDI is permitted in India. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) is the investment made by a foreign entity (individual, company, or government) in the productive capacity of a host country, with the intention of establishing a lasting interest and **management control** (typically defined as acquiring ≥10% of equity capital in a listed company, per RBI guidelines).

**Routes of FDI in India (5 marks):**

**1. Automatic Route (3 marks)**
- No prior approval from the Government of India or RBI is required.
- The investor simply notifies the RBI through the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) framework.
- Covers most sectors, including most manufacturing, services, and single-brand retail.
- Subject only to sectoral caps (e.g., 74% in private sector banks, 100% in manufacturing under the automatic route after 2020 reforms).

**2. Government Route (2 marks)**
- Requires prior approval from the relevant ministry/department under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**.
- Applies to restricted sectors such as:
  - Multi-Brand Retail Trading (with conditions)
  - Defence (above 49% threshold)
  - Telecom (earlier; now mostly automatic)
  - Pharmaceuticals (brownfield investments)
  - Broadcasting (in certain sub-sectors)
- The Foreign Investment Facilitation Portal (FIFP) is the single-window clearance mechanism.

**Additional Note (1 mark):** FDI inflows are also reported in the **FEMA-compliant** form, and the receiving company must file **Form FC-GPR** with the RBI within 30 days of issue of shares.

---

**(b) ABC Ltd. is evaluating two financing options for ₹50 Lakhs. Compute the effective cost of each option and advise which is cheaper. Tax rate = 30%.**

**Option 1:** Issue 10,000 equity shares at ₹500 each. Expected dividend = ₹8 per share, growth = 5%. Floatation cost = ₹50 per share.

**Option 2:** Issue 1,000 12% debentures of ₹500 each. Redeemable at par in 10 years. Floatation cost = ₹25 per debenture.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cost of Equity (Option 1) using DDM with floatation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_e &= \frac{D_1}{P_0 - F} + g \\
&= \frac{8}{500 - 50} + 0.05 \\
&= \frac{8}{450} + 0.05 \\
&= 0.01778 + 0.05 \\
&= 0.06778 = 6.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — After-tax Cost of Debenture (Option 2)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_d (1 - t) &= \frac{I (1 - t)}{NP} \\
&= \frac{12\% \times 500 (1 - 0.30)}{500 - 25} \\
&= \frac{60 \times 0.70}{475} \\
&= \frac{42}{475} \\
&= 0.0884 = 8.84\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare**
- Cost of Equity = 6.78%
- Cost of Debt (after tax) = 8.84%

**Step 4 — Advice**
Although **debt is typically cheaper**, here the cost of equity (6.78%) is **lower** than the after-tax cost of debt (8.84%). This is because of the high floatation cost on the equity issue and the dividend tax shield at the shareholder level (no DDT in India from 2020). 

**Recommendation:** ABC Ltd. should opt for **Option 1 — Equity** for this ₹50 Lakh requirement, as it is cheaper by **2.06 percentage points**.

> [!NOTE]
> *However, the company must also factor in the **loss of control** and **no tax shield** in equity. If promoters can take more risk, the leverage benefit of debt may still be preferred. This is a judgement call.*

*(Award: Step 1 = 2 marks; Step 2 = 2 marks; Step 3 = 1 mark; Step 4 = 2 marks. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Shield on Debt** — Students compute $K_d = 12\%$ instead of $K_d (1 - t) = 8.4\%$. This is the most common error; **lose 1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Ignoring Floatation Cost** — Always check whether the problem states a floatation cost. If yes, use $P_0 - F$ in the denominator. If forgotten, **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Using Book Value weights when Market Values are given** — When market prices are given, use market value weights. Mixing book and market values is a serious error.
> 4. **Misidentifying the formula for Cost of Trade Credit** — Use 360 or 365 days as per the problem. Sometimes KTU uses "Net 30" or "Net 60" — read carefully.
> 5. **Confusing FDI with FPI** — FDI involves **control**, FPI does not. A 1-mark concept question can cost you 2 marks if confused.
> 6. **Forgetting the growth term $g$ in DDM** — A classic error: using $K_e = D_1 / P_0$ without $+ g$. **Lose 1-2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "The 12 Essentials"**

1. **Sources of finance = Domestic + International.** Domestic raises in INR; International raises in foreign currency.
2. **Owned capital = Equity + Preference + Retained Earnings.** Debt is **cheaper after tax** because of the interest tax shield.
3. **Retained earnings is the cheapest source** — no floatation cost, no dividend obligation. Cost ≈ $K_e$ adjusted for personal tax.
4. **Cost of Equity (DDM) = $D_1 / P_0 + g$** — Gordon's Growth Model. Always remember the $+g$ term.
5. **Cost of Debt (after tax) = $I (1 - t) / NP$** — Interest is tax-deductible; this is the most-tested formula.
6. **WACC = $\sum w_i K_i$** — Weighted by the proportion of each source. Compare with project IRR for acceptance/rejection.
7. **Cost of Trade Credit = $\dfrac{d}{1 - d} \times \dfrac{360}{C - D}$** — The "forgone discount" formula. Use when given terms like "2/10, Net 30".
8. **FDI = control + long-term commitment**; FPI = passive financial investment. This distinction is a **guaranteed exam question**.
9. **ECBs require RBI compliance**, minimum 3-year average maturity, and end-use restrictions (no real estate, no stock markets).
10. **ADRs are US-only; GDRs are global; FCCBs are convertible bonds** — these three are the most-tested international instruments.
11. **Factors affecting financing decision:** Cost, Risk, Control, Flexibility, Tax Position, Floatation Cost, Market Conditions, Regulatory Framework.
12. **Planning = matching source with purpose:** Short-term needs → trade credit, CP, CC. Long-term needs → equity, debentures, ECB, FDI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Mantra for KTU ESE Module 3:**
> *"The right source of finance is not the cheapest — it is the one that optimally balances cost, risk, control, and flexibility for the firm's specific situation and timing."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Capital: Planning, Decisions and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Capital: Planning, Decisions and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Working Capital

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **Working Capital (WC)** refers to the **capital required for day-to-day operations of a business firm**, representing the funds invested in *short-term assets* (current assets) and the corresponding *short-term obligations* (current liabilities).

A working KTU-level definition, drawn directly from the **Institute of Cost and Management Accountants (ICMA), London**, is:

> *"Working Capital is the excess of current assets over current liabilities."*

This is the **Net Working Capital (NWC)** definition. However, working capital has **two complementary interpretations** that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Gross Working Capital (GWC):** The total investment in current assets (Cash, Marketable Securities, Accounts Receivable, Inventory, Prepaid Expenses).
2. **Net Working Capital (NWC):** Current Assets − Current Liabilities. It represents the **long-term funds** (permanent capital) used to finance current assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Most 14-mark questions in the ESE test the **Net Working Capital concept** along with the **Operating Cycle computation**. Always clearly state which interpretation (Gross or Net) you are using in your answer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Lifeblood of the Business

Think of a business as a **living human body**:

- **Heart** → pumps blood continuously to keep organs alive.
- **Working Capital** → circulates liquidity continuously through the firm's **Operating Cycle** (Cash → Raw Material → WIP → Finished Goods → Debtors → Cash).

Just as a person cannot survive if blood flow stops, a firm cannot survive if its working capital cycle gets **choked**. Too little working capital leads to **liquidity crisis** (the firm cannot pay salaries, suppliers, or operate). Too much working capital leads to **idle funds** (poor profitability, since excess cash earns very little).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook:** Working capital is **NOT free money**. Every rupee locked up in inventory or receivables is a rupee that could have earned a return elsewhere. The finance manager's job is to **balance Liquidity (safety) ↔ Profitability (return)**.

## 1.3 The Two Components of Working Capital — KTU Terminology

| Type | Definition | Nature |
|------|------------|--------|
| **Permanent (Fixed) Working Capital** | The minimum level of current assets maintained at all times to ensure uninterrupted operations. | Stays in the business permanently; financed by long-term sources. |
| **Temporary (Variable) Working Capital** | The additional working capital required to meet seasonal or cyclical demand fluctuations. | Fluctuates with business cycles; financed by short-term sources. |

**Standard Metric:** The relationship is often expressed as:

$$\text{Total Working Capital} = \text{Permanent Working Capital} + \text{Temporary Working Capital}$$

## 1.4 Operating Cycle — The Heart of Module 4

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the time period between the **acquisition of raw materials** and the **realization of cash from sales**. It is the single most important concept in Module 4.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Operating Cycle approach is the most modern and accepted method for estimating working capital needs. Always compute it **step-by-step** in your ESE answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of the Operating Cycle

The Operating Cycle is the **sum of four time periods**, minus the credit period received from suppliers:

$$\text{Operating Cycle (OC)} = R + W + F + D - C$$

Where each variable represents:

1. **R = Raw Material Storage Period** → Time raw materials sit in stores before being issued to production.
2. **W = Work-in-Progress Period** → Time semi-finished goods remain in production.
3. **F = Finished Goods Storage Period** → Time finished goods wait before being sold.
4. **D = Debtors (Receivables) Collection Period** → Time taken to collect cash from credit customers.
5. **C = Creditors (Payables) Payment Period** → Credit period granted by suppliers (this **reduces** the cycle).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — KTU Examiner Logic

A finance manager asking "How much working capital do I need?" is essentially asking:

- *"How many days of raw materials do I hold?"* → Locked cash in RM inventory.
- *"How many days does production take?"* → Locked cash in WIP.
- *"How many days do finished goods wait for sale?"* → Locked cash in FG.
- *"How many days until customers pay?"* → Locked cash in Debtors.
- *"How many days can I delay paying my suppliers?"* → **Frees** locked cash.

The shorter the OC, the **less capital is tied up**, and the more **profitable** the firm becomes (assuming constant sales).

## 2.3 Working Capital Management — The Three Core Decisions

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies **three decisions** as the pillars of working capital management:

### Decision 1: Financing Decision (Capital Mix Decision)
Deciding **how** to finance current assets — through long-term sources, short-term sources, or a mix.

### Decision 2: Investment Decision (Asset Mix Decision)
Deciding **how much to invest** in each current asset — Cash, Receivables, Inventory.

### Decision 3: Profitability vs Liquidity Trade-off
Balancing the **risk of insolvency** (low liquidity) against the **risk of low profitability** (excess liquidity).

## 2.4 Three Approaches to Financing Working Capital (Very High Yield for 14-mark Questions)

| Approach | Permanent WC | Temporary WC | Risk Profile | Cost |
|----------|--------------|--------------|--------------|------|
| **Hedging / Matching Approach** | Long-term funds | Short-term funds | Lowest Risk, Lowest Return | Moderate |
| **Conservative Approach** | Long-term funds (entire WC) | Long-term funds (entire WC) | Low Risk, Low Return | Highest Cost |
| **Aggressive Approach** | Short-term funds (entire WC) | Short-term funds (entire WC) | High Risk, High Return | Lowest Cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the "Aggressive Approach," even the **permanent** working capital is financed by short-term sources. This is **risky** but cheap. The firm must ensure it can roll over short-term loans.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **Gross Working Capital** | $\text{GWC} = \text{Current Assets}$ | Total investment in current assets. |
| **Net Working Capital** | $\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL}$ | Excess of CA over CL; permanent capital locked in current assets. |
| **Current Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{CA}}{\text{CL}}$ | Ideal standard: **2 : 1** |
| **Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio** | $\text{QR} = \dfrac{\text{CA} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{CL}}$ | Ideal standard: **1 : 1** |
| **Absolute Liquid Ratio** | $\text{ALR} = \dfrac{\text{Cash} + \text{Marketable Securities}}{\text{CL}}$ | Ideal standard: **1 : 2** (i.e., 0.5) |
| **Operating Cycle (days)** | $\text{OC} = R + W + F + D - C$ | Days from raw material purchase to cash collection. |
| **Raw Material Inventory** | $\text{RMI} = \dfrac{\text{RM Cost} \times R}{360}$ | RM Cost × (holding days ÷ 360) |
| **WIP Inventory (50% completion)** | $\text{WIP} = \dfrac{\text{Total Production Cost} \times W}{360} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Assumes uniform completion stage. |
| **Finished Goods Inventory** | $\text{FGI} = \dfrac{\text{COGS} \times F}{360}$ | Cost of Goods Sold × (holding days ÷ 360) |
| **Debtors (Receivables)** | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Sales} \times D}{360}$ | Sales × (collection days ÷ 360) |
| **Creditors (Payables)** | $\text{CP} = \dfrac{\text{RM Cost} \times C}{360}$ | RM Cost × (payment days ÷ 360) |
| **Working Capital Required** | $\text{WC} = \text{RMI} + \text{WIP} + \text{FGI} + \text{DR} - \text{CP}$ | Sum of all components. |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

In engineering and corporate finance, this framework is the **backbone of Treasury Management**. Every manufacturing company (e.g., an electronics firm, an automobile manufacturer, an FMCG company) uses operating cycle analysis to:
- Plan **production schedules**.
- Negotiate **supplier credit terms**.
- Set **customer credit policies**.
- Decide on **bank overdraft limits** and **cash credit facilities**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Computing Working Capital from an Operating Cycle

We will now solve a **canonical KTU-style problem** with exhaustive algebraic transitions. Every numerical step is shown explicitly. **No shortcuts.**

### Problem Statement

> From the following data relating to a manufacturing company, compute the **Operating Cycle** and the **amount of Working Capital required**:
>
> - Annual Sales (all on credit): ₹ 18,00,000
> - Annual Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): ₹ 14,40,000
> - Raw Material Cost (60% of COGS): ₹ 8,64,000
> - Raw Material holding period (R): 2 months
> - Work-in-Progress holding period (W): 1 month (assume 50% stage of completion)
> - Finished Goods holding period (F): 2 months
> - Credit period allowed to customers (D): 3 months
> - Credit period obtained from suppliers (C): 1 month
> - Time period of the year: 12 months (assume 360 days for banking convention)

---

### Step 1 — Compute Raw Material Inventory (RMI)

We are told that the **Raw Material Cost** is 60% of COGS:

$$\text{RM Cost} = 60\% \times 14{,}40{,}000 = \dfrac{60}{100} \times 14{,}40{,}000$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$= 0.60 \times 14{,}40{,}000 = 8{,}64{,}000$$

Now, applying the Raw Material Inventory formula:

$$\text{RMI} = \text{RM Cost} \times \dfrac{R}{12}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{RMI} = 8{,}64{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12}$$

Reducing the fraction:

$$\text{RMI} = 8{,}64{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{6}$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{RMI} = 1{,}44{,}000$$

**Result:** Raw Material Inventory = **₹ 1,44,000**.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Work-in-Progress Inventory (WIP)

When the problem does not split raw material and conversion costs separately, we apply the **uniform 50% completion assumption**. This means the WIP holds, on average, **half** of the production cost.

Using the WIP formula:

$$\text{WIP} = \text{Production Cost} \times \dfrac{W}{12} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{WIP} = 14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Multiplying the two fractions first:

$$\dfrac{1}{12} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{24}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{WIP} = 14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{24}$$

Performing the division:

$$14{,}40{,}000 \div 24 = 60{,}000$$

**Result:** Work-in-Progress Inventory = **₹ 60,000**.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Finished Goods Inventory (FGI)

The formula for FGI uses the **Cost of Goods Sold**:

$$\text{FGI} = \text{COGS} \times \dfrac{F}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{FGI} = 14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12}$$

Simplifying:

$$\text{FGI} = 14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{6}$$

Performing the division:

$$14{,}40{,}000 \div 6 = 2{,}40{,}000$$

**Result:** Finished Goods Inventory = **₹ 2,40,000**.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Debtors (Accounts Receivable)

Debtors are valued at **Sales price** (not cost), since the firm must finance the full selling price until cash is collected:

$$\text{Debtors} = \text{Sales} \times \dfrac{D}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Debtors} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{12}$$

Simplifying:

$$\text{Debtors} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$$

Performing the division:

$$18{,}00{,}000 \div 4 = 4{,}50{,}000$$

**Result:** Debtors = **₹ 4,50,000**.

---

### Step 5 — Compute Creditors (Accounts Payable)

Creditors are valued at the **Raw Material Cost** (since only material is purchased from suppliers):

$$\text{Creditors} = \text{RM Cost} \times \dfrac{C}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$\text{Creditors} = 8{,}64{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12}$$

Performing the division:

$$8{,}64{,}000 \div 12 = 72{,}000$$

**Result:** Creditors = **₹ 72,000**.

---

### Step 6 — Compute Total Working Capital

We now assemble the Operating Cycle calculation:

$$\text{WC} = \text{RMI} + \text{WIP} + \text{FGI} + \text{Debtors} - \text{Creditors}$$

Substituting all computed values:

$$\text{WC} = 1{,}44{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 - 72{,}000$$

Adding the positive components step-by-step:

$$1{,}44{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 2{,}04{,}000$$

$$2{,}04{,}000 + 2{,}40{,}000 = 4{,}44{,}000$$

$$4{,}44{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}94{,}000$$

Now subtracting creditors:

$$8{,}94{,}000 - 72{,}000 = 8{,}22{,}000$$

**Result:** **Working Capital Required = ₹ 8,22,000.**

---

### Step 7 — Compute the Operating Cycle (in months)

$$\text{OC} = R + W + F + D - C$$

$$\text{OC} = 2 + 1 + 2 + 3 - 1$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$\text{OC} = 5 \text{ months}$$

The firm's cash is locked up for **5 months** before the operating cycle completes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Summary of the Above Problem

| Component | Formula Used | Computation | Value (₹) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| Raw Material Inventory | $\text{RM Cost} \times \dfrac{R}{12}$ | $8{,}64{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12}$ | 1,44,000 |
| Work-in-Progress | $\text{COGS} \times \dfrac{W}{12} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$ | $14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{24}$ | 60,000 |
| Finished Goods | $\text{COGS} \times \dfrac{F}{12}$ | $14{,}40{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12}$ | 2,40,000 |
| Debtors | $\text{Sales} \times \dfrac{D}{12}$ | $18{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{12}$ | 4,50,000 |
| Less: Creditors | $\text{RM Cost} \times \dfrac{C}{12}$ | $8{,}64{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12}$ | (72,000) |
| **Total Working Capital** | Sum of above | — | **8,22,000** |
| **Operating Cycle** | $R+W+F+D-C$ | $2+1+2+3-1$ | **5 months** |

---

## 3.3 Logical Notes on Conversion Logic

- **Why 50% for WIP?** Because on average, every WIP unit is half-completed — some are 10% done, some are 90%, averaging 50%. Without further data, 50% is the KTU standard.
- **Why Debtors at Sales price?** Because the firm finances the *full selling price* from the moment of sale until collection. Profit margin is also locked up.
- **Why Creditors at RM Cost?** Because only raw materials are bought on credit. Wages and overheads are usually paid in cash.
- **Why subtract Creditors?** Because supplier credit is a *free source* of working capital finance — it reduces the firm's own funds tied up.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Operating Cycle Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

The Operating Cycle is best understood as a **circular flow** of cash through five stages. The diagram below maps the journey of capital from cash → raw materials → cash.

```mermaid
graph LR
stageA[STAGE 1: RAW MATERIAL PURCHASE] --> stageB[STAGE 2: RAW MATERIAL STORAGE R DAYS]
stageB --> stageC[STAGE 3: WORK IN PROGRESS W DAYS]
stageC --> stageD[STAGE 4: FINISHED GOODS STORAGE F DAYS]
stageD --> stageE[STAGE 5: CREDIT SALES TO CUSTOMERS]
stageE --> stageF[STAGE 6: DEBTORS COLLECTION D DAYS]
stageF --> stageG[STAGE 7: CASH REALIZATION]
stageG --> stageH[STAGE 8: PAYMENT TO SUPPLIERS C DAYS]
stageH -.-> stageA
stageH -.-> stageI[NET WORKING CAPITAL IS LOCKED FOR OC MINUS C DAYS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** trace the operating flow. The **dashed arrows** indicate that supplier credit (C) and net cash lock-up **return** to the start of the cycle, closing the loop.

---

## 4.2 Working Capital Financing Strategy Diagram (Mermaid)

The three classical approaches to financing current assets can be visualized as **risk-return trade-offs** in a block-level architecture.

```mermaid
graph TB
nodeRoot[WORKING CAPITAL FINANCING STRATEGIES]
nodeRoot --> nodeMatch[HEDGING OR MATCHING APPROACH]
nodeRoot --> nodeCons[CONSERVATIVE APPROACH]
nodeRoot --> nodeAgg[AGGRESSIVE APPROACH]
nodeMatch --> nodeMatchDesc[LONG TERM FUNDS FINANCE PERMANENT WC AND SHORT TERM FUNDS FINANCE TEMPORARY WC]
nodeCons --> nodeConsDesc[ENTIRE WC INCLUDING TEMPORARY IS FINANCED BY LONG TERM SOURCES]
nodeAgg --> nodeAggDesc[ENTIRE WC INCLUDING PERMANENT IS FINANCED BY SHORT TERM SOURCES]
nodeMatchDesc --> nodeMatchRisk[RISK MODERATE RETURN MODERATE]
nodeConsDesc --> nodeConsRisk[RISK LOW RETURN LOW]
nodeAggDesc --> nodeAggRisk[RISK HIGH RETURN HIGH]
```

---

## 4.3 Components of Working Capital Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
nodeWC[Gross Working Capital equals Total Current Assets]
nodeWC --> nodeCA1[CASH AND BANK BALANCES]
nodeWC --> nodeCA2[MARKETABLE SECURITIES]
nodeWC --> nodeCA3[ACCOUNTS RECEIVABLES DEBTORS]
nodeWC --> nodeCA4[INVENTORY RAW MATERIAL WIP FINISHED GOODS]
nodeWC --> nodeCA5[PREPAID EXPENSES]
nodeCA4 --> nodeInv1[RAW MATERIAL]
nodeCA4 --> nodeInv2[WORK IN PROGRESS]
nodeCA4 --> nodeInv3[FINISHED GOODS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define Working Capital. Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital.**

**Model Answer:**

> Working Capital refers to the capital required to finance the day-to-day operations of a business. It represents funds locked up in short-term assets.
>
> **Gross Working Capital (GWC):** The total investment in current assets. It is the *aggregate* of cash, receivables, inventory, and prepaid expenses. It is a *quantitative* concept.
>
> **Net Working Capital (NWC):** The excess of current assets over current liabilities. It represents the *qualitative* portion — the long-term funds used to finance current operations. Formula: NWC = CA − CL.
>
> While GWC focuses on the *amount* of current assets, NWC focuses on the *quality* and *solvency* of the firm.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the concept of Permanent and Temporary Working Capital with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Permanent Working Capital (PWC):** The minimum level of current assets that a firm must maintain at all times to ensure uninterrupted operations. It is **permanent** because it stays in the business throughout the year.
>
> **Temporary Working Capital (TWC):** The additional working capital required over and above the permanent level to meet **seasonal** or **cyclical** demand fluctuations. It is **variable** in nature.
>
> **Diagram:**
>
> On the X-axis: Time (months). On the Y-axis: Working Capital (₹).
>
> - A horizontal line represents **PWC** (constant).
> - A wavy curve fluctuating above PWC represents **TWC**.
> - The total = PWC + TWC.
>
> The firm always needs at least PWC; TWC rises during peak season and falls during lean season.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Working Capital numerical problems, students **lose 3-4 marks** by:
> 1. Computing **Debtors at cost price** instead of **sales price** (this is the most common mistake).
> 2. Forgetting to **deduct Creditors** from the total.
> 3. Computing **WIP without the 50% completion factor** when not given separately.
> 4. Mixing up **gross and net** working capital concepts in the theory section.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Numerical + Theory

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Numerical

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

From the following data, prepare an **Estimate of Working Capital** for the company:

| Item | Amount / Period |
|------|----------------|
| Estimated Annual Sales | ₹ 24,00,000 |
| Estimated Gross Profit | 25% on Sales |
| Raw Material in stock | 3 months |
| Work-in-Progress | 1 month (Raw Material 100% complete, Conversion 50% complete) |
| Finished Goods in stock | 2 months |
| Credit allowed by suppliers | 2 months |
| Credit allowed to customers | 3 months |
| Wages and Salaries payable | 1 month |
| Manufacturing Expenses outstanding | 0.5 month |
| Selling and Admin Expenses | ₹ 60,000 per month (paid in advance for 1 month) |

**Assumption:** Production is carried on uniformly throughout the year. Time period: 12 months.

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)**

Sales = ₹ 24,00,000
Gross Profit = 25% of Sales = ₹ 6,00,000
Therefore:

$$\text{COGS} = \text{Sales} - \text{Gross Profit} = 24{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Determine Raw Material Cost**

Assume Raw Material Cost = 60% of COGS (a common KTU assumption when not given):

$$\text{RM Cost} = 60\% \times 18{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}80{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute Raw Material Inventory**

$$\text{RMI} = 10{,}80{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{12} = 2{,}70{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Compute WIP (Raw Material 100% + Conversion 50%)**

Conversion Cost = 40% of COGS = ₹ 7,20,000

$$\text{WIP} = \underbrace{10{,}80{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12}}_{\text{RM in WIP}} + \underbrace{7{,}20{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12} \times 0.5}_{\text{Conversion in WIP}}$$

$$= 90{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Finished Goods Inventory**

$$\text{FGI} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12} = 3{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Debtors**

$$\text{Debtors} = 24{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{12} = 6{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 7 — Prepaid Expenses (Selling and Admin)**

$$\text{Prepaid} = 60{,}000 \times 1 = 60{,}000$$

**Step 8 — Total Current Assets**

$$\text{Total CA} = 2{,}70{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 13{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 9 — Compute Current Liabilities (Deductible)**

- Creditors (on RM): $\dfrac{10{,}80{,}000 \times 2}{12} = 1{,}80{,}000$
- Wages outstanding: Assume 25% of COGS = ₹ 4,50,000; Wages 1 month = $4{,}50{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12} = 37{,}500$
- Manufacturing Expenses outstanding (0.5 month) = $\dfrac{1}{2} \text{ month of conversion cost} = 7{,}20{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.5}{12} = 30{,}000$

$$\text{Total CL} = 1{,}80{,}000 + 37{,}500 + 30{,}000 = 2{,}47{,}500$$

**Step 10 — Net Working Capital**

$$\text{NWC} = 13{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}47{,}500 = 11{,}02{,}500$$

**Answer:** **Net Working Capital Required ≈ ₹ 11,02,500.**

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Theory

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Explain the three approaches to financing working capital with diagrams.**

**Model Answer (Synopsis):**

**(i) Hedging / Matching Approach:** Permanent WC financed by long-term sources; Temporary WC financed by short-term sources. *Risk: Moderate. Cost: Moderate.* It is the **ideal** approach.

**(ii) Conservative Approach:** Both permanent and temporary WC are financed by long-term sources. *Risk: Lowest. Return: Lowest. Cost: Highest.* The firm carries excess liquidity.

**(iii) Aggressive Approach:** Both permanent and temporary WC are financed by short-term sources. *Risk: Highest. Return: Highest. Cost: Lowest.* The firm risks insolvency if credit markets tighten.

(Diagrams of the three approaches, with axes Time vs Working Capital, and shaded regions showing long-term vs short-term financing, must accompany the answer for full 7 marks.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Set

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Theory

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Discuss the various factors affecting the working capital requirements of a firm.**

**Model Answer — Key Points:**

1. **Nature of Business:** Manufacturing firms need more WC than trading/service firms.
2. **Size of Firm:** Larger firms typically need larger absolute WC.
3. **Production Cycle:** Longer production cycles → higher WC.
4. **Sales Volume & Growth:** Higher sales → more WC tied up.
5. **Credit Policy:** Liberal credit → more debtors → more WC.
6. **Seasonality:** Seasonal industries face fluctuating WC needs.
7. **Procurement Policy:** Bulk buying locks more RM inventory.
8. **Operating Efficiency:** Better turnover ratios reduce WC needs.
9. **Price Level Changes:** Inflation increases WC needs.
10. **Availability of Credit:** Better supplier credit → lower WC.
11. **Dividend Policy:** High dividends reduce retained earnings, increasing external WC need.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**A company is considering a working capital proposal. From the following data, compute the working capital required:**

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Annual Sales | ₹ 20,00,000 |
| Cost of Production | ₹ 16,00,000 |
| Raw Material Cost (75% of production cost) | ₹ 12,00,000 |
| RM holding period | 2 months |
| WIP holding period | 1 month (assume 50% completion) |
| FG holding period | 2 months |
| Average collection period | 3 months |
| Average payment period | 1.5 months |

**Model Solution:**

- RMI: $12{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12} = 2{,}00{,}000$
- WIP: $16{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{1}{12} \times 0.5 = 66{,}667$
- FGI: $16{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{2}{12} = 2{,}66{,}667$
- Debtors: $20{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{3}{12} = 5{,}00{,}000$
- Creditors: $12{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{1.5}{12} = 1{,}50{,}000$

$$\text{NWC} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 66{,}667 + 2{,}66{,}667 + 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000$$

$$= 8{,}83{,}334$$

**Answer:** **Working Capital Required ≈ ₹ 8,83,334.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

- [x] **Working Capital** = funds for day-to-day operations. GWC = Current Assets; NWC = CA − CL.
- [x] **Permanent WC** = minimum level (always needed). **Temporary WC** = seasonal/cyclical addition.
- [x] **Operating Cycle** = R + W + F + D − C. Shorter cycle = better liquidity.
- [x] **WIP Formula:** When split given, use RM at 100% + Conversion at 50%. Otherwise, use the **50% uniform completion** rule.
- [x] **Debtors are valued at SALES price**, not cost. (Most common error.)
- [x] **Creditors are valued at RM Cost**, not total production cost.
- [x] **Three Financing Approaches:**
  - Hedging = Moderate risk.
  - Conservative = Low risk, high cost.
  - Aggressive = High risk, low cost.
- [x] **Current Ratio** ideal = 2:1. **Quick Ratio** ideal = 1:1.
- [x] **Profitability vs Liquidity:** Inverse trade-off — more liquidity = less profitability.
- [x] **Factors affecting WC:** Nature, size, cycle length, credit policy, seasonality, inflation, efficiency.
- [x] **Cash Conversion Cycle** = Operating Cycle − Payment Period = (R + W + F + D) − C.
- [x] **Always show your working** in numerical problems. Marks are split across each component.
- [x] **Use the convention 360 days for banking / 365 days for general** — check the problem statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical Models of Cash Management, Inventories (Including Risk Analysis), Trade Receivables, Trade Payables and Short-Term Financing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Models of Cash Management, Inventories (Including Risk Analysis), Trade Receivables, Trade Payables and Short-Term Financing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Working Capital Management — The Umbrella Concept

**Working Capital Management (WCM)** refers to the administration of a firm's short-term assets and short-term liabilities to ensure sufficient liquidity, operational efficiency, and profitability. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this module dissects the four operational pillars of WCM: **Cash**, **Inventory**, **Receivables**, and **Payables**, along with the short-term financing instruments that bridge temporary cash deficits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEIET743 / Module 4):**
> *Working capital management involves planning, organizing, directing, and controlling the level and composition of current assets and current liabilities such that the firm is able to meet its short-term obligations while maximizing shareholder value.*

**Standard Metrics Used Throughout the Module (in bold):**
- **Current Ratio (CR) = Current Assets / Current Liabilities** (Ideal benchmark: **2:1**)
- **Quick Ratio (QR) = (Current Assets − Inventory) / Current Liabilities** (Ideal benchmark: **1:1**)
- **Net Working Capital (NWC) = Current Assets − Current Liabilities**
- **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** — measured in **days**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

Imagine your business as a household that requires a constant flow of water to run daily chores (paying bills, salaries, buying groceries). 

- **Cash** is the water tank reservoir — it must always have *some* buffer, but holding too much is wasteful.
- **Inventory** is the stock of raw materials and finished goods in your storeroom.
- **Trade Receivables** is the money your friends (customers) owe you for goods taken on credit.
- **Trade Payables** is the money *you* owe your suppliers for goods you took on credit.
- **Short-Term Financing** is the emergency credit line you tap when the tank runs dry before the next inflow.

The art of WCM is to keep the tank optimally filled — not overflowing (idle cash) and not empty (default risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Quadrant — Mnemonic: "C-I-R-P"**
> **C**ash, **I**nventories, **R**eceivables, **P**ayables — these are the four working capital components a KTU examiner will test.

---

### 1.3 Theoretical Models of Cash Management

**Cash Management** is the process of collecting, managing, and disbursing cash to optimize liquidity and maximize the availability of funds for productive use. The major theoretical models are:

**(a) William J. Baumol's Inventory Model (1952)** — Adapted from EOQ, it treats cash as an inventory that is replenished by selling marketable securities.

**(b) Miller-Orr Model (1966)** — A stochastic model that defines upper and lower control limits for cash balance, designed for firms with unpredictable cash flows.

**(c) Stochastic Model of Cash Management** — A generalized approach incorporating probability distributions of cash inflows and outflows.

**(d) Cash Budgeting Model** — A deterministic, time-series planning tool forecasting cash receipts and disbursements over monthly/quarterly horizons.

---

### 1.4 Inventories and Risk Analysis

**Inventory** represents the stock of raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, and MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating) supplies held by a firm. **Risk Analysis** in inventory deals with:

- **Stockout Risk** — The risk of running out of inventory.
- **Obsolescence Risk** — Inventory becoming unsellable due to technological change or expiry.
- **Price Risk** — Volatility in input material costs.
- **Carrying Cost Risk** — High holding cost eroding profitability.

---

### 1.5 Trade Receivables and Trade Payables

**Trade Receivables (Sundry Debtors)** = amounts owed to a firm by customers for goods/services sold on credit.

**Trade Payables (Sundry Creditors)** = amounts owed by a firm to suppliers for goods/services purchased on credit.

---

### 1.6 Short-Term Financing

**Short-Term Financing** refers to funds raised for periods typically less than **one year**, used to finance working capital needs. Major sources include: bank overdraft, cash credit, commercial paper, treasury bills, factoring, and trade credit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Baumol Model (Economic Order Quantity for Cash)

Baumol assumed a firm begins with an optimal cash balance **C\***, which is depleted at a constant rate **b** (transactions demand for cash) over a planning period. Whenever the balance hits zero, the firm sells marketable securities worth **C\*** to refill the cash account. The total cost of cash holding has two components:

- **Opportunity Cost (Holding Cost)** = (Average Cash Balance) × (Cost of Capital)
- **Transaction Cost (Ordering Cost)** = (Number of Orders per Period) × (Fixed Cost per Conversion)

**Optimal Cash Balance formula:**

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 b T}{i}}$$

Where:
- **b** = total cash needed for transactions over the period
- **T** = transaction cost per conversion (brokerage, fees)
- **i** = opportunity cost of holding cash (annualized interest rate)

**Average Cash Balance = C\* / 2**

**Optimal Number of Orders:**

$$N^{*} = \dfrac{b}{C^{*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{b i}{2 T}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions of the Baumol Model (High-Frequency Exam Topic):**
> 1. Cash usage is **constant and predictable** per period.
> 2. Marketable securities can be sold instantly at no loss.
> 3. Transaction cost (T) is **fixed** per transaction.
> 4. Holding cost (i) is **constant** per period.
> 5. No safety stock of cash is maintained.

---

### 2.2 The Miller-Orr Model

Developed by Merton H. Miller and Daniel Orr (1966), this model handles **random and fluctuating cash flows**. It establishes two control limits:

- **Lower Limit (L)** = minimum cash balance (set by management judgment)
- **Upper Limit (U)** = the trigger point at which cash is converted into marketable securities
- **Return Point (R)** = the optimal cash balance to return to

The model assumes cash flows follow a **symmetric random walk** with daily variance $\sigma^{2}$.

**Return Point Formula:**

$$R = L + \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 T \sigma^{2}}{4 i}}$$

**Upper Limit Formula:**

$$U = R + 2 \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 T \sigma^{2}}{4 i}}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If cash balance < L → Sell marketable securities to bring balance back to R.
- If cash balance > U → Buy marketable securities to bring balance back to R.
- If L < cash < U → Do nothing.

---

### 2.3 Inventory Management Models

**(a) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 A O}{C_{c}}}$$

Where:
- **A** = Annual demand (units)
- **O** = Ordering cost per order
- **C_c** = Carrying cost per unit per year (also denoted **I**)

**(b) Reorder Point (ROP):**

$$ROP = (d \times L) + SS$$

Where:
- **d** = average daily demand
- **L** = lead time in days
- **SS** = safety stock

**(c) Safety Stock with Risk Analysis:**

$$SS = z \times \sigma_{dL}$$

Where **z** = service level z-score, $\sigma_{dL}$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time.

**(d) Total Inventory Cost (TIC):**

$$TIC = \left(\dfrac{A}{Q}\right) O + \left(\dfrac{Q}{2}\right) C_{c}$$

**(e) Reorder Frequency:**

$$N = \dfrac{A}{EOQ}$$

---

### 2.4 Trade Receivables — Credit Evaluation Metrics

**(a) Average Collection Period (ACP):**

$$ACP = \dfrac{\text{Accounts Receivable}}{\text{Net Credit Sales}} \times 365$$

**(b) Aging Schedule** — classification of receivables by age (0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+ days).

**(c) Probability of Default (PD)** — assessed via credit scoring.

**(d) Effective Interest Rate of Trade Credit (when discount is offered):**

$$r_{effective} = \dfrac{\text{Discount \%}}{100 - \text{Discount \%}} \times \dfrac{365}{\text{Credit Period} - \text{Discount Period}}$$

---

### 2.5 Trade Payables — Cost Analysis

**Cost of Forgoing Cash Discount:**

Same as effective interest rate formula. If effective cost > cost of bank loan, **take the discount**; otherwise, **forgo the discount**.

**Payables Deferral Period (Stretching):**

$$\text{Stretching Period} = \text{True Credit Period} - \text{Stated Credit Period}$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Unit |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Baumol Optimal Cash | $C^{*} = \sqrt{2 b T / i}$ | b = cash need, T = txn cost, i = interest | ₹ |
| 2 | Baumol # Orders | $N^{*} = \sqrt{b i / 2 T}$ | Same as above | Orders/period |
| 3 | Miller-Orr Return Point | $R = L + \sqrt[3]{3 T \sigma^{2} / 4 i}$ | L = lower limit, $\sigma^{2}$ = variance | ₹ |
| 4 | Miller-Orr Upper Limit | $U = R + 2 \sqrt[3]{3 T \sigma^{2} / 4 i}$ | Same as above | ₹ |
| 5 | EOQ | $EOQ = \sqrt{2 A O / C_{c}}$ | A = demand, O = order cost, $C_{c}$ = carry cost | Units |
| 6 | Reorder Point | $ROP = d \times L + SS$ | d = daily demand, L = lead time, SS = safety | Units |
| 7 | Safety Stock | $SS = z \times \sigma_{dL}$ | z = service level, $\sigma_{dL}$ = std dev | Units |
| 8 | Total Inventory Cost | $TIC = (A/Q)O + (Q/2)C_{c}$ | Q = order quantity | ₹ |
| 9 | Avg Collection Period | $ACP = AR / \text{Credit Sales} \times 365$ | AR = receivables | Days |
| 10 | Effective Credit Cost | $r_{e} = \dfrac{d}{100-d} \times \dfrac{365}{c-p}$ | d = discount %, c = credit, p = discount period | % |
| 11 | Cash Conversion Cycle | $CCC = DOS + DSO - DPO$ | Days Inventory + AR − AP | Days |
| 12 | Current Ratio | $CR = CA / CL$ | Current Assets / Liabilities | Ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering Industries:**
> - **Cash models** are deployed in treasury management systems (SAP, Oracle Treasury).
> - **EOQ** drives SAP MM (Materials Management) modules in manufacturing firms.
> - **Receivables aging** is automated in ERP subledgers for B2B firms.
> - **Miller-Orr** is used in real-time corporate dashboards of large conglomerates.

---

### 2.7 The Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) — The Heart of WCM

$$CCC = \text{Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO)} + \text{Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)} - \text{Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)}$$

- **DIO** = (Inventory / COGS) × 365
- **DSO** = (Receivables / Net Credit Sales) × 365
- **DPO** = (Payables / Net Credit Purchases) × 365

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Intuition:** A shorter CCC means the firm recovers cash faster. Firms like **Apple** maintain a *negative* CCC by demanding upfront customer payments and stretching payables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Baumol Model

Let:
- **C** = amount of marketable securities converted to cash per order
- **b** = total cash requirement for the period
- **T** = transaction cost (fixed) per order
- **i** = interest rate (cost of capital) per period
- **Number of orders (N)** = b / C

**Step 1:** Total Transaction Cost (TC):

$$TC = N \times T = \dfrac{b}{C} \times T = \dfrac{bT}{C}$$

**Step 2:** Average Cash Balance:

Since cash is depleted linearly from C to 0, the average is:

$$\bar{C} = \dfrac{C + 0}{2} = \dfrac{C}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Total Holding Cost (Opportunity Cost) (HC):

$$HC = \bar{C} \times i = \dfrac{C}{2} \times i = \dfrac{Ci}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Total Cost (TOC):

$$TOC = TC + HC = \dfrac{bT}{C} + \dfrac{Ci}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate TOC w.r.t. C and set to zero (First Order Condition):

$$\dfrac{d(TOC)}{dC} = -\dfrac{bT}{C^{2}} + \dfrac{i}{2} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for C:

$$\dfrac{bT}{C^{2}} = \dfrac{i}{2}$$

$$C^{2} = \dfrac{2 bT}{i}$$

$$\boxed{C^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 bT}{i}}}$$

**Step 7:** Verify minimum (Second Order Condition):

$$\dfrac{d^{2}(TOC)}{dC^{2}} = \dfrac{2 bT}{C^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{(Confirmed minimum)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Baumol Model

**Problem [KTU University Exam - June 2022]:**
A firm requires ₹ 6,00,000 per quarter for transactions. Each conversion of securities into cash costs ₹ 150. The firm can earn 12% per annum on marketable securities. Calculate the optimal cash balance, average cash balance, and number of conversions per quarter.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- b = ₹ 6,00,000 per quarter
- T = ₹ 150 per conversion
- i = 12% per annum = 3% per quarter = 0.03

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 6,00,000 \times 150}{0.03}}$$

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1,80,00,000}{0.03}}$$

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{60,00,00,000}$$

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{6 \times 10^{9}} = 77,459.67$$

$$\boxed{C^{*} \approx \text{₹ } 77,460}$$

**Step 3:** Average Cash Balance:

$$\bar{C} = \dfrac{77,460}{2} = \text{₹ } 38,730$$

**Step 4:** Number of conversions per quarter:

$$N^{*} = \dfrac{6,00,000}{77,460} = 7.746 \approx 8 \text{ conversions}$$

**Step 5:** Total optimal cost per quarter:

$$TOC = \dfrac{6,00,000 \times 150}{77,460} + \dfrac{77,460 \times 0.03}{2}$$

$$TOC = 1,161.89 + 1,161.90 = \text{₹ } 2,323.79$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The firm should sell ₹ 77,460 of marketable securities **~8 times per quarter** to minimize its total cash management cost. The total cost at optimum is split equally between transaction cost and opportunity cost — a hallmark of the EOQ-class models.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Miller-Orr Model

**Problem [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]:**
A firm has a lower control limit of ₹ 50,000. The transaction cost is ₹ 200 per trade. The variance of daily net cash flows is ₹ 25,00,000. The interest rate is 0.025% per day. Calculate the Return Point and Upper Limit.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- L = ₹ 50,000
- T = ₹ 200
- $\sigma^{2}$ = 25,00,000
- i = 0.00025 per day

**Step 2:** Compute the cube root term:

$$\sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 \times 200 \times 25,00,000}{4 \times 0.00025}}$$

$$= \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{15,00,00,00,000}{0.001}}$$

$$= \sqrt[3]{1.5 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$= \sqrt[3]{15,000,000,000,000}$$

$$= 246,621.98$$

**Step 3:** Return Point:

$$R = 50,000 + 246,621.98 = \text{₹ } 296,621.98$$

**Step 4:** Upper Limit:

$$U = R + 2 \times 246,621.98 = 296,621.98 + 493,243.96 = \text{₹ } 789,865.94$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- If cash < ₹ 50,000 → Sell securities to restore to ₹ 2,96,622.
- If cash > ₹ 7,89,866 → Buy securities to restore to ₹ 2,96,622.
- Otherwise, do nothing.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — EOQ and ROP

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2023]:**
A manufacturing firm uses 24,000 units of raw material annually. Ordering cost is ₹ 200 per order. Carrying cost is ₹ 4 per unit per year. Lead time is 7 days. Calculate EOQ, ROP (assume 300 working days), and total inventory cost.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- A = 24,000 units
- O = ₹ 200
- $C_{c}$ = ₹ 4
- L = 7 days
- Working days = 300

**Step 2:** Compute EOQ:

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 24,000 \times 200}{4}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{96,00,000}{4}} = \sqrt{24,00,000}$$

$$EOQ = 1,549.19 \approx 1,550 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Average daily demand:

$$d = \dfrac{24,000}{300} = 80 \text{ units/day}$$

**Step 4:** Reorder Point (assuming zero safety stock):

$$ROP = 80 \times 7 = 560 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5:** Number of orders per year:

$$N = \dfrac{24,000}{1,549.19} = 15.49 \approx 16 \text{ orders}$$

**Step 6:** Total Inventory Cost:

$$TIC = \left(\dfrac{24,000}{1,549.19}\right) \times 200 + \left(\dfrac{1,549.19}{2}\right) \times 4$$

$$TIC = 3,098.39 + 3,098.39 = \text{₹ } 6,196.78$$

---

### 3.5 Risk Analysis in Inventory — Safety Stock Calculation

**Problem [KTU University Exam - June 2023]:**
A firm wants a 95% service level (z = 1.65). The standard deviation of daily demand is 20 units. Lead time = 6 days. The average daily demand is 100 units. Compute safety stock and ROP.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Standard deviation of demand during lead time:

$$\sigma_{dL} = \sigma_{d} \times \sqrt{L} = 20 \times \sqrt{6} = 20 \times 2.449 = 48.99 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Safety Stock:

$$SS = z \times \sigma_{dL} = 1.65 \times 48.99 = 80.83 \approx 81 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3:** Reorder Point:

$$ROP = (100 \times 6) + 81 = 600 + 81 = 681 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4:** Risk Interpretation:
- A 95% service level means there is a **5% probability of stockout** per replenishment cycle.
- Higher SS → Lower stockout risk → Higher carrying cost.
- **Trade-off:** Risk reduction vs. cost efficiency.

---

### 3.6 Trade Receivables — Credit Cost Analysis

**Problem:**
A firm offers terms "2/10, Net 30". A customer is deciding whether to take the discount. The bank loan rate is 14% per annum. Should the customer take the discount?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the effective interest rate formula:

$$r_{e} = \dfrac{2}{100 - 2} \times \dfrac{365}{30 - 10}$$

$$r_{e} = \dfrac{2}{98} \times \dfrac{365}{20}$$

$$r_{e} = 0.0204 \times 18.25 = 0.3724 = 37.24\%$$

**Step 2:** Compare:
- Effective cost of forgoing discount = **37.24%**
- Bank loan rate = **14%**

**Step 3:** Decision: Since 37.24% > 14%, **the customer should TAKE the discount** and finance the early payment via a bank loan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Rule:** Effective cost > cost of capital ⇒ Pay early. Effective cost < cost of capital ⇒ Defer payment (forgo discount).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Working Capital Management (WCM) Master System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INP["INPUT LAYER"]
        nodeIN1[/"Forecasted Cash Inflows"/]
        nodeIN2[/"Forecasted Cash Outflows"/]
        nodeIN3[/"Inventory Data"/]
        nodeIN4[/"Credit Sales Data"/]
        nodeIN5[/"Supplier Credit Terms"/]
    end

    subgraph MOD["PROCESSING MODELS LAYER"]
        nodeM1["BAUMOL MODEL\nDeterministic Cash"]
        nodeM2["MILLER ORR MODEL\nStochastic Cash"]
        nodeM3["EOQ AND SAFETY STOCK\nInventory Engine"]
        nodeM4["CREDIT SCORING\nReceivables Engine"]
        nodeM5["PAYABLE OPTIMIZER\nTrade Payables"]
    end

    subgraph DEC["DECISION LAYER"]
        nodeD1["Optimal Cash Balance"]
        nodeD2["Reorder Policy"]
        nodeD3["Credit Policy"]
        nodeD4["Payment Schedule"]
        nodeD5["Financing Mix"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        nodeO1[/"Treasury Action Plan"/]
        nodeO2[/"Inventory Control Plan"/]
        nodeO3[/"AR Aging Report"/]
        nodeO4[/"AP Aging Report"/]
        nodeO5[/"Liquidity Dashboard"/]
    end

    nodeIN1 --> nodeM1
    nodeIN2 --> nodeM1
    nodeIN1 --> nodeM2
    nodeIN2 --> nodeM2
    nodeIN3 --> nodeM3
    nodeIN4 --> nodeM4
    nodeIN5 --> nodeM5
    nodeM1 --> nodeD1
    nodeM2 --> nodeD1
    nodeM3 --> nodeD2
    nodeM4 --> nodeD3
    nodeM5 --> nodeD4
    nodeD1 --> nodeO1
    nodeD2 --> nodeO2
    nodeD3 --> nodeO3
    nodeD4 --> nodeO4
    nodeD1 --> nodeO5
    nodeD2 --> nodeO5
    nodeD3 --> nodeO5
    nodeD4 --> nodeO5
```

---

### 4.2 Miller-Orr Cash Control State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalZone
    NormalZone --> SellSecurities : Cash Balance falls below Lower Limit L
    NormalZone --> BuySecurities : Cash Balance rises above Upper Limit U
    NormalZone --> NormalZone : Cash between L and U
    SellSecurities --> ReturnToR : Refill to Return Point R
    BuySecurities --> ReturnToR : Invest surplus to reach R
    ReturnToR --> NormalZone
    note right of NormalZone : No transaction occurs\nin this zone
```

---

### 4.3 Inventory Management Decision Flow with Risk Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA((Start Cycle)) --> nodeB[Check Current Stock Level]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Stock at or below ROP?}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeBB[Continue Operations]
    nodeBB --> nodeB
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD[Place Order of EOQ Units]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Receive Goods after Lead Time L]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Update Inventory Database]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Recalculate Safety Stock\nbased on Demand Variance]
    nodeG --> nodeB
```

---

### 4.4 Trade Receivables & Payables Risk-Return Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REC["RECEIVABLES MANAGEMENT"]
        nodeR1[Loose Credit Policy] --> nodeR2[Higher Sales Volume]
        nodeR2 --> nodeR3[Higher AR and Bad Debt Risk]
        nodeR3 --> nodeR4{Risk Reward}
    end

    subgraph PAY["PAYABLES MANAGEMENT"]
        nodeP1[Tight Payable Policy] --> nodeP2[Lower Trade Credit Cost]
        nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Damaged Supplier Relations]
        nodeP3 --> nodeP4{Optimal Delay}
    end

    nodeR4 --> nodeFIN[/"Net Working Capital Optimization"/]
    nodeP4 --> nodeFIN
```

---

### 4.5 Short-Term Financing Source Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[/"Spontaneous Sources\nTrade Credit, Accruals"/] --> nodeS2[/"Bank Sources\nOverdraft, Cash Credit, Term Loans"/]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[/"Market Sources\nCommercial Paper, Treasury Bills"/]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[/"Specialized Sources\nFactoring, Forfaiting, Inter-corporate Deposits"/]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[/"Hybrid Sources\nSecuritization, Working Capital Bonds"/]
    nodeS5 --> nodeEND((Optimal WCM Mix))
```

> [!NOTE]
