> The diagrams follow the "Input → Process → Decision → Output" flow that the KTU 2024 Scheme expects in any Module 4 question. Mermaid syntax adheres to the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all IDs prefixed with letters, e.g., `nodeIN1`, `nodeM3`).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
*State the assumptions of the Baumol Model of cash management. Why is it considered a deterministic model?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Baumol Model is based on the following assumptions:

1. **Constant Cash Usage:** The firm uses cash at a uniform and predictable rate over the planning period.
2. **Instant Conversion:** Marketable securities can be instantaneously converted into cash without loss of value.
3. **Fixed Transaction Cost:** Each conversion of securities into cash incurs a fixed brokerage/transaction cost.
4. **Constant Opportunity Cost:** The interest rate (opportunity cost) remains stable over the period.
5. **No Safety Stock:** The firm holds no precautionary cash balance.

**Why Deterministic:** Because all the parameters (cash usage, cost of capital, transaction cost) are assumed to be known with certainty, and the timing of cash flows is predictable, the model is classified as a **deterministic inventory-type model**.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for each correct assumption; 1 mark for the "why deterministic" justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**  
*Define the Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) and explain its significance in working capital management.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** is the time period between the firm's outflow of cash for raw material purchases and the inflow of cash from sales of finished goods.

$$CCC = \text{Days Inventory Outstanding} + \text{Days Sales Outstanding} - \text{Days Payable Outstanding}$$

**Significance:**
1. A **shorter CCC** indicates faster recovery of cash, improving liquidity.
2. A **negative CCC** (e.g., Amazon, Apple) means the firm collects from customers *before* paying suppliers, which is a strong indicator of operational excellence.
3. CCC helps in benchmarking working capital efficiency across firms and industries.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*ABC Ltd. requires ₹ 12,00,000 per month for transactions. The cost of converting marketable securities into cash is ₹ 250 per transaction. The firm can earn 9% per annum on its marketable securities. Compute:*  
*(i) Optimal cash balance using the Baumol Model.*  
*(ii) Average cash balance.*  
*(iii) Number of transactions per month and per annum.*  
*(iv) Total cost of cash management at the optimum.*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- b = ₹ 12,00,000 per month
- T = ₹ 250
- i = 9% p.a. = 0.75% per month = 0.0075

**(i) Optimal Cash Balance:**

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12,00,000 \times 250}{0.0075}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\dfrac{60,00,00,000}{0.0075}} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$= \sqrt{80,000,00,00,000} = 2,82,842.71$$

$$\boxed{C^{*} \approx \text{₹ } 2,82,843}$$

**(ii) Average Cash Balance:**

$$\bar{C} = \dfrac{2,82,843}{2} = \text{₹ } 1,41,421.5}$$

**(iii) Number of Transactions:**
- Per month: N = 12,00,000 / 2,82,843 = **4.24 ≈ 4 transactions/month**
- Per annum: 4.24 × 12 = **50.91 ≈ 51 transactions/year**

**(iv) Total Optimal Cost:**

$$TOC = \dfrac{12,00,000 \times 250}{2,82,843} + \dfrac{2,82,843 \times 0.0075}{2}$$

$$TOC = 1,060.66 + 1,060.66 = \text{₹ } 2,121.32 \text{ per month}$$

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*Explain the Miller-Orr Model of cash management. How does it differ from the Baumol Model? Discuss its practical utility for firms with fluctuating cash flows.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The **Miller-Orr Model (1966)**, developed by Merton H. Miller and Daniel Orr, is a **stochastic** cash management model designed for firms with **random and uncertain cash flows**.

**Salient Features:**
1. The model sets two control limits — a **Lower Limit (L)** and an **Upper Limit (U)** — with a **Return Point (R)** in between.
2. Cash flows are assumed to follow a **symmetric random walk**.
3. Variance of net cash flows ($\sigma^{2}$) is incorporated explicitly.
4. Transactions are triggered only when the cash balance breaches the upper or lower limit.

**Formulas:**

$$R = L + \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 T \sigma^{2}}{4 i}} \quad , \quad U = R + 2 \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{3 T \sigma^{2}}{4 i}}$$

**Differences from Baumol Model:**

| Parameter | Baumol Model | Miller-Orr Model |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| Nature | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| Cash Flows | Constant | Random |
| Control Limits | Single optimum | Upper & Lower limits |
| Safety Stock | Absent | Built-in (lower limit L) |
| Practical Use | Payroll-style firms | Trading & IT firms |

**Practical Utility:** The Miller-Orr Model is highly suitable for **technology firms, trading companies, and e-commerce businesses** where daily cash flows are volatile. It provides a robust "no-action zone" that minimizes transaction frequency while maintaining liquidity buffers.

---

### ALTERNATIVE — QUESTION 3 OPTION B (14 MARKS)

**Q3 OPT B(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**  
*A firm consumes 36,000 units of raw material annually. The ordering cost is ₹ 300 per order and the carrying cost is ₹ 6 per unit per year. The lead time is 10 days, and the firm operates 300 days a year. Compute:*  
*(i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ).*  
*(ii) Reorder Level.*  
*(iii) Total annual inventory cost.*  
*(iv) If a safety stock of 200 units is maintained, what is the revised Reorder Level?*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- A = 36,000 units
- O = ₹ 300
- $C_{c}$ = ₹ 6
- L = 10 days
- Working days = 300

**(i) EOQ:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 36,000 \times 300}{6}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2,16,00,000}{6}} = \sqrt{36,00,000} = 1,897.37 \approx 1,898 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Reorder Level (without safety stock):**

$$\text{Daily demand} = 36,000 / 300 = 120 \text{ units/day}$$

$$ROL = 120 \times 10 = 1,200 \text{ units}$$

**(iii) Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$TIC = \left(\dfrac{36,000}{1,897.37}\right) \times 300 + \left(\dfrac{1,897.37}{2}\right) \times 6$$

$$TIC = 5,692.05 + 5,692.05 = \text{₹ } 11,384.10$$

**(iv) Reorder Level with Safety Stock (200 units):**

$$ROL_{revised} = 1,200 + 200 = 1,400 \text{ units}$$

---

**Q3 OPT B(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**  
*Discuss the risk analysis in inventory management. Explain how the level of service influences the safety stock and total inventory cost.*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Risk in Inventory Management:**

Inventory risk refers to the probability and magnitude of losses arising from inadequate or excessive inventory levels. The major types are:

1. **Stockout Risk:** The risk that inventory is insufficient to meet demand, leading to lost sales, customer dissatisfaction, and backorder costs.
2. **Obsolescence Risk:** The risk that inventory becomes unsellable due to expiry, technological obsolescence, or shifts in consumer preferences.
3. **Carrying Cost Risk:** High holding cost of inventory erodes profit margins.
4. **Price Risk:** Volatility in input costs can either benefit or hurt inventory value.
5. **Pilferage and Spoilage Risk:** Physical loss due to theft, damage, or perishability.

**Service Level and Safety Stock:**

The **service level** is the probability that the firm will not experience a stockout during a replenishment cycle. Higher service levels require higher safety stock:

$$SS = z \times \sigma_{dL}$$

For a 90% service level, z = 1.28; for 95%, z = 1.65; for 99%, z = 2.33.

**Trade-off Curve:**

| Service Level (%) | z-score | Safety Stock | Carrying Cost | Stockout Cost |
|-------------------|---------|--------------|---------------|----------------|
| 90 | 1.28 | Low | Low | Moderate |
| 95 | 1.65 | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| 99 | 2.33 | High | High | Very Low |

**Total Cost Implication:** As service level rises, **safety stock rises linearly with z**, but **carrying cost rises quadratically** with the order quantity. The optimal service level is achieved where the **marginal carrying cost = marginal stockout cost**.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- ABC analysis to prioritize high-value items.
- Vendor-managed inventory (VMI).
- Just-in-Time (JIT) for low-variability SKUs.
- Safety stock optimization using probability distributions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Baumol Model:** Many students use **annual** interest rate with **monthly** cash flow. Convert everything to a *common period* before substituting.
> 2. **EOQ vs. ROP Confusion:** EOQ is the **order quantity**; ROP is the **stock level triggering the order**. Never use them interchangeably.
> 3. **Safety Stock Formula:** Some students forget to take the **square root of lead time** ($\sigma_{dL} = \sigma_{d} \sqrt{L}$). This is a frequent 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Miller-Orr Cube Root:** The cube root in Miller-Orr is the most-skipped step. Use a calculator's cube root function, not square root.
> 5. **Trade Credit Cost Comparison:** Always compare the **effective annualized cost** with the **bank loan rate**, not the stated discount percentage.
> 6. **CCC Direction:** A *reduction* in CCC is good, but a *negative* CCC is exceptional — explain what business model enables it.
> 7. **Assumption Listing:** When asked about "limitations" of Baumol, students often list only 2-3 assumptions. List **all five** for full marks.
> 8. **Miller-Orr's Lower Limit:** Some answers set L = 0. The lower limit is set by **management judgment**, not a formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — PEIET743)**

- **Cash Models:** Two main models — **Baumol (deterministic)** and **Miller-Orr (stochastic)**. Know the formulas and assumptions by heart.
- **Baumol Formula:** $C^{*} = \sqrt{2 b T / i}$ — use the **square root** of the expression.
- **Miller-Orr Formula:** $R = L + \sqrt[3]{3 T \sigma^{2} / 4 i}$ — note the **cube root**.
- **EOQ Formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{2 A O / C_{c}}$ — the *Wilson Formula* (1923).
- **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \times L + SS$ — daily demand × lead time + safety stock.
- **Safety Stock:** $SS = z \times \sigma_{d} \sqrt{L}$ — z depends on service level (1.28, 1.65, 2.33).
- **CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO** — shorter CCC = better liquidity. Negative CCC = best-in-class.
- **Trade Credit Effective Cost:** $r_{e} = \dfrac{d}{100-d} \times \dfrac{365}{c-p}$ — compare with cost of capital.
- **Decision Rule on Discounts:** If effective cost > cost of capital, **TAKE** the discount. Otherwise, **FORGO** it.
- **Current Ratio:** Ideal = 2:1. **Quick Ratio:** Ideal = 1:1.
- **Inventory Risks:** Stockout, Obsolescence, Carrying, Price, Pilferage.
- **Short-Term Financing Hierarchy:** Spontaneous → Bank → Market → Specialized → Hybrid.
- **Assumption Counts (Exam Favorites):** Baumol has 5; Miller-Orr assumes random walk; EOQ assumes constant demand.
- **Factoring:** Sale of receivables to a third party (factor) at a discount — improves cash flow but reduces profit margin.
- **Commercial Paper:** Unsecured short-term promissory note issued by large corporates — interest rate typically below bank rate.
- **VCM (Vector of Cash Management) for KTU:** Always mention the *trade-off* between liquidity and profitability in any cash management answer.
- **Safety Stock Trade-off:** Higher service level = Higher safety stock = Higher carrying cost.
- **Common Numerical Format:** KTU typically provides a 14-mark split as **7 marks problem + 7 marks theory**.
- **Always Convert Periods:** If cash flow data is monthly and interest is annual, convert interest to monthly (i/12) before applying Baumol.
- **Miller-Orr Decision Zone:** "Do nothing" zone is between L and U — remember this rule for MCQs.
- **Negative CCC Examples:** Amazon, Apple, Walmart, IKEA — these firms use supplier credit and customer prepayment.
- **Forgetting Variance Term:** In Miller-Orr, $\sigma^{2}$ is the **variance**, not the standard deviation. Many students confuse the two.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Theoretical Models of Cash Management, Inventories, Trade Receivables, Trade Payables & Short-Term Financing**
> **Course:** PEIET743 — Financial Management | **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Cycles
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Cycles

# Operating Cycles in Working Capital Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** of a firm is the average time interval between the **acquisition of raw materials** and the **final realization of cash** from the sale of finished goods. It is the heartbeat of a firm's working capital requirement, representing the complete journey of capital as it gets converted from one form to another — from Cash → Raw Materials → Work-in-Progress → Finished Goods → Debtors → Cash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** The Operating Cycle is the sum total of the time durations during which cash remains tied up in various working capital components (inventory and receivables), reduced by the time period for which the firm gets credit from its suppliers (payables).

### 1.2 Constituent Stages of the Operating Cycle

A typical manufacturing operating cycle consists of **five sequential stages**:

| Stage | Working Capital Form | Description |
|-------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Stage 1** | Raw Material Storage | Period raw materials remain in store before issue to production |
| **Stage 2** | Work-in-Progress (WIP) | Period during which raw materials are converted into finished goods |
| **Stage 3** | Finished Goods Storage | Period finished goods remain in warehouse before sale |
| **Stage 4** | Receivables (Debtors) | Credit period allowed to customers for payment |
| **Stage 5** | Payables (Creditors) | Credit period received from suppliers *(deducted)* |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Biryani Pot" Cycle 🍛**
> Imagine a street food vendor in Kerala preparing Chicken Biryani. He first goes to the market and **buys chicken, rice, and spices** (Raw Material Stage). The money is now locked in raw materials. He then **cooks** the biryani (WIP Stage), holds it in a hot-case until a customer orders (Finished Goods Stage), and **delivers it on credit to a college canteen** promising payment after 7 days (Debtors Stage). However, his **vegetable supplier** gave him 4 days of credit (Payables Stage).
>
> The Operating Cycle is essentially the **"money-locked-in-kitchen" duration** — how long his rupees are stuck in the kitchen before they return as cash to his pocket!

### 1.4 The Cash Operating Cycle vs. Net Operating Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Operating Cycle (NOC) = Operating Cycle − Payables Deferral Period**
>
> The NOC represents the *true* period for which the firm must arrange financing, since suppliers' credit partially funds the operating cycle.

![Operating Cycle Visualization — Block Diagram]

```
CASH ──► RAW MATERIALS ──► WIP ──► FINISHED GOODS ──► DEBTORS ──► CASH
   ◄────────────── OPERATING CYCLE (OC) ──────────────────────┘
                                              ┌──── CREDITOR PERIOD ────┐
                                              │   (Subtracted from OC)   │
                                              ▼                          │
                                       NET OPERATING CYCLE              │
                                       (Cash Conversion Cycle) ◄────────┘
```

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics

- **Standard Conversion Basis:** **365 days per year** (KTU conventional)
- **Alternative:** **360 days per year** (banker's year, occasionally used)
- **Industry Benchmark:** Manufacturing firms typically have an operating cycle of **60 to 120 days**; FMCG firms have shorter cycles (**30–60 days**), while heavy-engineering firms have longer cycles (**150–240 days**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases — Detailed Logical Breakdown

### Phase 1: Raw Material Storage Period (R)

This is the average duration for which raw materials are held in the store before being issued to the production department. It begins when raw materials are received and ends when they are issued.

**Why it matters:** Capital is locked up in raw material inventory. The longer this period, the more working capital is tied up without generating any value addition.

**Practical drivers:**
- Bulk purchase discounts → increases R
- Just-in-Time (JIT) inventory → decreases R
- Seasonal availability of materials → increases R
- Storage capacity constraints → decreases R

### Phase 2: Work-in-Progress Period (W)

This represents the average time taken by the production process to convert raw materials into finished goods. The time raw materials remain in the production cycle awaiting completion.

**Why it matters:** A longer WIP period signals inefficiency in production scheduling, bottlenecks in machinery, or labor inefficiencies. It directly inflates working capital requirements.

**Practical drivers:**
- Production cycle length (machining, assembly, testing)
- Production scheduling efficiency
- Labor productivity
- Bottleneck operations

### Phase 3: Finished Goods Storage Period (F)

The average period for which finished goods remain in the warehouse awaiting sale. Begins when production is complete and ends when goods are dispatched to customers.

**Why it matters:** Unsold finished goods represent dead capital. They incur storage costs, insurance, and risk of obsolescence.

**Practical drivers:**
- Demand-supply synchronization
- Order book position
- Marketing & distribution efficiency

### Phase 4: Receivables (Debtors) Collection Period (D)

The average time taken by the firm to collect cash from its credit customers. Begins from the date of sale on credit and ends on the date of cash collection.

**Why it matters:** This is the terminal stage of the operating cycle where the firm finally recovers cash. Longer debtor periods tie up substantial working capital and increase bad-debt risk.

**Practical drivers:**
- Credit policy (liberal vs. strict)
- Customer profile (retail vs. institutional)
- Collection efficiency

### Phase 5: Payables (Creditors) Payment Period (C)

The average time taken by the firm to pay its suppliers for purchases made on credit. Begins from the date of purchase and ends on the date of payment to suppliers.

**Why it matters:** This is the *only stage* that *reduces* the working capital requirement, because suppliers are essentially financing the firm's operations during this period. The longer this deferral, the lower the firm's own funding need.

**Practical drivers:**
- Industry norms
- Firm's bargaining power
- Supplier relationships

## 2.2 The Logical Chain of Capital Conversion

$$
\text{Cash} \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 1}} \text{Raw Material} \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 2}} \text{WIP} \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 3}} \text{Finished Goods} \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 4}} \text{Debtors} \xrightarrow{\text{Stage 5}} \text{Cash}
$$

The entire operating cycle is essentially a **cycle of liquidity transformation**, where illiquid forms (inventory) are progressively converted into liquid form (cash).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units |
|---|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | **Raw Material Storage Period (R)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Raw Material Stock}}{\text{Raw Material Consumption per day}}$ | Days |
| 2 | **Work-in-Progress Period (W)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. WIP Stock}}{\text{Production Cost per day}}$ | Days |
| 3 | **Finished Goods Storage Period (F)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Finished Goods Stock}}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold per day}}$ | Days |
| 4 | **Receivables Collection Period (D)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Debtors}}{\text{Net Credit Sales per day}}$ | Days |
| 5 | **Payables Payment Period (C)** | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Creditors}}{\text{Net Credit Purchases per day}}$ | Days |
| 6 | **Gross Operating Cycle (OC)** | $OC = R + W + F + D$ | Days |
| 7 | **Net Operating Cycle (NOC)** | $NOC = R + W + F + D - C$ | Days |
| 8 | **Daily Cost of Goods Sold (COGS/day)** | $\dfrac{\text{Annual COGS}}{365}$ | ₹/day |
| 9 | **Daily Raw Material Consumption** | $\dfrac{\text{Annual Raw Material Consumption}}{365}$ | ₹/day |
| 10 | **Working Capital Requirement (approx.)** | $NOC \times \text{Daily Operating Cost}$ | ₹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Always use **365 days** as the divisor unless specifically stated otherwise. Mixing 360 and 365 days within the same problem attracts a **0.5 mark penalty** per KTU valuation norms.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operating cycle analysis is critical in:
- **Production Planning:** Determining the procurement schedule
- **Working Capital Financing:** Banks use NOC to determine short-term loan limits
- **Working Capital Management:** TATA Motors, Maruti, and other manufacturers optimize each stage to reduce NOC
- **Supply Chain Management:** KPI for vendor performance and logistics
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System's famous reduction of operating cycle to **less than 5 days** in some models
- **Working Capital Ratings:** CRISIL and ICRA factor NOC into corporate credit ratings
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Cycle Formula

### Step 1: Define Each Component
Let:
- $R$ = Raw material storage period (days)
- $W$ = WIP holding period (days)
- $F$ = Finished goods storage period (days)
- $D$ = Debtors collection period (days)
- $C$ = Creditors payment period (days)

### Step 2: Identify the Capital Lock-up Journey

Capital is locked up sequentially as:

$$
T_{\text{lock}} = t_{R} + t_{W} + t_{F} + t_{D}
$$

This gives the **Gross Operating Cycle**:

$$
OC_{\text{gross}} = R + W + F + D
$$

### Step 3: Account for Supplier Credit

The firm's suppliers finance the purchase of raw materials for a period $C$. This effectively **reduces** the duration for which the firm needs to fund the cycle from its own sources:

$$
OC_{\text{net}} = OC_{\text{gross}} - C
$$

### Step 4: Final Operating Cycle Equation

$$
\boxed{NOC = R + W + F + D - C}
$$

This is the **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** — the period for which the firm must arrange working capital financing.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### Problem Statement
> A manufacturing firm provides the following data for the year ending 31-03-2024:
> - Raw Material Consumption: ₹ 18,25,000
> - Average Raw Material Stock: ₹ 3,00,000
> - Total Production Cost: ₹ 36,50,000
> - Average WIP Stock: ₹ 2,00,000
> - Cost of Goods Sold: ₹ 54,75,000
> - Average Finished Goods Stock: ₹ 4,50,000
> - Total Sales: ₹ 73,00,000 (90% credit)
> - Average Debtors: ₹ 9,00,000
> - Average Creditors: ₹ 2,70,000
> - Raw Material Purchases: ₹ 20,00,000
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Raw Material Storage Period, (b) WIP Period, (c) Finished Goods Storage Period, (d) Receivables Collection Period, (e) Payables Payment Period, (f) Net Operating Cycle.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

#### (a) Raw Material Storage Period (R)

$$
R = \frac{\text{Average Raw Material Stock}}{\text{Raw Material Consumption per day}}
$$

$$
\text{RM consumption per day} = \frac{18{,}25{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 5{,}000
$$

$$
R = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000} = 60 \text{ days}
$$

#### (b) Work-in-Progress Period (W)

$$
W = \frac{\text{Average WIP Stock}}{\text{Production Cost per day}}
$$

$$
\text{Production cost per day} = \frac{36{,}50{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 10{,}000
$$

$$
W = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 20 \text{ days}
$$

#### (c) Finished Goods Storage Period (F)

$$
F = \frac{\text{Average Finished Goods Stock}}{\text{COGS per day}}
$$

$$
\text{COGS per day} = \frac{54{,}75{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 15{,}000
$$

$$
F = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{15{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

#### (d) Receivables Collection Period (D)

$$
D = \frac{\text{Average Debtors}}{\text{Credit Sales per day}}
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales} = 73{,}00{,}000 \times 0.90 = \text{₹ } 65{,}70{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales per day} = \frac{65{,}70{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 18{,}000
$$

$$
D = \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{18{,}000} = 50 \text{ days}
$$

#### (e) Payables Payment Period (C)

$$
C = \frac{\text{Average Creditors}}{\text{Credit Purchases per day}}
$$

$$
\text{Credit purchases per day} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 5{,}479.45
$$

$$
C = \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{5{,}479.45} = 49.27 \approx 49 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board convention: Many textbooks round this to 50 days if total purchases are assumed 100% credit. Always state the assumption clearly.

#### (f) Net Operating Cycle (NOC)

$$
NOC = R + W + F + D - C
$$

$$
NOC = 60 + 20 + 30 + 50 - 49
$$

$$
\boxed{NOC = 111 \text{ days}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stage-wise Impact Table

| Stage | Duration (days) | Capital Locked (₹) | % of Total OC |
|-------|----------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Raw Material (R) | 60 | 3,00,000 | 40.0% |
| WIP (W) | 20 | 2,00,000 | 13.3% |
| Finished Goods (F) | 30 | 4,50,000 | 20.0% |
| Debtors (D) | 50 | 9,00,000 | 33.3% |
| **Subtotal (Gross OC)** | **160** | **18,50,000** | **—** |
| Less: Creditors (C) | 49 | 2,70,000 | (14.6%) |
| **Net OC (Cash Cycle)** | **111** | **15,80,000** | **100%** |

### 3.4 Working Capital Requirement Computation

$$
\text{Daily Operating Cost} = \frac{54{,}75{,}000}{365} = \text{₹ } 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Net Working Capital Required} = NOC \times \text{Daily Operating Cost}
$$

$$
WC = 111 \times 15{,}000 = \text{₹ } 16{,}65{,}000
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The firm's working capital requirement of ₹16.65 lakh must be financed from a mix of **permanent and temporary sources** (e.g., bank overdraft, cash credit, commercial paper). This estimation is the foundation of **Tandon's Committee and Chore Committee** norms for bank lending in India.

### 3.5 Effect of Decisions on Operating Cycle

| Decision | Effect on NOC | Impact on WC |
|----------|---------------|--------------|
| Buying raw material in bulk | ↑ R | ↑ WC |
| Faster production methods | ↓ W | ↓ WC |
| Improved marketing & sales | ↓ F | ↓ WC |
| Stricter credit policy | ↓ D | ↓ WC |
| Renegotiating supplier credit | ↑ C | ↓ WC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define the term 'Operating Cycle' as used in working capital management. State its significance in financial decision-making."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Operating Cycle is the average time period that elapses between the acquisition of raw materials and the final realization of cash from the sale of finished goods. Mathematically:

$$
OC = R + W + F + D
$$

where $R$ = Raw material storage period, $W$ = WIP period, $F$ = Finished goods storage period, and $D$ = Debtors collection period.

**Significance (1 Mark):**
The operating cycle determines the **working capital requirement** of a firm. A shorter cycle implies **lower financing needs**, **better liquidity**, and **higher profitability**, making it a critical metric for treasury and working capital management decisions.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Distinguish between Gross Operating Cycle and Net Operating Cycle."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Basis | Gross Operating Cycle | Net Operating Cycle |
|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Definition** | Total time from RM purchase to cash realization | Time for which firm must arrange own finance |
| **Formula** | $R + W + F + D$ | $R + W + F + D - C$ |
| **Creditors Period** | Not deducted | Deducted |
| **Use** | Indicates total capital lock-up | Indicates actual financing need |
| **Also called** | Operating Cycle | Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Internal Choice Pattern]**

**PQR Ltd. provides the following information for the year ended 31-03-2024:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Raw Material Consumed | 14,60,000 |
| Average Raw Material Stock | 2,92,000 |
| Average Work-in-Progress Stock | 1,82,500 |
| Total Manufacturing Cost | 36,50,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 54,75,000 |
| Average Finished Goods Stock | 4,50,000 |
| Total Sales | 73,00,000 (80% on credit) |
| Average Debtors | 9,12,500 |
| Average Creditors | 1,82,500 |
| Raw Material Purchases | 16,42,500 |

**(a) Calculate the duration of each stage of the operating cycle. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Determine the Net Operating Cycle and estimate the working capital requirement. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Stage Durations (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Daily Equivalents (using 365 days)**

$$
\text{RM/day} = \frac{14{,}60{,}000}{365} = 4{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Production cost/day} = \frac{36{,}50{,}000}{365} = 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{COGS/day} = \frac{54{,}75{,}000}{365} = 15{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales} = 73{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = 58{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales/day} = \frac{58{,}40{,}000}{365} = 16{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Credit purchases/day} = \frac{16{,}42{,}500}{365} = 4{,}500
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Period Formulas**

$$
R = \frac{2{,}92{,}000}{4{,}000} = 73 \text{ days}
$$

$$
W = \frac{1{,}82{,}500}{10{,}000} = 18.25 \approx 18 \text{ days}
$$

$$
F = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{15{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}
$$

$$
D = \frac{9{,}12{,}500}{16{,}000} = 57.03 \approx 57 \text{ days}
$$

$$
C = \frac{1{,}82{,}500}{4{,}500} = 40.56 \approx 41 \text{ days}
$$

| Stage | Duration |
|-------|----------|
| R | 73 days |
| W | 18 days |
| F | 30 days |
| D | 57 days |
| C | 41 days |

**Part (b) — Net Operating Cycle & Working Capital (7 Marks)**

**Step 3: Compute NOC**

$$
NOC = R + W + F + D - C
$$

$$
NOC = 73 + 18 + 30 + 57 - 41
$$

$$
\boxed{NOC = 137 \text{ days}}
$$

**Step 4: Working Capital Requirement**

$$
WC = NOC \times \text{Daily Operating Cost}
$$

$$
WC = 137 \times 15{,}000 = \text{₹ } 20{,}55{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**
The firm requires approximately **₹20.55 lakh** of working capital financing to sustain its 137-day cash conversion cycle. Management should explore opportunities to **reduce D (debtors)** and **increase C (creditors)** to bring down the WC requirement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Internal Choice Pattern]**

**ABC Manufacturing Co. has furnished the following data:**

| Item | Amount (₹) |
|------|-----------|
| Average Raw Material Stock | 5,00,000 |
| Average WIP Stock | 3,00,000 |
| Average Finished Goods Stock | 6,00,000 |
| Average Debtors | 8,00,000 |
| Average Creditors | 4,00,000 |
| Raw Material Consumption | 25,00,000 |
| Manufacturing Cost | 40,00,000 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 60,00,000 |
| Net Credit Sales | 80,00,000 |
| Credit Purchases | 30,00,000 |

**(a) Calculate the Operating Cycle and Cash Operating Cycle. Comment on the liquidity position. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Suggest FOUR specific managerial actions to reduce the operating cycle. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — OC and NOC Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Daily Equivalents (365 days)**

$$
\text{RM/day} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{365} = 6{,}849.32
$$

$$
\text{Production/day} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{365} = 10{,}958.90
$$

$$
\text{COGS/day} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{365} = 16{,}438.36
$$

$$
\text{Credit sales/day} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{365} = 21{,}917.81
$$

$$
\text{Credit purchases/day} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{365} = 8{,}219.18
$$

**Step 2: Stage Durations**

$$
R = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{6{,}849.32} \approx 73 \text{ days}
$$

$$
W = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{10{,}958.90} \approx 27 \text{ days}
$$

$$
F = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{16{,}438.36} \approx 37 \text{ days}
$$

$$
D = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{21{,}917.81} \approx 37 \text{ days}
$$

$$
C = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}219.18} \approx 49 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3: Operating Cycle (Gross)**

$$
OC = 73 + 27 + 37 + 37 = 174 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4: Net Operating Cycle**

$$
NOC = 174 - 49 = 125 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 5: Liquidity Comment (1 Mark)**

The NOC of **125 days** is **fairly long** for a manufacturing firm, indicating **substantial working capital tied up** and the need for sizeable short-term borrowings. Liquidity is **moderate but strained**.

**Part (b) — Managerial Actions to Reduce OC (7 Marks — 4 actions × ~1.75 marks each)**

1. **Reduce Raw Material Storage Period (R):** Adopt **Just-in-Time (JIT)** procurement, enter into long-term supply contracts, and avoid bulk buying unless discounts justify holding cost.

2. **Reduce WIP Period (W):** Improve **production scheduling**, eliminate bottlenecks, deploy **Lean Manufacturing** and Six Sigma tools, and invest in faster machinery.

3. **Reduce Finished Goods Storage (F):** Strengthen **marketing and distribution networks**, use demand-forecasting tools, and offer **early-bird discounts** to accelerate sales.

4. **Reduce Debtors Period (D) / Increase Creditors Period (C):** Tighten **credit policy** (5/10, 2/15 terms), use **factoring services**, incentivize early payment, and renegotiate **longer credit terms** with suppliers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Observations):**
>
> 1. **Mixing the divisor:** Using 360 for some stages and 365 for others. **Use 365 consistently** unless the problem states otherwise. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Confusing "Total Sales" with "Credit Sales":** Many students use total sales for debtors' period. Always use **Net Credit Sales**. **−1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to credit "Net Operating Cycle" properly:** Some students compute NOC = R+W+F+D and stop. Always subtract **C**. **−2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Using Raw Material Stock instead of Consumption for R:** The denominator must be **Raw Material Consumption per day**, not purchases. **−1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Skipping daily equivalent calculations:** Writing 3,00,000 / 4,000 directly is acceptable, but **must show daily cost computation step**. **−0.5 mark.**
>
> 6. **Ignoring the parallel nature of credit period:** Some students plot the creditor period *after* the gross cycle. Remember — **credit period overlaps the raw material stage**, so it is deducted. **−1 mark.**
>
> 7. **No interpretation:** Even in computational questions, the KTU board expects a **1-line conclusion** on liquidity/working capital position. **−0.5 to −1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Operating Cycles (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Operating Cycle = R + W + F + D** (Gross)
- ✅ **Net Operating Cycle = R + W + F + D − C** (Cash Conversion Cycle)
- ✅ **Always use 365 days** as the annual divisor (KTU standard)
- ✅ **Denominator for D = Credit Sales per day** (NOT total sales)
- ✅ **Denominator for C = Credit Purchases per day** (NOT total purchases)
- ✅ **Denominator for R = Raw Material Consumption per day** (NOT purchases)
- ✅ **Working Capital = NOC × Daily COGS**
- ✅ **Shorter OC = Better liquidity = Lower financing cost**
- ✅ **Creditor period is the only stage that REDUCES WC need**
- ✅ **Tandon Committee & Chore Committee** norms in India use NOC-based lending limits
- ✅ **JIT, Lean, Six Sigma** are real-world tools to compress OC
- ✅ **Industry benchmarks:** FMCG ≈ 30–60 days; Manufacturing ≈ 90–120 days; Heavy Engg. ≈ 150–240 days
- ✅ **The 4 management levers:** Procurement, Production, Inventory, Credit
- ✅ **Parallel & Sequential stages** — Creditor period overlaps RM stage, not the entire OC
- ✅ **Interpretation step is mandatory** in KTU 14-mark answers (1–2 marks reserved)
- ✅ **Formula recall is critical** — Write the formulas before substituting numerical values

**Mnemonic for Order of Stages: "R W F D" → "Robert Went For Dinner" 🍽️**
*Remember to subtract Creditor (C) for Net OC.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Budgeting: Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capital Budgeting: Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Capital Budgeting?

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Capital Budgeting (also called Capital Expenditure Decisions / Investment Appraisal)** is the process of planning, evaluating, and selecting long-term investment projects that are expected to generate cash flows over multiple years. It involves committing a firm's current funds to long-term assets with the objective of **maximizing shareholder wealth**.

The word "Capital" refers to long-term funds, and "Budgeting" refers to planning. Therefore, capital budgeting is essentially **planning for long-term capital investments**.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Why is it called "Budgeting"?** A capital budget lists all proposed capital expenditures along with the expected timing of cash outflows and inflows, much like a personal budget, but spread over years and involving substantial amounts (often **₹1 Lakh to ₹100 Crore+**).

---

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy 🏠

Imagine you are a young professional with **₹10,00,000 in savings**. You have three options:

| Option | Investment | Returns (per year) | Risk |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|------|
| A | Buy a small flat and rent it out | ₹80,000 | Low |
| B | Start a tiffin service | ₹1,20,000 (but uneven) | Medium |
| C | Invest in the share market | ₹1,50,000 (uncertain) | High |

You cannot pursue all three. So, you need a **scientific method to evaluate**:
- How soon will I recover my ₹10 Lakh?
- What is the *real* return after considering the time value of money?
- Which option truly maximizes my wealth?

This decision-making process — comparing large long-term investments to maximize wealth — is exactly what **Capital Budgeting** does for a business.

> [!TIP] 
> **Key Insight:** A company replaces a ₹2 Crore machine, opens a new factory, or launches a new product — every such long-term decision is a **capital budgeting decision**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Capital Budgeting Methods

```
Capital Budgeting Methods
        │
        ├── 1. CONVENTIONAL (Non-Discounted / Traditional)
        │       ├── Payback Period Method
        │       └── Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)
        │
        └── 2. DISCOUNTED CASH FLOW (DCF) Methods
                ├── Net Present Value (NPV)
                ├── Internal Rate of Return (IRR)
                ├── Profitability Index (PI)
                └── Discounted Payback Period
```

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> The fundamental difference between the two categories is that **DCF methods consider the Time Value of Money (TVM)**, while conventional methods do not. The KTU 2024 syllabus places heavy weightage on DCF methods because they are theoretically superior and recommended by modern finance theory.

---

## 1.4 Steps in the Capital Budgeting Process

1. **Identification** of investment opportunities (new product, expansion, replacement).
2. **Estimation of Cash Flows** (initial outlay + future net cash inflows).
3. **Evaluation** using suitable techniques (Conventional / DCF).
4. **Selection** of the most profitable project within the capital rationing constraint.
5. **Implementation** and **Post-Audit** of the chosen project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional (Non-Discounted) Methods — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Payback Period (PBP) Method

**Concept:** The Payback Period is the **length of time** required to recover the original investment from the project's net cash inflows.

**Logic Step-by-Step:**
- Step 1: Add cash inflows year by year (cumulatively).
- Step 2: Identify the year in which cumulative cash flow equals the initial investment.
- Step 3: If recovery occurs mid-year, use interpolation.

**Two Variants:**

**(a) Simple Payback Period** — when cash inflows are uniform:
$$\text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$

**(b) Cumulative Payback Period** — when cash inflows are uneven:
$$\text{Payback Period} = \text{Year before full recovery} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount at start of that year}}{\text{Cash flow during that year}}$$

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common error is forgetting that Payback ignores the time value of money AND ignores cash flows that occur *after* the payback period. A project with 3-year payback but 10-year life still has 7 years of cash flows the method ignores!

**Decision Rule:** Accept the project if Payback ≤ Target Payback set by management.

---

### 2.1.2 Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) Method

**Concept:** ARR measures the **average accounting profit** (not cash flow) as a percentage of the **average investment**.

**Key Idea:** Uses accounting numbers (Net Profit after depreciation) rather than cash flows, hence it's the method most aligned with **financial statements**.

---

## 2.2 Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Net Present Value (NPV)

**Concept:** NPV = Present Value of all future cash inflows − Present Value of all outflows (including initial investment).

**Why NPV is considered the most reliable method:**
- It accounts for the **Time Value of Money**.
- It considers **all** cash flows over the project's life.
- It directly measures **wealth creation** in monetary terms.
- It uses the **opportunity cost of capital** as the discount rate.

**Decision Rule:**
- If NPV > 0 → **Accept** (project earns more than the cost of capital)
- If NPV = 0 → Indifferent (project earns exactly the cost of capital)
- If NPV < 0 → **Reject** (project destroys value)

### 2.2.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

**Concept:** IRR is the discount rate that makes the NPV of a project equal to **zero**. It is the project's **own rate of return**.

**The Logic of "Internal":** The rate is *internal* to the project itself — it does not depend on the market rate or cost of capital (though we compare IRR to cost of capital to decide).

**Decision Rule:** Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital (r).

> [!NOTE] 
> **Mathematical Note:** Solving IRR requires iterative methods (Trial & Error, or interpolation) because the equation is a polynomial of degree n in IRR and has no closed-form solution.

### 2.2.3 Profitability Index (PI)

**Concept:** PI is the ratio of **Present Value of future cash inflows** to the **Present Value of outflows** (or to the initial investment in single-project cases).

**Decision Rule:** Accept if PI > 1. For mutually exclusive projects with capital rationing, PI helps rank projects by value created per rupee invested.

### 2.2.4 Discounted Payback Period

**Concept:** Same logic as Payback, but uses **discounted cash flows** (i.e., present values) instead of raw cash flows. It overcomes the major flaw of simple payback by considering TVM.

---

## 2.3 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Key Input |
|---|--------|---------|---------------|-----------|
| 1 | Simple Payback | $\text{PBP} = \dfrac{I_0}{\text{Annual CF}}$ | $\text{PBP} \le \text{Target}$ | Uniform CF |
| 2 | Cumulative Payback | $A + \dfrac{\text{Unrecovered at }A}{CF_A}$ | $\text{PBP} \le \text{Target}$ | Uneven CF |
| 3 | Accounting Rate of Return | $\text{ARR} = \dfrac{\text{Avg. Annual Profit}}{\text{Avg. Investment}} \times 100$ | $\text{ARR} \ge \text{Target ARR}$ | Accounting Profit |
| 4 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | $NPV > 0$ | Discount rate r |
| 5 | NPV (Annuity case) | $NPV = CF \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} - I_0$ | $NPV > 0$ | Uniform CF |
| 6 | Internal Rate of Return | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | $IRR > r$ | Iterative |
| 7 | IRR (Interpolation) | $IRR = r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(r_H - r_L)$ | $IRR > r$ | Two guess rates |
| 8 | Profitability Index | $PI = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{I_0}$ | $PI > 1$ | PV of inflows |
| 9 | Discounted Payback | Same as Payback, but on discounted CFs | $\text{DPBP} \le \text{Target}$ | Discounted CF |

**Where the symbols mean:**
- $I_0$ = Initial Investment
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year t
- $r$ = Cost of capital / discount rate
- $r_L$ = Lower trial rate
- $r_H$ = Higher trial rate
- $NPV_L$ = NPV at lower rate (positive)
- $NPV_H$ = NPV at higher rate (negative)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Methods

| Industry Application | Method Most Used | Why |
|----------------------|------------------|-----|
| Manufacturing capacity expansion | NPV | Accurate wealth measurement |
| Pharmaceutical R&D | IRR | Compare high-risk returns |
| IT project prioritization | PI | Capital rationing is common |
| Government projects | Discounted Payback | Liquidity is critical |
| SME machinery purchase | Payback Period | Quick decisions, low cost analysis |

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — All Methods in One Problem

**Problem Statement (Unified for all methods):**

> A company is evaluating a project with the following details:
> - **Initial Investment** = ₹1,00,000
> - **Annual Cash Inflow (uniform)** = ₹30,000 per year for **5 years**
> - **Salvage Value** = Nil
> - **Cost of Capital (r)** = **10%**
> - **Depreciation (Straight Line)** = ₹1,00,000 ÷ 5 = **₹20,000 per year**
> - **Target Payback Period** = 3 years
> - **Target ARR** = 20%

We will evaluate this project using **all six methods** below.

---

### METHOD 1: Simple Payback Period

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback Period} &= \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}} \\
&= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&= 3.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cumulative Table (Verification):**

| Year | Cash Inflow | Cumulative CF |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | 30,000 | 30,000 |
| 2 | 30,000 | 60,000 |
| 3 | 30,000 | 90,000 |
| 4 | 30,000 | 1,20,000 ← Exceeds 1,00,000 in Year 4 |

**Refined Payback (using interpolation):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PBP} &= 3 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 90{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&= 3 + \frac{10{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&= 3 + 0.33 \\
&= 3.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Payback (3.33 yrs) > Target (3 yrs) → **REJECT** ❌

---

### METHOD 2: Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)

**Step 1: Compute Annual Accounting Profit**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Accounting Profit} &= \text{Cash Inflow} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 30{,}000 - 20{,}000 \\
&= 10{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Investment**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Investment} &= \frac{\text{Initial Cost} + \text{Salvage Value}}{2} \\
&= \frac{1{,}00{,}000 + 0}{2} \\
&= 50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute ARR**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARR} &= \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** ARR (20%) = Target ARR (20%) → **In different** (just acceptable) 🟡

---

### METHOD 3: Net Present Value (NPV) — DCF Method

**Step 1: Identify the Annuity Factor Formula**
$$
\text{PVIFA}(r, n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Annuity Factor at 10% for 5 years**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(10\%, 5) &= \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} \\
&= \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} \\
&= \frac{0.3791}{0.10} \\
&= 3.7908
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Present Value of Cash Inflows**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{inflows}} &= 30{,}000 \times 3.7908 \\
&= 1{,}13{,}724
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate NPV**
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= PV_{\text{inflows}} - \text{Initial Investment} \\
&= 1{,}13{,}724 - 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= +13{,}724
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** NPV > 0 → **ACCEPT** ✅

> [!NOTE] 
> **Important Observation:** The same project was **REJECTED by Payback (3.33 yrs)** and **ACCEPTED by NPV (₹13,724)**. This is because NPV considers all 5 years of cash flows and the time value of money, while Payback stops counting after year 3.33. This is the most common contradiction in KTU problems!

---

### METHOD 4: Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — DCF Method

**Step 1: Set up the IRR Equation**
$$
NPV = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{30{,}000}{(1+IRR)^t} - 1{,}00{,}000 = 0
$$

**Step 2: Solve Annuity Factor**
$$
\begin{aligned}
30{,}000 \times \text{PVIFA}(IRR, 5) &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{PVIFA}(IRR, 5) &= \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} \\
&= 3.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Trial Rates — Find where PVIFA = 3.3333**

| Trial Rate | PVIFA(r, 5) | NPV |
|------------|-------------|-----|
| 12% | 3.6048 | +8,144 |
| 13% | 3.5172 | +5,516 |
| 15% | 3.3522 | +1,566 |
| 16% | 3.2743 | -1,771 |

So IRR lies between 15% and 16%.

**Step 4: Linear Interpolation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L) \\
&= 15\% + \frac{1{,}566}{1{,}566 - (-1{,}771)} \times (16\% - 15\%) \\
&= 15\% + \frac{1{,}566}{3{,}337} \times 1\% \\
&= 15\% + 0.4694\% \\
&\approx 15.47\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** IRR (15.47%) > Cost of Capital (10%) → **ACCEPT** ✅

---

### METHOD 5: Profitability Index (PI) — DCF Method

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{PV \text{ of Cash Inflows}}{Initial\ Investment} \\
&= \frac{1{,}13{,}724}{1{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 1.137
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** PI (1.137) > 1 → **ACCEPT** ✅

---

### METHOD 6: Discounted Payback Period — DCF Method

**Step 1: Discount each year's cash flow at 10%**

| Year | Cash Flow | Discount Factor | Discounted CF | Cumulative DCF |
|------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | 30,000 | 1/(1.10)¹ = 0.9091 | 27,273 | 27,273 |
| 2 | 30,000 | 1/(1.10)² = 0.8264 | 24,793 | 52,066 |
| 3 | 30,000 | 1/(1.10)³ = 0.7513 | 22,539 | 74,605 |
| 4 | 30,000 | 1/(1.10)⁴ = 0.6830 | 20,490 | 95,095 |
| 5 | 30,000 | 1/(1.10)⁵ = 0.6209 | 18,628 | 1,13,723 |

**Step 2: Identify Recovery Year**
- After Year 4: ₹95,095 recovered
- Need: ₹1,00,000 → Short by ₹4,905
- Year 5 DCF: ₹18,628

**Step 3: Interpolation**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Discounted Payback} &= 4 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 95{,}095}{18{,}628} \\
&= 4 + \frac{4{,}905}{18{,}628} \\
&= 4 + 0.263 \\
&= 4.26 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Discounted Payback (4.26 yrs) > 3 yrs → **REJECT** ❌

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Six Methods on the Same Project

| Method | Value Computed | Decision Criterion | Result | Accept? |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|--------|---------|
| Payback Period | 3.33 years | ≤ 3 years | Fail | ❌ Reject |
| ARR | 20% | ≥ 20% | Equal | 🟡 Marginal |
| NPV | +₹13,724 | > 0 | Pass | ✅ Accept |
| IRR | 15.47% | > 10% | Pass | ✅ Accept |
| PI | 1.137 | > 1 | Pass | ✅ Accept |
| Discounted Payback | 4.26 years | ≤ 3 years | Fail | ❌ Reject |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **The Major Insight:** Conventional methods (Payback, ARR) reject the project while DCF methods (NPV, IRR, PI) accept it. This happens because DCF methods properly value **cash flows occurring after the payback period** and account for the **time value of money**. KTU examiners love testing this contradiction!

---

## 3.3 Master Comparison Table: Conventional vs DCF Methods

| Parameter | Conventional Methods | DCF Methods |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Time Value of Money** | ❌ Ignored | ✅ Considered |
| **Cash Flow Type Used** | Accounting Profit (ARR) / Raw CF (Payback) | Discounted (Present Value) CF |
| **All Cash Flows Considered** | ❌ No (Payback ignores post-recovery flows) | ✅ Yes |
| **Discount Rate Required** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Cost of Capital) |
| **Theoretical Superiority** | Lower | Higher |
| **Computational Complexity** | Simple | Moderate to Complex |
| **Practical Use Today** | Supplementary / Quick screening | Primary decision tool |
| **Objective** | Recovery / Book return | Wealth Maximization |
| **Measure of Output** | Years / % | ₹ amount / % return / Ratio |
| **Recommended for Final Decision** | No | Yes (NPV is the gold standard) |

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Capital Budgeting Process — Top-Down Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: Identify Investment Opportunities] --> B[STEP 2: Estimate Project Cash Flows]
    B --> C[STEP 3: Determine Cost of Capital r]
    C --> D[STEP 4: Apply Capital Budgeting Methods]
    D --> E1[Payback Period]
    D --> E2[ARR]
    D --> F1[NPV]
    D --> F2[IRR]
    D --> F3[PI]
    D --> F4[Discounted Payback]
    E1 --> G[STEP 5: Compare Results with Criteria]
    E2 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{NPV Greater Than Zero and IRR Greater Than r}
    H -- Yes --> I[ACCEPT the Project]
    H -- No --> J[REJECT the Project]
    I --> K[STEP 6: Implementation and Post Audit]
    J --> L[Re-evaluate or Drop]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Conventional vs DCF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Project Proposal] --> B{Which Method Used}
    B -- Conventional --> C[Payback Analysis]
    B -- Conventional --> D[ARR Analysis]
    B -- DCF --> E[NPV Analysis]
    B -- DCF --> F[IRR Analysis]
    B -- DCF --> G[PI Analysis]
    C --> H{PBP Within Target}
    D --> I{ARR Above Target}
    E --> J{NPV Positive}
    F --> K{IRR Above Cost of Capital}
    G --> L{PI Greater Than 1}
    H -- Yes --> M[Accept]
    H -- No --> N[Reject]
    I -- Yes --> M
    I -- No --> N
    J -- Yes --> M
    J -- No --> N
    K -- Yes --> M
    K -- No --> N
    L -- Yes --> M
    L -- No --> N
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Capital Budgeting Methods (Preference Order)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[KTU Recommended Priority for Project Decisions] --> B[Rank 1: NPV]
    A --> C[Rank 2: IRR]
    A --> D[Rank 3: PI]
    A --> E[Rank 4: Discounted Payback]
    A --> F[Rank 5: Simple Payback]
    A --> G[Rank 6: ARR]
    B --> H[Most Theoretically Sound]
    G --> I[Least Reliable]
    H --> J[Use as Final Decision Tool]
    I --> K[Use Only for Quick Screening]
```

> [!NOTE] 
> **Reading the Diagram:** KTU/finance theory ranks NPV as the **gold standard** because it directly measures wealth created (in ₹ terms). IRR is secondary because it gives a % return which is comparable across projects of different sizes. PI is used in **capital rationing** situations. ARR and simple Payback are screening tools only.
