# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define Capital Budgeting. List the various methods of capital budgeting with a brief note on each."**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Capital Budgeting is the process of planning and evaluating long-term investment decisions involving substantial capital outlays, with the objective of maximizing shareholder wealth. It deals with allocating a firm's funds to long-term assets such as plant, machinery, land, and new product lines.

**Methods of Capital Budgeting:**

1. **Payback Period Method** – Time required to recover the initial investment.
2. **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)** – Average accounting profit as a % of average investment.
3. **Net Present Value (NPV)** – Difference between present value of cash inflows and outflows.
4. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** – Discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
5. **Profitability Index (PI)** – Ratio of PV of inflows to PV of outflows.
6. **Discounted Payback Period** – Payback using discounted cash flows.

The first two are **Conventional (Non-Discounted)** methods, and the rest are **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** methods.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + Listing 6 methods with 1-line each (2) = **3 marks**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Distinguish between Conventional and Discounted Cash Flow methods of capital budgeting."**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Conventional Methods | DCF Methods |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|
| Time Value of Money | Not considered | Considered |
| Discount Rate | Not required | Required (Cost of capital) |
| Cash Flow Type | Accounting profit / raw CF | Discounted CF (Present values) |
| All Cash Flows Considered | No (Payback ignores post-recovery) | Yes |
| Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Examples | Payback, ARR | NPV, IRR, PI, Discounted Payback |

Conventional methods serve as **screening tools**, while DCF methods are used for **final investment decisions**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Any 3 valid distinctions with brief explanation = **3 marks**

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE

---

### **🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — DCF Methods Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Net Present Value (NPV) method** in detail. State its **merits and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A project requires an **initial investment of ₹5,00,000** and generates a **uniform annual cash inflow of ₹1,50,000 for 6 years**. The cost of capital is **12% p.a.** Compute:
   - (i) Net Present Value
   - (ii) Internal Rate of Return
   - (iii) Profitability Index

Advise whether the project should be accepted. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a) NPV Method (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
> NPV is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows over the life of a project, discounted at the cost of capital.

**Formula (1 Mark):**
$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$$

**Merits (3 Marks):**
1. **Considers Time Value of Money** – All cash flows are discounted.
2. **Considers all cash flows** over the project's life.
3. **Directly measures wealth creation** in absolute ₹ terms.
4. **Objective** – Uses market-based discount rate.
5. **Consistent with shareholder wealth maximization** objective.

**Limitations (2 Marks):**
1. Requires accurate estimation of cost of capital and future cash flows.
2. Difficult to compute for projects with unequal lives.
3. Conflicts with IRR in mutually exclusive projects.
4. Not useful in capital rationing situations.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b) Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 5{,}00{,}000$
- $CF = 1{,}50{,}000$ per year
- $n = 6$ years
- $r = 12\%$

---

**(i) Net Present Value (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute the Annuity Factor.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PVIFA}(12\%, 6) &= \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-6}}{0.12} \\
&= \frac{1 - 0.5066}{0.12} \\
&= \frac{0.4934}{0.12} \\
&= 4.1114
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute PV of Inflows.
$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_{\text{inflows}} &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 4.1114 \\
&= 6{,}16{,}710
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute NPV.
$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= 6{,}16{,}710 - 5{,}00{,}000 \\
&= +1{,}16{,}710
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** NPV > 0 → **Project should be ACCEPTED** ✅

---

**(ii) Internal Rate of Return (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Set up equation.
$$
\begin{aligned}
1{,}50{,}000 \times \text{PVIFA}(IRR, 6) &= 5{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{PVIFA}(IRR, 6) &= 3.3333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Try trial rates.

| Rate | PVIFA(r, 6) | NPV |
|------|-------------|-----|
| 20% | 3.3255 | -11,675 |
| 19% | 3.4098 | +11,470 |

**Step 3:** Interpolate.
$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= 19\% + \frac{11{,}470}{11{,}470 - (-11{,}675)} \times (20\% - 19\%) \\
&= 19\% + \frac{11{,}470}{23{,}145} \times 1\% \\
&= 19\% + 0.4955\% \\
&\approx 19.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** IRR (19.50%) > Cost of Capital (12%) → **ACCEPT** ✅

---

**(iii) Profitability Index (1.5 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{I_0} = \frac{6{,}16{,}710}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.233
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** PI > 1 → **ACCEPT** ✅

**Final Advice:** All three DCF methods recommend acceptance. **Project is financially viable.**

---

### **🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Conventional Methods & Project Comparison

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Payback Period method** and **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) method** of capital budgeting. Discuss their **merits and demerits**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company is considering two mutually exclusive projects with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is **10%** and the target payback is **3 years**. Evaluate using **NPV and IRR** and recommend the best project. **(7 Marks)**

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|------|---------------|---------------|
| Initial Investment | 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 80,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 1,80,000 |
| 4 | 1,80,000 | 2,50,000 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 3,00,000 |

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a) Conventional Methods (7 Marks)

**Payback Period Method (3.5 Marks):**

**Definition:** The Payback Period is the time required for a project's cumulative cash inflows to equal the initial investment.

**Formula (uniform CF):** PBP = I₀ / Annual CF

**Formula (uneven CF):**
$$\text{PBP} = A + \frac{\text{Unrecovered at start of year A}}{CF \text{ in year A}}$$

**Merits (2):**
1. Simple and easy to understand.
2. Useful for projects with liquidity concerns.
3. Favors early cash recovery — reduces risk.

**Demerits (1.5):**
1. Ignores time value of money.
2. Ignores cash flows after payback.
3. Biased against long-term, value-creating projects.
4. Subjective target setting.

---

**ARR Method (3.5 Marks):**

**Definition:** ARR is the average annual accounting profit expressed as a percentage of the average investment.

**Formula:**
$$\text{ARR} = \frac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$$

**Merits (2):**
1. Uses accounting data (easy to obtain).
2. Considers the entire project life.
3. Useful for comparing profitability.

**Demerits (1.5):**
1. Ignores time value of money.
2. Based on accounting profit, not cash flow.
3. No objective benchmark rate.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b) Numerical Comparison (7 Marks)

**Project A — NPV Calculation (2 Marks):**

| Year | CF | PVF@10% | PV | Cumulative PV |
|------|----|---------|----|---------------|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 0.9091 | 1,36,365 | 1,36,365 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 0.8264 | 1,48,752 | 2,85,117 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 0.7513 | 1,50,260 | 4,35,377 |
| 4 | 1,80,000 | 0.6830 | 1,22,940 | 5,58,317 |
| 5 | 1,50,000 | 0.6209 | 93,135 | 6,51,452 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= 6{,}51{,}452 - 5{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}51{,}452
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project A — IRR (0.5 Mark):**
Try 20%: PVIFA = 2.9906 → NPV = -54,700
Try 18%: PVIFA = 3.1272 → NPV = -36,400
Try 17%: PVIFA = 3.1992 → NPV = -3,963
Try 16%: PVIFA = 3.2743 → NPV = +37,143

Linear interpolation between 16% and 17%:
$$
IRR_A = 16\% + \frac{37{,}143}{37{,}143 + 3{,}963} \times 1\% \approx 16.90\%
$$

---

**Project B — NPV Calculation (2 Marks):**

| Year | CF | PVF@10% | PV | Cumulative PV |
|------|----|---------|----|---------------|
| 1 | 80,000 | 0.9091 | 72,728 | 72,728 |
| 2 | 1,20,000 | 0.8264 | 99,168 | 1,71,896 |
| 3 | 1,80,000 | 0.7513 | 1,35,234 | 3,07,130 |
| 4 | 2,50,000 | 0.6830 | 1,70,750 | 4,77,880 |
| 5 | 3,00,000 | 0.6209 | 1,86,270 | 6,64,150 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= 6{,}64{,}150 - 5{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}64{,}150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project B — IRR (0.5 Mark):**
Try 18%: NPV = -9,400
Try 17%: NPV = +5,250

$$
IRR_B = 17\% + \frac{5{,}250}{5{,}250 + 9{,}400} \times 1\% \approx 17.36\%
$$

---

**Project A — Payback (1 Mark):**
| Year | CF | Cumulative |
|------|----|-----------|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 3,30,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 5,30,000 ← Exceeds 5,00,000 |

$$
\text{PBP}_A = 2 + \frac{3{,}30{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ (already)}}{\ldots}
$$

Recompute: At end of Year 2, ₹3,30,000 recovered. Need ₹1,70,000 more from Year 3 CF of ₹2,00,000.
$$
\text{PBP}_A = 2 + \frac{1{,}70{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.85 \text{ years}
$$

**Project B — Payback (0.5 Mark):**
At end of Year 3, recovered ₹3,80,000. Need ₹1,20,000 more from Year 4 CF of ₹2,50,000.
$$
\text{PBP}_B = 3 + \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 3.48 \text{ years}
$$

---

**Final Recommendation (0.5 Mark):**

| Criterion | Project A | Project B | Winner |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| NPV | ₹1,51,452 | ₹1,64,150 | **Project B** |
| IRR | 16.90% | 17.36% | **Project B** |
| Payback | 2.85 yrs ✓ | 3.48 yrs ✗ | Project A |

**Recommendation:** Although Project A meets the target payback, **Project B is recommended** because:
- It has higher **NPV** (more wealth created).
- It has higher **IRR** (better rate of return).
- Payback rejection for B is marginal (0.48 years over target).

> [!WARNING] 
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **NPV must be calculated using PVF tables OR the formula** — do not use approximate factors. **Wrong PVF = 0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 2. **Always show the cumulative column** in payback — examiners explicitly check for the "interpolation year."
> 3. **IRR interpolation must use the correct formula** — swapping $NPV_L$ and $NPV_H$ signs is the most common error. Remember: at $r_L$, NPV is **positive**; at $r_H$, NPV is **negative**.
> 4. **Decision conflicts** (Payback rejects but NPV accepts, or NPV and IRR rank differently) — **always resolve in favor of NPV** as it is the gold standard.
> 5. **In mutually exclusive project comparison**, NPV is preferred over IRR when rankings conflict.
> 6. **Currency & unit** — mention the final amount with proper ₹ symbol and "rupees" in the conclusion.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP] 
> **Save this checklist — these are the most-tested facts in KTU 2024 Module 4 exams.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Capital Budgeting** → Long-term investment planning for wealth maximization.
- **Payback Period** → Time to recover initial investment.
- **ARR** → Avg. annual accounting profit ÷ Avg. investment × 100.
- **NPV** → Σ PV of inflows − Initial outlay.
- **IRR** → Discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
- **PI** → PV of inflows ÷ Initial investment.

### ⚡ The "Golden Rules" (Frequently Asked in KTU)
- ✅ **Accept project** if NPV > 0.
- ✅ **Accept project** if IRR > Cost of Capital.
- ✅ **Accept project** if PI > 1.
- ✅ **Accept project** if Payback ≤ Target.
- ✅ **Accept project** if ARR ≥ Target ARR.

### 🧠 Critical Comparative Points
- **NPV is the gold standard** — preferred over IRR for mutually exclusive projects.
- **Conventional methods** ignore TVM; **DCF methods** consider TVM.
- **Payback & ARR** are **screening tools**; **NPV/IRR/PI** are **decision tools**.
- **NPV and PI** give the same accept/reject decision for independent projects.
- **IRR may have multiple values** for non-conventional cash flows (not in KTU syllabus but good to know).

### 🧮 Must-Know PV Annuity Factor Formula
$$
\text{PVIFA}(r, n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

### 🪜 IRR Interpolation Formula (Most-Tested)
$$
IRR = r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)
$$
where $NPV_L > 0$ and $NPV_H < 0$.

### 📊 Common KTU Pitfalls
1. Mixing **accounting profit** (ARR) with **cash flow** (other methods).
2. Forgetting to **subtract initial investment** in NPV.
3. **Wrong sign convention** in IRR interpolation.
4. **Ignoring the salvage value** in ARR's average investment calculation.
5. Comparing projects with **unequal lives** without adjustment.
6. **Payback comparison** between projects of different sizes (not meaningful).

### 🎓 Final Exam Strategy (Board Pattern)
- **Part A (3 marks):** Focus on definitions, lists, and short comparisons.
- **Part B (14 marks):** Always solve numerical questions using a **tabular format** — examiners reward clarity. Show **3 steps minimum**: Setup → Calculation → Decision.
- **Always write the decision statement** at the end of every numerical sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Capital Budgeting Decision Criteria** refer to the systematic set of quantitative and qualitative rules, techniques, and benchmarks employed by a firm's financial management to evaluate, rank, and select among competing long-term investment proposals (capital projects). These criteria transform forecasted incremental cash flows into a single, comparable decision metric, allowing the firm to allocate scarce financial resources to projects that maximize shareholder wealth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *Capital Budgeting Decision Criteria are the standardized evaluative yardsticks — such as Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Profitability Index (PI), Payback Period, Discounted Payback Period, and Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) — used to accept or reject capital projects based on the time value of money and the firm's cost of capital.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Well" Story

Imagine a village headman, *Krishnan*, who has ₹10,00,000 saved. Three farmers approach him for investment:

1. **Farmer A** wants to dig a deep well — guarantees water for 10 years, returning ₹1,50,000 every year.
2. **Farmer B** wants to buy a tractor — returns ₹1,20,000/year for 8 years, but the tractor wears out fast.
3. **Farmer C** wants to set up a rice mill — returns ₹2,00,000/year for 12 years, but the initial cost is ₹15,00,000.

Krishnan cannot finance all three with just ₹10,00,000. So he needs **decision criteria** — rules to rank these projects. Should he pick the one that returns money fastest? The one that gives the biggest total cash? The one that gives the best "bang per rupee"? Each criterion answers a *different* question. Capital budgeting is exactly this — the firm's money is the village fund, and the decision criteria are the tools Krishnan uses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of an Ideal Decision Criterion (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. It must consider **all** cash flows of the project.
> 2. It must respect the **time value of money (TVM)**.
> 3. It must be consistent with the firm's overarching goal of **shareholder wealth maximization (SWM)**.

### 1.3 Classification of Decision Criteria

| **Category** | **Criteria** | **TVM Considered?** | **Primary Question Answered** |
|--------------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Discounted (Time-Value Based)** | Net Present Value (NPV) | ✅ Yes | How much value is created? |
| **Discounted (Time-Value Based)** | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | ✅ Yes | What is the project's break-even return? |
| **Discounted (Time-Value Based)** | Profitability Index (PI) | ✅ Yes | How much value per rupee invested? |
| **Non-Discounted (Traditional)** | Payback Period (PBP) | ❌ No | How fast is the money recovered? |
| **Non-Discounted (Traditional)** | Discounted Payback Period (DPBP) | ✅ Partially | How fast on a discounted basis? |
| **Non-Discounted (Traditional)** | Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) | ❌ No | What is the accounting profit percentage? |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** The KTU valuation key typically awards full marks only to **discounted criteria** (NPV, IRR, PI) because they consider the time value of money. Always state TVM compliance explicitly in your answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Net Present Value (NPV) — The Gold Standard

NPV is the **present value** of all expected future cash flows (inflows minus outflows), discounted at the firm's cost of capital, minus the initial investment.

**Why NPV is the "Gold Standard":**
- Considers **all** cash flows over the project's life.
- Directly measures **dollar value creation** in today's money.
- Aligns perfectly with the goal of **shareholder wealth maximization**.
- A positive NPV project increases the firm's market value by exactly that amount.

**Decision Rule:**
- **NPV > 0** → Accept (project earns more than cost of capital).
- **NPV = 0** → Indifferent (project earns exactly the cost of capital).
- **NPV < 0** → Reject (project destroys value).

### 2.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — The Break-Even Return

IRR is the discount rate that makes the **NPV of a project equal to zero**. In simple words, it is the project's own earning rate.

**Decision Rule:**
- **IRR > Cost of Capital (k)** → Accept.
- **IRR = k** → Indifferent.
- **IRR < k** → Reject.

### 2.3 Profitability Index (PI) — The "Bang per Rupee" Metric

PI measures the **present value of future cash flows per rupee of initial investment**. It is especially useful in **capital rationing** situations.

**Decision Rule:**
- **PI > 1** → Accept.
- **PI < 1** → Reject.

### 2.4 Payback Period (PBP) — The "How Soon" Metric

The time required for the cumulative cash inflows to recover the original investment. Shorter payback = less risk.

**Decision Rule:**
- If PBP < Target/Standard Period → Accept.
- If PBP > Target/Standard Period → Reject.

### 2.5 Discounted Payback Period (DPBP) — PBP with TVM

Same as PBP, but uses **discounted** cash flows. It answers: *"How long until we recover the investment in present-value terms?"*

### 2.6 Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) — The "Book-Value" Metric

ARR uses **accounting profits** (not cash flows) and **book value of investment** (not market value). It ignores TVM.

**Decision Rule:**
- If ARR > Target/Required Rate → Accept.
- If ARR < Target/Required Rate → Reject.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Criterion** | **Formula** | **Decision Rule** | **Units** | **Discounts Cash Flow?** |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| **NPV** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t} - C_0$ | $NPV \geq 0$ Accept | Currency (₹) | ✅ Yes |
| **IRR** | $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = C_0$ | $IRR \geq k$ Accept | Percentage (%) | ✅ Yes |
| **PI** | $PI = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}}{C_0}$ | $PI \geq 1$ Accept | Ratio (unitless) | ✅ Yes |
| **PBP** | $PBP = \text{Years before full recovery} + \dfrac{\text{Unrecovered cost at start of year}}{\text{Cash flow during the year}}$ | $PBP \leq \text{Target}$ | Years | ❌ No |
| **DPBP** | Same as PBP, but using $\dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$ | $DPBP \leq \text{Target}$ | Years | ✅ Yes |
| **ARR** | $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Accounting Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$ | $ARR \geq \text{Target Rate}$ | Percentage (%) | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used in KTU Problems:**
> - $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year $t$
> - $C_0$ = Initial Investment (cash outflow at $t = 0$)
> - $k$ = Cost of Capital / Discount Rate / Required Rate of Return
> - $n$ = Project Life (in years)
> - $PVIFA(k, n)$ = Present Value Interest Factor of Annuity

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering project finance (e.g., setting up a manufacturing plant, R&D for a new semiconductor line, building a hydroelectric dam), the choice of decision criterion is **not academic** — it determines whether the firm invests in the right technology.

- **NPV** is used by Tata Steel for plant expansion decisions.
- **IRR** is reported in feasibility reports because it is intuitive ("the project yields 18%").
- **PBP** is preferred by small manufacturers and startups with liquidity crunch.
- **PI** is used in **capital rationing** (e.g., PSUs with a fixed annual capex budget).
- **ARR** appears in performance scorecards for divisional managers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Computing All Six Criteria

**KTU-Style Problem Statement:**

> A firm is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of **₹5,00,000**. The project is expected to generate net cash inflows of **₹1,50,000 per year for 5 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 5 is **₹50,000**. The firm's cost of capital is **10%**. The target payback period is **3 years**, and the required ARR is **20%**.
>
> Compute: **(a) NPV, (b) IRR, (c) PI, (d) PBP, (e) DPBP, (f) ARR**, and recommend whether to accept the project.

**Given Data:**
- $C_0 = 5,00,000$
- $CF_1 = CF_2 = CF_3 = CF_4 = CF_5 = 1,50,000$
- $SV_5 = 50,000$ (added to year 5 cash flow)
- $k = 10\% = 0.10$
- Target PBP = 3 years; Required ARR = 20%

---

### 3.2 (a) Net Present Value (NPV) — Exhaustive Computation

The cash flow in year 5 becomes $1,50,000 + 50,000 = 2,00,000$.

We need the PV of all cash inflows and subtract the initial cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= \left[ \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.10^1} + \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.10^2} + \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.10^3} + \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.10^4} + \dfrac{2,00,000}{1.10^5} \right] - 5,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Compute each discount factor and PV.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.1000} = 1,36,363.64 \\
PV_2 &= \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.2100} = 1,23,966.94 \\
PV_3 &= \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.3310} = 1,12,697.22 \\
PV_4 &= \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.4641} = 1,02,452.02 \\
PV_5 &= \dfrac{2,00,000}{1.6105} = 1,24,184.42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum all present values (using PVIFA shortcut is shown below).**

Method A — Direct Sum:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum PV &= 1,36,363.64 + 1,23,966.94 + 1,12,697.22 + 1,02,452.02 + 1,24,184.42 \\
&= 5,99,664.24
\end{aligned}
$$

Method B — Using PVIFA Annuity Factor (4 years only) + Spot PV of Year 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIFA(10\%, 5) &= \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} = \dfrac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = 3.7908 \\
PV_{CF(1 \to 5)} &= 1,50,000 \times 3.7908 = 5,68,620 \\
\text{Extra salvage PV} &= 50,000 \times (1.10)^{-5} = 50,000 \times 0.6209 = 31,045 \\
\sum PV &= 5,68,620 + 31,045 = 5,99,665
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract initial investment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= 5,99,665 - 5,00,000 = 99,665
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $NPV = +₹99,665 > 0$ → **ACCEPT** the project.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the PV table *and* the shortcut using PVIFA. Examiners give bonus marks for showing both methods, but using *only* the shortcut without any explanation is penalized.

---

### 3.3 (b) Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Interpolation Method

Since IRR is the rate at which $NPV = 0$, we use **trial-and-error** + **linear interpolation**.

**Trial 1: At k = 12%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{12\%} &= 1,50,000 \times PVIFA(12\%, 5) + 50,000 \times PVIF(12\%, 5) - 5,00,000 \\
PVIFA(12\%, 5) &= \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12} = 3.6048 \\
PVIF(12\%, 5) &= (1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674 \\
NPV_{12\%} &= 1,50,000 \times 3.6048 + 50,000 \times 0.5674 - 5,00,000 \\
&= 5,40,720 + 28,370 - 5,00,000 = 69,090
\end{aligned}
$$

Still positive, so try a higher rate.

**Trial 2: At k = 15%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIFA(15\%, 5) &= \dfrac{1 - (1.15)^{-5}}{0.15} = 3.3522 \\
PVIF(15\%, 5) &= (1.15)^{-5} = 0.4972 \\
NPV_{15\%} &= 1,50,000 \times 3.3522 + 50,000 \times 0.4972 - 5,00,000 \\
&= 5,02,830 + 24,860 - 5,00,000 = 27,690
\end{aligned}
$$

Still positive, so try higher.

**Trial 3: At k = 18%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIFA(18\%, 5) &= \dfrac{1 - (1.18)^{-5}}{0.18} = 3.1272 \\
PVIF(18\%, 5) &= (1.18)^{-5} = 0.4371 \\
NPV_{18\%} &= 1,50,000 \times 3.1272 + 50,000 \times 0.4371 - 5,00,000 \\
&= 4,69,080 + 21,855 - 5,00,000 = -9,065
\end{aligned}
$$

Now NPV is negative. We bracket IRR between **15% and 18%**.

**Step 4: Apply Linear Interpolation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= LR + \dfrac{NPV_{LR}}{NPV_{LR} - NPV_{HR}} \times (HR - LR) \\
IRR &= 15\% + \dfrac{27,690}{27,690 - (-9,065)} \times (18\% - 15\%) \\
IRR &= 15\% + \dfrac{27,690}{36,755} \times 3\% \\
IRR &= 15\% + 0.7534 \times 3\% \\
IRR &= 15\% + 2.26\% \\
IRR &= 17.26\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $IRR = 17.26\% > k = 10\%$ → **ACCEPT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting to add the salvage value's PV to the annuity PV. The salvage value is a *separate* terminal cash flow at year $n$, not part of the annuity. Always show both terms clearly.

---

### 3.4 (c) Profitability Index (PI) — Direct Computation

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \dfrac{\sum PV \text{ of inflows}}{C_0} = \dfrac{5,99,665}{5,00,000} = 1.1993
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $PI = 1.20 > 1$ → **ACCEPT**.

**Alternate formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= 1 + \dfrac{NPV}{C_0} = 1 + \dfrac{99,665}{5,00,000} = 1.1993
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 (d) Payback Period (PBP) — Cumulative Cash Flow Table

| **Year** | **Cash Inflow (₹)** | **Cumulative Inflow (₹)** | **Unrecovered Balance (₹)** |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 1,50,000 | 5,00,000 − 1,50,000 = **3,50,000** |
| 2 | 1,50,000 | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 − 3,00,000 = **2,00,000** |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 4,50,000 | 5,00,000 − 4,50,000 = **50,000** |
| 4 | 1,50,000 | 6,00,000 | Fully recovered ✅ |
| 5 | 2,00,000 | 8,00,000 | — |

**Step 1: Find the year in which full recovery occurs.**
- End of Year 3 → ₹4,50,000 (still short of ₹5,00,000 by ₹50,000).
- End of Year 4 → ₹6,00,000 (fully recovered).

So payback occurs **during Year 4**.

**Step 2: Apply the fractional formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PBP &= 3 + \dfrac{50,000}{1,50,000} = 3 + 0.3333 = 3.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $PBP = 3.33$ years > Target = 3 years → **REJECT** by PBP rule (note the conflict with NPV/IRR!).

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The PBP rule rejects a project with positive NPV! This is the classic **PBP vs NPV conflict**. The reason: PBP ignores cash flows *after* payback and ignores TVM. Always flag this conflict in your answer.

---

### 3.6 (e) Discounted Payback Period (DPBP) — Using Discounted Inflows

Using the PVs we computed earlier:

| **Year** | **Discounted CF (₹)** | **Cumulative Discounted CF (₹)** |
|----------|------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | 1,36,363.64 | 1,36,363.64 |
| 2 | 1,23,966.94 | 2,60,330.58 |
| 3 | 1,12,697.22 | 3,73,027.80 |
| 4 | 1,02,452.02 | 4,75,479.82 |
| 5 | 1,24,184.42 | 5,99,664.24 |

The investment of ₹5,00,000 is recovered **between Year 4 and Year 5**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPBP &= 4 + \dfrac{5,00,000 - 4,75,479.82}{1,24,184.42} \\
&= 4 + \dfrac{24,520.18}{1,24,184.42} \\
&= 4 + 0.1975 \\
&= 4.20 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $DPBP = 4.20$ years > 3 years → **REJECT** by DPBP rule.

---

### 3.7 (f) Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) — Book-Value Method

**Step 1: Compute depreciation (assuming straight-line, ignoring salvage).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation} &= \dfrac{C_0 - SV}{n} = \dfrac{5,00,000 - 50,000}{5} = \dfrac{4,50,000}{5} = 90,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Average Annual Accounting Profit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Profit} &= \text{Average Cash Inflow} - \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 1,50,000 - 90,000 = 60,000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Average Investment.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Investment} &= \dfrac{C_0 + SV}{2} = \dfrac{5,00,000 + 50,000}{2} = 2,75,000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute ARR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ARR &= \dfrac{60,000}{2,75,000} \times 100 = 21.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** $ARR = 21.82\% > \text{Required } 20\%$ → **ACCEPT**.

> [!TIP]
> **Two ARR Definitions in KTU Papers:**
> 1. **On Original Investment:** $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Profit}}{C_0} \times 100$
> 2. **On Average Investment:** $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Profit}}{(C_0 + SV)/2} \times 100$
> Always read the question carefully to identify which is asked. The "average investment" version is more common in KTU 2024 scheme papers.

---

### 3.8 Final Decision Matrix

| **Criterion** | **Computed Value** | **Required / Target** | **Decision** |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| NPV | +₹99,665 | ≥ 0 | ✅ Accept |
| IRR | 17.26% | ≥ 10% (k) | ✅ Accept |
| PI | 1.20 | ≥ 1 | ✅ Accept |
| PBP | 3.33 yrs | ≤ 3 yrs | ❌ Reject |
| DPBP | 4.20 yrs | ≤ 3 yrs | ❌ Reject |
| ARR | 21.82% | ≥ 20% | ✅ Accept |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Recommendation:** Since 4 out of 6 criteria (including all discounted ones: NPV, IRR, PI) recommend acceptance, and the discounted criteria are theoretically superior, **ACCEPT the project**. The non-discounted criteria (PBP, DPBP) penalize the project for not recovering capital fast enough, but they ignore the substantial post-payback cash flows and the time value of money.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Capital Budgeting Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Capital Budgeting Decision Problem"] --> B{"Discounted or Non-Discounted?"}

    B -- Discounted TVM Yes --> C["Discounted Criteria"]
    B -- Non Discounted TVM No --> D["Traditional Criteria"]

    C --> E["NPV Net Present Value"]
    C --> F["IRR Internal Rate of Return"]
    C --> G["PI Profitability Index"]
    C --> H["DPBP Discounted Payback"]

    D --> I["PBP Simple Payback Period"]
    D --> J["ARR Accounting Rate of Return"]

    E --> K{"NPV greater than 0"}
    F --> L{"IRR greater than k"}
    G --> M{"PI greater than 1"}
    H --> N{"DPBP within target"}
    I --> O{"PBP within target"}
    J --> P{"ARR greater than required rate"}

    K -- Yes --> Q["ACCEPT PROJECT"]
    K -- No --> R["REJECT PROJECT"]
    L -- Yes --> Q
    L -- No --> R
    M -- Yes --> Q
    M -- No --> R
    N -- Yes --> Q
    N -- No --> R
    O -- Yes --> Q
    O -- No --> R
    P -- Yes --> Q
    P -- No --> R

    Q --> S["Final Selection Based on NPV Ranking"]
    S --> T["Project Maximizing Shareholder Wealth Selected"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style Q fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#4169E1,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — NPV vs IRR Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Project A Cash Flows"] --> B["Compute NPV at k"]
    A --> C["Compute IRR by interpolation"]
    B --> D["NPV greater than 0"]
    C --> E["IRR greater than k"]
    D -- Both True --> F["Both Criteria Agree Accept"]
    D -- Conflict --> G["NPV and IRR Conflict"]
    E -- Conflict --> G
    G --> H["Reasons Size Difference Timing Difference"]
    H --> I["Prefer NPV Superior Criterion"]
    I --> J["Accept or Reject based on NPV"]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capital Rationing with PI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Multiple Independent Projects"] --> B["Compute PI for Each Project"]
    B --> C["Rank by PI in Descending Order"]
    C --> D{"Available Capital Budget Sufficient?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Select All Projects with PI greater than 1"]
    D -- No --> F["Capital Rationing Applies"]
    F --> G["Select Projects in PI Order until Budget Exhausted"]
    G --> H["Maximize Total NPV Subject to Budget Constraint"]
    H --> I["Final Project Portfolio Selected"]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#4169E1,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set 2]**

**Q1. (a)** *"What is the Net Present Value (NPV) of a capital budgeting project? State the decision rule."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Net Present Value (NPV) is the difference between the present value of cash inflows generated by a project and the present value of its cash outflows (including the initial investment), discounted at the firm's cost of capital $k$.

**Formula:**

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t} - C_0
$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If $NPV > 0$ → **Accept** the project (value created).
- If $NPV = 0$ → **Indifferent** (project yields exactly the cost of capital).
- If $NPV < 0$ → **Reject** the project (value destroyed).

NPV is considered the most reliable capital budgeting criterion because it directly measures the dollar value addition to the firm and aligns with the goal of shareholder wealth maximization.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q1. (b)** *"Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR)."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis of Difference** | **NPV** | **IRR** |
|--------------------------|---------|---------|
| **Meaning** | Difference between PV of inflows and PV of outflows at cost of capital | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| **Unit of Measurement** | Currency (e.g., ₹) | Percentage (%) |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | Cash flows reinvested at cost of capital $k$ | Cash flows reinvested at IRR (often unrealistic) |
| **Decision Rule** | $NPV \geq 0$ Accept | $IRR \geq k$ Accept |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Preferred (theoretically superior) | May be misleading in mutually exclusive projects |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Modified from Dec 2022]**

A manufacturing company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, **Project X** and **Project Y**. Both require an initial investment of **₹10,00,000** and have a life of **4 years**. The cost of capital is **12%**. The expected cash inflows are:

| **Year** | **Project X (₹)** | **Project Y (₹)** |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 2 | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 4 | 6,00,000 | 2,00,000 |

**Required:**
- **(a)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of both projects. **[7 marks]**
- **(b)** Compute the **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** of both projects using the interpolation method and advise on which project to select. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — NPV Computation [7 marks]**

**Discount factor table at k = 12%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PVIF(12\%, 1) &= 0.8929 \\
PVIF(12\%, 2) &= 0.7972 \\
PVIF(12\%, 3) &= 0.7118 \\
PVIF(12\%, 4) &= 0.6355
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project X — NPV Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= 3,00,000(0.8929) + 4,00,000(0.7972) + 5,00,000(0.7118) + 6,00,000(0.6355) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 2,67,870 + 3,18,880 + 3,55,900 + 3,81,300 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 13,23,950 - 10,00,000 \\
&= +₹3,23,950
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Y — NPV Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= 5,00,000(0.8929) + 4,00,000(0.7972) + 3,00,000(0.7118) + 2,00,000(0.6355) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 4,46,450 + 3,18,880 + 2,13,540 + 1,27,100 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 11,05,970 - 10,00,000 \\
&= +₹1,05,970
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary:**

| Project | NPV (₹) | Decision |
|---------|---------|----------|
| X | 3,23,950 | Accept |
| Y | 1,05,970 | Accept |

Both have positive NPVs.

---

#### **Part (b) — IRR Computation [7 marks]**

**Project X — IRR by Interpolation:**

Try $k = 20\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X(20\%) &= 3,00,000(0.8333) + 4,00,000(0.6944) + 5,00,000(0.5787) + 6,00,000(0.4823) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 2,49,990 + 2,77,760 + 2,89,350 + 2,89,380 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 11,06,480 - 10,00,000 = +1,06,480
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $k = 25\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X(25\%) &= 3,00,000(0.8000) + 4,00,000(0.6400) + 5,00,000(0.5120) + 6,00,000(0.4096) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 2,40,000 + 2,56,000 + 2,56,000 + 2,45,760 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 9,97,760 - 10,00,000 = -2,240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpolate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR_X &= 20\% + \dfrac{1,06,480}{1,06,480 + 2,240} \times 5\% \\
&= 20\% + \dfrac{1,06,480}{1,08,720} \times 5\% \\
&= 20\% + 0.9794 \times 5\% \\
&= 20\% + 4.90\% = 24.90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Y — IRR by Interpolation:**

Try $k = 18\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y(18\%) &= 5,00,000(0.8475) + 4,00,000(0.7182) + 3,00,000(0.6086) + 2,00,000(0.5158) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 4,23,750 + 2,87,280 + 1,82,580 + 1,03,160 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 9,96,770 - 10,00,000 = -3,230
\end{aligned}
$$

Try $k = 17\%$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y(17\%) &= 5,00,000(0.8547) + 4,00,000(0.7305) + 3,00,000(0.6244) + 2,00,000(0.5337) - 10,00,000 \\
&= 4,27,350 + 2,92,200 + 1,87,320 + 1,06,740 - 10,00,000 \\
&= 10,13,610 - 10,00,000 = +13,610
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpolate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR_Y &= 17\% + \dfrac{13,610}{13,610 + 3,230} \times 1\% \\
&= 17\% + \dfrac{13,610}{16,840} \times 1\% \\
&= 17\% + 0.8082\% = 17.81\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison & Recommendation:**

| Project | NPV (₹) | IRR (%) |
|---------|---------|---------|
| X | **3,23,950** | 24.90 |
| Y | 1,05,970 | 17.81 |

**Recommendation:** Select **Project X** because it has a **higher NPV** (₹3,23,950 vs ₹1,05,970). When NPV and IRR give conflicting ranks in mutually exclusive projects, **NPV is the superior criterion** because it directly measures wealth creation and uses a more realistic reinvestment assumption (cost of capital).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Modified from May 2023]**

A company is evaluating a new project requiring an initial investment of **₹8,00,000**. The project will generate annual cash inflows of **₹2,50,000 for 5 years** with no salvage value. The firm's cost of capital is **14%**.

**Required:**
- **(a)** Calculate the **Payback Period**, **Discounted Payback Period**, and **Accounting Rate of Return (on average investment)** assuming depreciation is charged on a straight-line basis. **[7 marks]**
- **(b)** Calculate the **Profitability Index (PI)** and **Net Present Value (NPV)**. Advise whether the project should be accepted. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — PBP, DPBP, ARR [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Payback Period.**

Cumulative cash flow table:

| **Year** | **Cash Inflow (₹)** | **Cumulative (₹)** |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 2,50,000 | 2,50,000 |
| 2 | 2,50,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 3 | 2,50,000 | 7,50,000 |
| 4 | 2,50,000 | 10,00,000 |

Recovery occurs during Year 4. Unrecovered balance at start of Year 4 = ₹8,00,000 − ₹7,50,000 = ₹50,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PBP &= 3 + \dfrac{50,000}{2,50,000} = 3 + 0.20 = \mathbf{3.20 \text{ years}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Discounted Payback Period.**

Discount factors at 14%:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 2,50,000 \times 0.8772 = 2,19,300 \\
PV_2 &= 2,50,000 \times 0.7695 = 1,92,375 \\
PV_3 &= 2,50,000 \times 0.6750 = 1,68,750 \\
PV_4 &= 2,50,000 \times 0.5921 = 1,48,025 \\
PV_5 &= 2,50,000 \times 0.5194 = 1,29,850
\end{aligned}
$$

Cumulative discounted cash flow:

| **Year** | **Discounted CF (₹)** | **Cumulative (₹)** |
|----------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 2,19,300 | 2,19,300 |
| 2 | 1,92,375 | 4,11,675 |
| 3 | 1,68,750 | 5,80,425 |
| 4 | 1,48,025 | 7,28,450 |
| 5 | 1,29,850 | 8,58,300 |

Recovery occurs during Year 5. Unrecovered balance at start of Year 5 = ₹8,00,000 − ₹7,28,450 = ₹71,550.

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPBP &= 4 + \dfrac{71,550}{1,29,850} = 4 + 0.5510 = \mathbf{4.55 \text{ years}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Accounting Rate of Return.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depreciation} &= \dfrac{8,00,000 - 0}{5} = 1,60,000 \text{ per year} \\
\text{Average Profit} &= 2,50,000 - 1,60,000 = 90,000 \text{ per year} \\
\text{Average Investment} &= \dfrac{8,00,000 + 0}{2} = 4,00,000 \\
ARR &= \dfrac{90,000}{4,00,000} \times 100 = \mathbf{22.50\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Part (b) — PI, NPV, Recommendation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Profitability Index.**

Total PV of inflows = ₹8,58,300 (from DPBP table above).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \dfrac{8,58,300}{8,00,000} = \mathbf{1.073}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Net Present Value.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= 8,58,300 - 8,00,000 = \mathbf{+₹58,300}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Recommendation.**

| Criterion | Value | Decision |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| NPV | +₹58,300 | ✅ Accept (NPV > 0) |
| PI | 1.073 | ✅ Accept (PI > 1) |
| PBP | 3.20 years | Decision depends on target |
| DPBP | 4.55 years | Decision depends on target |
| ARR | 22.50% | Decision depends on target rate |

Since NPV > 0 and PI > 1, and these are the **theoretically superior discounted criteria**, the **project should be ACCEPTED**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to subtract the initial investment in NPV, or treating it as a positive value. Always write: *PV of inflows − C₀*.
> 2. **Salvage Value Omission (–1 to –2 marks):** Failing to add the salvage value to the final year's cash flow before discounting. The salvage value is a separate terminal cash flow.
> 3. **Interpolation Direction Confusion (–1 mark):** In IRR interpolation, the *lower rate (LR)* corresponds to the *higher NPV (positive)*, and the *higher rate (HR)* to the *lower NPV (negative)*. Reversing these gives a wrong IRR.
> 4. **PBP Formula Misuse (–1 mark):** Writing the PBP as the year when cumulative ≥ C₀ is correct, but you *must* show the fractional formula. Simply stating "3 years" without the decimal part loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring TVM in PBP/ARR (–1 mark):** When asked to compare criteria, always mention that PBP and ARR ignore time value of money, making them inferior to NPV/IRR/PI.
> 6. **PI vs NPV Conflict in Capital Rationing (–2 marks):** PI can rank projects differently from NPV when projects have *different scales*. Always state: *"PI is used in capital rationing to rank projects; NPV is used to choose among mutually exclusive projects."*
> 7. **IRR Reinvestment Assumption (–1 mark):** Always mention that NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital, while IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR — the latter is unrealistic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Criteria in Capital Budgeting**

- ✅ **Six Criteria to Remember:** NPV, IRR, PI, PBP, DPBP, ARR.
- ✅ **Three "Best" Criteria (Discounted):** NPV, IRR, PI — they respect TVM.
- ✅ **NPV is the Gold Standard:** Maximizes shareholder wealth; ranks mutually exclusive projects correctly.
- ✅ **IRR** is intuitive but can give multiple answers (non-conventional cash flows) and ranks mutually exclusive projects incorrectly.
- ✅ **PI = 1 + (NPV / C₀):** Use this shortcut to verify PI from NPV.
- ✅ **PI > NPV** in capital rationing situations when project sizes differ.
- ✅ **PBP & ARR** are *traditional* criteria — they ignore TVM and are merely indicators of risk/liquidity.
- ✅ **DPBP** uses discounted cash flows but still ignores post-payback cash flows.
- ✅ **Linear Interpolation Formula:**

$$
IRR = LR + \left[ \dfrac{NPV_{LR}}{NPV_{LR} - NPV_{HR}} \right] \times (HR - LR)
$$

- ✅ **NPV vs IRR Conflict** occurs due to *differences in project size*, *timing of cash flows*, or *duration*. Resolution: **Always prefer NPV.**
- ✅ **Capital Rationing:** Use **PI ranking** when the budget is constrained and projects are independent.
- ✅ **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Use **NPV** (never IRR or PI alone).
- ✅ **Acceptance Rule Quick Recall:**
  - NPV ≥ 0 → Accept
  - IRR ≥ k → Accept
  - PI ≥ 1 → Accept
  - PBP ≤ Target → Accept
  - DPBP ≤ Target → Accept
  - ARR ≥ Required Rate → Accept
- ✅ **Depreciation Formula (Straight Line):** $(C_0 - SV) / n$
- ✅ **Average Investment:** $(C_0 + SV) / 2$
- ✅ **PVIFA Shortcut:** For equal annual cash flows, multiply the annual CF by PVIFA(k, n) instead of discounting year-by-year — but show the discount factor table for partial marks.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Hall Mantra:** *"If a question asks which is the 'best' or 'most appropriate' criterion, the answer is always NPV."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Comparisons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET743 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Comparisons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Net Present Value (NPV) — Formal KTU Definition

**Net Present Value (NPV)** is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows over a period of time. It is the most rigorous discounted cash flow (DCF) technique used in capital budgeting to determine the absolute profitability of a project in monetary terms.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

where $CF_t$ is the cash flow at time $t$, $r$ is the discount rate (cost of capital), and $n$ is the project's life.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Decision Rule (NPV):**
> - **Accept** the project if NPV > 0
> - **Reject** the project if NPV < 0
> - **Indifferent** if NPV = 0
> A positive NPV means the project earns more than the required rate of return and adds value to the firm.

### 1.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Formal KTU Definition

**Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate that equates the present value of expected future cash inflows to the initial investment outlay, making the Net Present Value of the project equal to zero. It represents the project’s expected percentage rate of return.

$$NPV = 0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Decision Rule (IRR):**
> - **Accept** the project if IRR > Cost of Capital (hurdle rate)
> - **Reject** the project if IRR < Cost of Capital
> - **Indifferent** if IRR = Cost of Capital

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two friends, **Ravi** and **Anita**, each investing **₹1,00,000** in different businesses:

- **Ravi** wants to know: *“After accounting for the time value of money, exactly how much wealth (in rupees) does my business add to me?”* → This is the **NPV** mindset (absolute rupee gain).
- **Anita** wants to know: *“What is the percentage return my business is giving me per year?”* → This is the **IRR** mindset (relative % return).

If both invest the same amount, NPV tells you the *size of the pie*, and IRR tells you *how fast the pie is growing*. The **comparison** between NPV and IRR is like asking: *Should I judge an investment by the absolute wealth created (NPV) or by the rate at which it grows (IRR)?*

### 1.4 Core Metrics at a Glance

- **Hurdle Rate (Cost of Capital, $r$):** The minimum acceptable rate of return. Standard benchmark: **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** = **10% – 15%** in most KTU numerical problems.
- **Crossover Rate:** The discount rate at which two projects have **equal NPVs**.
- **Reinvestment Rate Assumption:** NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital; IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself.

> [!NOTE] **Why NPV vs IRR Comparison Matters:**
> For **independent projects**, both NPV and IRR give identical accept/reject decisions. However, for **mutually exclusive projects** (where only one can be chosen), they may give *conflicting rankings*, leading to the need for **Incremental IRR Analysis** and the **NPV Profile** method.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of NPV

The NPV method rests on three core financial principles:
1. **Time Value of Money (TVM):** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow.
2. **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Technique:** All future cash flows are discounted back to the present using a risk-adjusted rate.
3. **Wealth Maximization Objective:** A project is accepted only if it increases shareholders’ wealth, which NPV directly measures.

**Logic of NPV Calculation (Step-by-Step):**
- Identify all cash inflows and outflows over the project's life.
- Determine the appropriate discount rate (cost of capital, $r$).
- Discount each cash flow back to time zero: $PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.
- Sum all present values: $PV_{inflows} - PV_{outflows}$.
- If the result is positive, the project earns more than $r$ and creates value.

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundation of IRR

IRR is essentially a solution to the equation $NPV = 0$. The IRR is found by trial-and-error, interpolation, or financial calculator methods. It is the project's "break-even" discount rate — the rate at which the project neither creates nor destroys value.

**Key Insight:** If the IRR is plotted on a graph, it is the point where the **NPV Profile curve crosses the X-axis**.

### 2.3 Why NPV and IRR May Conflict

Although both methods are DCF-based, they can produce **conflicting rankings** for mutually exclusive projects due to:

1. **Size (Scale) Disparity:** A smaller project may have a higher IRR but a lower NPV, while a larger project has a lower IRR but a higher NPV.
2. **Timing (Cash Flow Pattern) Disparity:** A project with early cash flows may have a higher IRR but lower NPV compared to a project with delayed but larger cash flows.
3. **Reinvestment Rate Assumption:** NPV assumes reinvestment at the *cost of capital* (more realistic), while IRR assumes reinvestment at the *IRR* (often unrealistic when IRR is high).
4. **Multiple IRR Problem:** Projects with non-conventional cash flows (e.g., negative cash flows after initial positive flows) can produce multiple IRRs, making IRR unreliable. The **Modified Internal Rate of Return (MIRR)** solves this.

### 2.4 Resolution of NPV-IRR Conflict

The KTU-recommended procedure for resolving conflicts in mutually exclusive projects is:

- **Step 1:** Compute NPV of both projects at the cost of capital.
- **Step 2:** Compute IRR of both projects.
- **Step 3:** If NPV and IRR rankings differ, compute the **Incremental Cash Flows** (Project with higher investment minus Project with lower investment).
- **Step 4:** Compute the **Incremental IRR** ($IRR_{B-A}$) on the incremental cash flows.
- **Step 5:** Compare $IRR_{B-A}$ with the cost of capital:
  - If $IRR_{B-A} > r$ → Choose the larger project (Project B).
  - If $IRR_{B-A} < r$ → Choose the smaller project (Project A).
- **Conclusion:** Always **prefer the project with the higher NPV** in case of conflict (theoretically superior method).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Decision Rule** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Accept if NPV > 0 | Measures absolute value addition |
| Present Value of Cash Flow | $PV = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | — | Discounting factor |
| Internal Rate of Return | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Accept if IRR > $r$ | Rate where NPV = 0 |
| IRR by Interpolation | $IRR = r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$ | — | $r_L$ = lower rate, $r_H$ = higher rate |
| Profitability Index (PI) | $PI = \dfrac{PV \text{ of Inflows}}{Initial \text{ Investment}}$ | Accept if PI > 1 | Related to NPV |
| Crossover Rate | $NPV_A = NPV_B$ at $r = r_{cross}$ | — | Discount rate where both projects have equal NPV |
| Incremental IRR | $IRR_{B-A} : \sum \dfrac{(\Delta CF_t)}{(1+IRR_{B-A})^t} = 0$ | Choose B if $IRR_{B-A} > r$ | Used in conflict resolution |
| Modified IRR (MIRR) | $MIRR = \left(\dfrac{FV \text{ of Inflows}}{PV \text{ of Outflows}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Accept if MIRR > $r$ | Solves multiple IRR problem |
| Reinvestment Rate (NPV) | Cost of Capital ($r$) | — | Realistic assumption |
| Reinvestment Rate (IRR) | IRR itself | — | Often unrealistic |

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Utility in Engineering:**
> In infrastructure and large-scale engineering projects (e.g., a new manufacturing plant, a hydroelectric dam, or a 5G tower rollout), finance managers use NPV to decide whether the project is worth its absolute cost, and IRR to communicate the *percentage return* to stakeholders and investors. KTU examiners often frame IRR-NPV conflicts around two engineering investment proposals of differing scale and life.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: NPV Calculation

**Problem:** A company is evaluating a project requiring an initial investment of **₹5,00,000**. The project generates cash inflows of ₹1,80,000, ₹2,00,000, ₹2,20,000, and ₹1,50,000 at the end of Years 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. The cost of capital is **10%**. Calculate the NPV and advise whether to accept the project.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify the discount rate and cash flow pattern.
- Initial investment ($CF_0$) = −₹5,00,000
- $CF_1$ = ₹1,80,000
- $CF_2$ = ₹2,00,000
- $CF_3$ = ₹2,20,000
- $CF_4$ = ₹1,50,000
- Discount rate ($r$) = 10% = 0.10

**Step 2:** Compute the Present Value (PV) of each cash flow using $PV_t = \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{1,80,000}{(1.10)^1} = \frac{1,80,000}{1.10} = 1,63,636.36 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{2,00,000}{(1.10)^2} = \frac{2,00,000}{1.21} = 1,65,289.26 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{2,20,000}{(1.10)^3} = \frac{2,20,000}{1.331} = 1,65,289.26 \\
PV_4 &= \frac{1,50,000}{(1.10)^4} = \frac{1,50,000}{1.4641} = 1,02,452.09 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum all present values of inflows.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum PV_{inflows} &= 1,63,636.36 + 1,65,289.26 + 1,65,289.26 + 1,02,452.09 \\
&= 5,96,666.97
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute NPV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV &= \sum PV_{inflows} - Initial\ Investment \\
&= 5,96,666.97 - 5,00,000 \\
&= \text{Rs. } 96,666.97
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Decision.
Since NPV > 0, **accept the project** — it adds ₹96,666.97 in value to the firm.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: IRR by Interpolation Method

**Problem:** A project requires an investment of **₹1,00,000** and yields a single cash inflow of **₹1,50,000** at the end of **5 years**. Calculate the IRR.

**Step 1:** Set up the equation $NPV = 0$.

$$
\frac{1,50,000}{(1+IRR)^5} - 1,00,000 = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $(1+IRR)^5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1+IRR)^5 &= \frac{1,50,000}{1,00,000} = 1.50 \\
1+IRR &= (1.50)^{1/5} \\
IRR &= (1.50)^{0.2} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply trial and error / interpolation to find IRR.
- At $r$ = **8%**: $NPV = \dfrac{1,50,000}{(1.08)^5} - 1,00,000 = \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.4693} - 1,00,000 = 1,02,084 - 1,00,000 = +2,084$
- At $r$ = **10%**: $NPV = \dfrac{1,50,000}{(1.10)^5} - 1,00,000 = \dfrac{1,50,000}{1.6105} - 1,00,000 = 93,138 - 1,00,000 = -6,862$

**Step 4:** Apply linear interpolation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L) \\
IRR &= 8\% + \frac{2,084}{2,084 - (-6,862)} \times (10\% - 8\%) \\
IRR &= 8\% + \frac{2,084}{8,946} \times 2\% \\
IRR &= 8\% + 0.2329 \times 2\% \\
IRR &= 8\% + 0.4658\% \\
IRR &\approx 8.47\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Decision Rule.
If the cost of capital is below 8.47%, **accept**; otherwise, **reject**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: NPV vs IRR Conflict Resolution

**Problem:** A firm with a cost of capital of **10%** has two mutually exclusive projects:

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −50,000 | −1,00,000 |
| 1 | +30,000 | +40,000 |
| 2 | +30,000 | +50,000 |
| 3 | +15,000 | +60,000 |

**Step 1:** Compute NPV of each at 10%.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= -50,000 + \frac{30,000}{1.10} + \frac{30,000}{1.21} + \frac{15,000}{1.331} \\
&= -50,000 + 27,272.73 + 24,793.39 + 11,269.72 \\
&= 13,335.84
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= -1,00,000 + \frac{40,000}{1.10} + \frac{50,000}{1.21} + \frac{60,000}{1.331} \\
&= -1,00,000 + 36,363.64 + 41,322.31 + 45,078.89 \\
&= 22,764.84
\end{aligned}
$$

- $NPV_A$ = ₹13,335.84 ; $NPV_B$ = ₹22,764.84 → **Project B has higher NPV.**

**Step 2:** Compute IRR of each (by trial and error).
- For **Project A** at $r = 30\%$: $NPV = -50,000 + 23,076.92 + 17,751.48 + 6,810.58 = -2,361.02$
- For **Project A** at $r = 25\%$: $NPV = -50,000 + 24,000 + 19,200 + 7,680 = +880$
- $IRR_A \approx 25\% + \dfrac{880}{880+2361} \times 5\% \approx 25\% + 1.36\% \approx 26.36\%$

- For **Project B** at $r = 20\%$: $NPV = -1,00,000 + 33,333.33 + 34,722.22 + 34,722.22 = +2,777.77$
- For **Project B** at $r = 22\%$: $NPV = -1,00,000 + 32,786.89 + 33,602.46 + 32,985.72 = -624.93$
- $IRR_B \approx 20\% + \dfrac{2,777.77}{2,777.77+624.93} \times 2\% \approx 20\% + 1.63\% \approx 21.63\%$

- **Observation:** $IRR_A$ (26.36%) > $IRR_B$ (21.63%), but $NPV_B$ > $NPV_A$. **Conflict exists!**

**Step 3:** Compute Incremental Cash Flows (B − A).

| Year | B − A (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | −50,000 |
| 1 | +10,000 |
| 2 | +20,000 |
| 3 | +45,000 |

**Step 4:** Compute Incremental IRR.
- At $r = 15\%$: $NPV = -50,000 + 8,695.65 + 15,122.87 + 29,571.43 = +3,389.95$
- At $r = 22\%$: $NPV = -50,000 + 8,196.72 + 13,440.99 + 24,739.29 = -3,622.99$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR_{B-A} &= 15\% + \frac{3,389.95}{3,389.95 + 3,622.99} \times 7\% \\
&= 15\% + \frac{3,389.95}{7,012.94} \times 7\% \\
&= 15\% + 0.4834 \times 7\% \\
&= 15\% + 3.38\% \\
&\approx 18.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final Decision.
Since $IRR_{B-A}$ (18.38%) > Cost of Capital (10%), the additional investment of ₹50,000 in Project B is justified. **Choose Project B.**

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **NPV Method** | **IRR Method** |
|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Absolute wealth addition (₹) | Percentage return (%) |
| **Decision Criterion** | NPV > 0 | IRR > Cost of Capital |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | At cost of capital (realistic) | At IRR (often unrealistic) |
| **Reliability with Mutually Exclusive Projects** | High — always reliable | Can give misleading rankings |
| **Multiple IRR Problem** | Not affected | Yes, for non-conventional cash flows |
| **Ease of Communication** | Less intuitive for non-financial managers | Easier — expressed as a % |
| **Theoretical Superiority** | **Yes — preferred method** | No, but widely used in practice |
| **Computational Ease** | Direct computation | Requires iteration / interpolation |
| **Best Used For** | Final decision-making | Initial screening / communication |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart — NPV vs IRR Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Capital Budgeting Decision] --> B{Independent or Mutually Exclusive?}
    B -- Independent --> C[Compute NPV at Cost of Capital]
    C --> D{NPV > 0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Accept Project]
    D -- No --> F[Reject Project]
    B -- Mutually Exclusive --> G[Compute NPV of All Projects]
    G --> H[Compute IRR of All Projects]
    H --> I{NPV and IRR Give Same Rank?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Select Project with Higher NPV / IRR]
    I -- No --> K[Compute Incremental Cash Flows]
    K --> L[Compute Incremental IRR]
    L --> M{Incremental IRR > Cost of Capital?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Choose Larger Investment Project]
    M -- No --> O[Choose Smaller Investment Project]
    J --> P[Final Decision: Highest NPV]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    E --> Q[End]
    F --> Q
    P --> Q
```

### 4.2 NPV Profile Schematic — Crossover Rate Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Discount Rate = 0 percent] --> A2[NPV equals Sum of Cash Flows]
    A2 --> A3[NPV Profile Curve: X axis = Discount Rate]
    A3 --> A4[Y axis = NPV in Rupees]
    A4 --> A5[Curve Slopes Downward as Rate Increases]
    A5 --> A6[Project A IRR Point: Curve Crosses X axis]
    A6 --> A7[Project B IRR Point: Curve Crosses X axis]
    A7 --> A8[Intersection Point: Crossover Rate]
    A8 --> A9[Left of Crossover: Smaller Project A Dominates]
    A8 --> A10[Right of Crossover: Larger Project B Dominates]
    A9 --> A11[KTU Insight: Choose Project with Higher NPV at Cost of Capital]
    A10 --> A11
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — NPV-IRR Conflict Resolution Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Input Module: Cash Flow Series A] --> proc1[Process: Discount at Cost of Capital r]
    input2[Input Module: Cash Flow Series B] --> proc1
    input3[Input Module: Cost of Capital r] --> proc1
    proc1 --> comp1[Compute NPV of A]
    proc1 --> comp2[Compute NPV of B]
    proc1 --> comp3[Compute IRR of A by Interpolation]
    proc1 --> comp4[Compute IRR of B by Interpolation]
    comp1 --> rank1[Rank Module: NPV Based Ranking]
    comp2 --> rank1
    comp3 --> rank2[Rank Module: IRR Based Ranking]
    comp4 --> rank2
    rank1 --> check{Rank Conflict Detected?}
    rank2 --> check
    check -- No --> out1[Output: Select Higher NPV Project]
    check -- Yes --> inc1[Incremental Analysis Engine]
    inc1 --> inc2[Compute Cash Flow B minus A]
    inc2 --> inc3[Compute Incremental IRR]
    inc3 --> judge{Incremental IRR greater than r?}
    judge -- Yes --> out2[Output: Choose Project B Larger Investment]
    judge -- No --> out3[Output: Choose Project A Smaller Investment]
    out1 --> final[Final Recommendation Module]
    out2 --> final
    out3 --> final
```

### 4.4 Reinvestment Rate Assumption — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Reinvestment Rate Concept] --> a[NPV Method]
    root --> b[IRR Method]
    a --> a1[Assumes Cash Flows Reinvested at Cost of Capital r]
    a1 --> a2[Realistic for Corporate Projects]
    a2 --> a3[Conservative Estimate of Future Value]
    b --> b1[Assumes Cash Flows Reinvested at IRR Itself]
    b1 --> b2[Overstates Future Value when IRR is High]
    b2 --> b3[Can Lead to Biased Project Selection]
    a3 --> conclusion[Conclusion: NPV is Theoretically Superior]
    b3 --> conclusion
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) as techniques of capital budgeting."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **NPV** | **IRR** |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Difference between PV of inflows and outflows | Discount rate that makes NPV = 0 |
| Measurement | Absolute value (₹) | Percentage return (%) |
| Decision Rule | Accept if NPV > 0 | Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital |
| Reinvestment Assumption | At cost of capital | At IRR |
| Reliability | Theoretically superior, always reliable | Can give conflicting rankings for mutually exclusive projects |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition of each, 1 for distinguishing points, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Explain the concept of 'Crossover Rate' in the context of NPV and IRR comparison."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Crossover Rate** is the discount rate at which two mutually exclusive projects have **equal Net Present Values**. Beyond this rate, the ranking of the two projects based on NPV reverses. It is computed by finding the IRR of the **incremental cash flows** between the two projects.

The crossover rate is important because it identifies the discount rate below which one project is preferred and above which the other is preferred. In KTU-style problems, the crossover rate helps in selecting the correct project when NPV and IRR give conflicting rankings.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for computation logic, 1 for practical significance)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often choose the project with the higher IRR without computing incremental IRR. **Always compute incremental IRR when NPV-IRR conflict exists.** (Penalty: −3 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In IRR interpolation, students forget to maintain the sign of NPV_L as positive and NPV_H as negative. **The interpolation formula requires the lower rate to give positive NPV and the higher rate to give negative NPV.** (Penalty: −2 marks)
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students frequently use `r = 0` or skip the discount factor for Year 1. **Always apply $1/(1+r)^t$ for every cash flow including Year 1.** (Penalty: −1 mark)
> - **Common Mistake 4:** When NPV > 0, the project is accepted — students sometimes incorrectly state "Profit = NPV." **NPV is value addition, not accounting profit.** (Penalty: −1 mark)

---

#### **PART B — CHOICE A (14 Marks)**

##### **Question A (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**A company is considering two mutually exclusive projects. The initial investment for Project X is ₹2,00,000 and for Project Y is ₹3,00,000. The cost of capital is 12%. The cash inflows are:**

| **Year** | **Project X (₹)** | **Project Y (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80,000 | 1,00,000 |
| 2 | 90,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 3 | 1,00,000 | 1,50,000 |

**Compute the NPV of both projects and recommend which project to accept.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute NPV of Project X.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -2,00,000 + \frac{80,000}{1.12} + \frac{90,000}{(1.12)^2} + \frac{1,00,000}{(1.12)^3} \\
&= -2,00,000 + 71,428.57 + 71,779.55 + 71,178.02 \\
&= 14,386.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute NPV of Project Y.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= -3,00,000 + \frac{1,00,000}{1.12} + \frac{1,20,000}{(1.12)^2} + \frac{1,50,000}{(1.12)^3} \\
&= -3,00,000 + 89,285.71 + 95,706.04 + 1,06,767.03 \\
&= -8,241.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decision.**
- $NPV_X$ = ₹14,386.14 (Positive → Accept)
- $NPV_Y$ = −₹8,241.22 (Negative → Reject)

**Recommendation: Accept Project X** because only it has a positive NPV and creates wealth for the firm.

---

##### **Question A (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 and yields annual cash inflows of ₹1,50,000 for 5 years. Calculate the IRR using the interpolation method. The cost of capital is 12%. Advise whether the project is acceptable.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Set up the NPV equation.**

$$
NPV = -5,00,000 + \frac{1,50,000}{(1+IRR)^1} + \frac{1,50,000}{(1+IRR)^2} + \dots + \frac{1,50,000}{(1+IRR)^5}
$$

**Step 2: Try $r = 15\%$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{15\%} &= -5,00,000 + 1,50,000 \times PVIFA(15\%, 5) \\
&= -5,00,000 + 1,50,000 \times 3.3522 \\
&= -5,00,000 + 5,02,830 \\
&= +2,830
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Try $r = 16\%$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{16\%} &= -5,00,000 + 1,50,000 \times PVIFA(16\%, 5) \\
&= -5,00,000 + 1,50,000 \times 3.2743 \\
&= -5,00,000 + 4,91,145 \\
&= -8,855
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Linear Interpolation Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IRR &= r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L) \\
&= 15\% + \frac{2,830}{2,830 - (-8,855)} \times (16\% - 15\%) \\
&= 15\% + \frac{2,830}{11,685} \times 1\% \\
&= 15\% + 0.2422\% \\
&\approx 15.24\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Decision.**
- $IRR \approx 15.24\%$
- Cost of Capital = 12%
- Since IRR (15.24%) > Cost of Capital (12%), **the project is acceptable.**

---

#### **PART B — CHOICE B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE OPTION]**

##### **Question B (a) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the four major reasons why NPV and IRR methods may give conflicting rankings for mutually exclusive projects. How is this conflict resolved?**

**Model Solution:**

**The four major reasons for NPV-IRR conflict:**

**1. Size (Scale) Disparity:** When one project requires a much larger investment than the other, the larger project may have a higher NPV but lower IRR (because percentage returns shrink as base grows). Example: A ₹10,00,000 project with 18% IRR may yield a higher NPV than a ₹1,00,000 project with 25% IRR.

**2. Timing (Cash Flow Pattern) Disparity:** When one project generates cash flows early and the other late, the early-cash-flow project has a higher IRR (because of faster recovery), but the late-cash-flow project may have a higher NPV.

**3. Reinvestment Rate Assumption:** NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital (realistic), while IRR assumes reinvestment at the IRR itself (often unrealistic and overstates future value).

**4. Multiple IRR Problem:** Projects with non-conventional cash flows (e.g., + − + −) may produce multiple IRRs, making the IRR method unreliable. The **Modified IRR (MIRR)** overcomes this.

**Conflict Resolution Procedure:**

- **Step 1:** Calculate NPV of both projects at the cost of capital.
- **Step 2:** Calculate IRR of both projects.
- **Step 3:** If rankings conflict, compute **Incremental Cash Flows** (B − A).
- **Step 4:** Compute **Incremental IRR** ($IRR_{B-A}$).
- **Step 5:** If $IRR_{B-A} > r$ → Choose the larger project. If $IRR_{B-A} < r$ → Choose the smaller project.
- **Final Rule:** When in doubt, **always prefer the project with the higher NPV** as it directly measures wealth creation.

*(7 marks: 2 for explaining 4 reasons, 3 for resolution steps, 1 for conclusion, 1 for final recommendation rule)*

---

##### **Question B (b) — 7 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**A firm is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects with the following cash flows. The cost of capital is 10%.**

| **Year** | **Project M (₹)** | **Project N (₹)** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | −4,00,000 | −6,00,000 |
| 1 | 1,50,000 | 2,00,000 |
| 2 | 1,80,000 | 2,50,000 |
| 3 | 2,00,000 | 3,00,000 |

**Compute NPV and IRR of both projects. If a conflict exists, resolve it using the Incremental IRR method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: NPV of Project M.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_M &= -4,00,000 + \frac{1,50,000}{1.10} + \frac{1,80,000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{2,00,000}{(1.10)^3} \\
&= -4,00,000 + 1,36,363.64 + 1,48,760.33 + 1,50,263.05 \\
&= 35,387.02
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: NPV of Project N.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_N &= -6,00,000 + \frac{2,00,000}{1.10} + \frac{2,50,000}{(1.10)^2} + \frac{3,00,000}{(1.10)^3} \\
&= -6,00,000 + 1,81,818.18 + 2,06,611.57 + 2,25,394.59 \\
&= 13,824.34
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute IRR (trial values).**

- For **Project M** at 15%: $NPV = -4,00,000 + 1,30,434.78 + 1,36,132.80 + 1,31,687.24 = -1,745.18$
- For **Project M** at 14%: $NPV = -4,00,000 + 1,31,578.95 + 1,38,503.27 + 1,34,971.30 = +5,053.52$
- $IRR_M \approx 14\% + \dfrac{5,053.52}{5,053.52 + 1,745.18} \times 1\% \approx 14.74\%$

- For **Project N** at 12%: $NPV = -6,00,000 + 1,78,571.43 + 1,99,297.07 + 2,13,562.78 = -8,568.72$
- For **Project N** at 10%: $NPV = 13,824.34$ (already computed)
- $IRR_N \approx 10\% + \dfrac{13,824.34}{13,824.34 + 8,568.72} \times 2\% \approx 11.23\%$

**Step 4: Conflict Detection.**
- NPV: $NPV_M$ (35,387) > $NPV_N$ (13,824) → **Project M ranks higher by NPV**
- IRR: $IRR_M$ (14.74%) > $IRR_N$ (11.23%) → **Project M ranks higher by IRR**
- **No conflict in this case** — both methods favor Project M.

**Step 5: Recommendation.**
Since both NPV and IRR rank Project M higher, **accept Project M.** The incremental IRR analysis is not strictly required here, but for completeness:

Incremental Cash Flows (M − N) = (−4L, +1.5L, +1.8L, +2L) − (−6L, +2L, +2.5L, +3L) = (+2L, −0.5L, −0.7L, −1L)

Since incremental cash flows are negative after Year 0, the incremental NPV is negative, confirming **Project M is preferred.**

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — NPV & IRR Comparison)**

- **NPV Formula:** $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ — accept if **NPV > 0**.
- **IRR Formula:** Solve $\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ — accept if **IRR > Cost of Capital**.
- **NPV measures** absolute value addition in **rupees**; **IRR measures** percentage return.
- **NPV assumes** reinvestment at **cost of capital**; **IRR assumes** reinvestment at **IRR itself**.
- For **independent projects**, both NPV and IRR give the same accept/reject decision.
- For **mutually exclusive projects**, conflict may arise due to **size, timing, reinvestment assumptions**, or **multiple IRR problem**.
- **Conflict Resolution:** Compute **Incremental IRR** ($IRR_{B-A}$) and compare with cost of capital.
- **Crossover Rate** = discount rate at which two projects have **equal NPVs**.
- **Modified IRR (MIRR):** $MIRR = \left(\dfrac{FV\ of\ Inflows}{PV\ of\ Outflows}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ — solves multiple IRR problem.
- **Theoretical Superiority:** **NPV is always preferred** in case of NPV-IRR conflict (Fisher's Theorem of Capital Budgeting).
- **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **decision rule** explicitly before computing numerical values.
- **Interpolation Formula:** $IRR = r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$ — keep NPV_L positive and NPV_H negative.
- **Profitability Index (PI):** $PI = \dfrac{PV\ of\ Inflows}{Initial\ Investment}$ — accept if PI > 1; rank when NPV same but investment differs.
- **Practical Application:** Used in **engineering project selection** — e.g., choosing between two plant capacities, two equipment alternatives, or two R&D proposals.
- **Standard Cost of Capital** in KTU problems: typically **10%, 12%, or 15%** unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAPITAL RATIONING** is the situation in which a firm faces a **constraint on the amount of capital budget available for investment in new projects**, even when several otherwise-acceptable (positive NPV) projects exist. Under capital rationing, the firm must select an **optimal subset of projects** that maximizes the aggregate Net Present Value (NPV) subject to the available capital ceiling.

In the standard KTU 2024 Scheme financial management framework, capital rationing is treated as a **constrained optimization problem** in capital budgeting. Unlike the unconstrained (theoretical) case where all independent projects with positive NPV are accepted, capital rationing forces a **ranking-and-selection exercise** under a binding resource limit.

### 1.1.1 Types of Capital Rationing (Board-Exam Favorite)

| # | Type | Origin of the Constraint | Decision Authority |
|---|------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Hard Rationing (External)** | Imposed by the **external capital market** (e.g., lenders, equity market, government regulations). | Outside the firm's control. |
| 2 | **Soft Rationing (Internal)** | Imposed **internally by management** (e.g., retaining earnings to preserve control, avoiding debt covenants, dividend policy). | Within the firm's control. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** The Board frequently frames questions asking students to *distinguish between hard and soft rationing*. Memorize the source of the constraint — **External = Hard**, **Internal = Soft**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Birthday Gift Dilemma"

Imagine you are a college student with exactly **₹500** in your pocket for a friend's birthday. You enter a gift shop and see four attractive gifts:

| Gift | Cost (₹) | Happiness Points (Utility) |
|------|----------|---------------------------|
| Headphones | 350 | 9 |
| Book | 200 | 6 |
| Chocolate box | 150 | 5 |
| Greeting card | 50 | 3 |

You cannot buy all four because your **budget is rationed at ₹500**. You want to **maximize happiness**. The natural thought process is:
- Isolate the *cost per unit of happiness* (a **Profitability Index** analog).
- Combine items to fully use the budget while **maximizing total utility (analogous to total NPV)**.

This is **exactly** the capital rationing problem in capital budgeting. Each project is a "gift," the **capital ceiling is ₹500**, and the firm wants to **maximize total NPV (happiness)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Capital Rationing Matters in Engineering Enterprises

For B.Tech students, this concept is relevant in:
- **Project management of large engineering ventures** (e.g., a construction firm choosing between two infra projects with limited working capital).
- **Start-up decisions** where founders face seed-funding ceilings.
- **Capital-intensive industries** — semiconductors, automotive, civil infra — where rationing is the norm, not the exception.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight (Board-Exam Gold):** When projects are **divisible**, you can apply the **Profitability Index (PI)** method. When projects are **indivisible** (the realistic case), you need **Integer Programming / Branch-and-Bound / Trial-and-Error** with ranking.

---

## 1.4 Single-Period vs Multi-Period Rationing

| Dimension | Single-Period Rationing | Multi-Period Rationing |
|-----------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| **Time Frame** | Constraint applies to **one** budgeting period. | Constraint applies across **multiple** periods. |
| **Method** | Simple PI ranking, enumeration. | **Linear Programming (LP)** required. |
| **Complexity** | Low — KTU 3-mark territory. | High — KTU 14-mark territory. |
| **Project Cash Flows** | All outflows occur in period 0. | Outflows spread over multiple years. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) focuses predominantly on **Single-Period Capital Rationing with Indivisible Projects**, where a firm has a fixed budget in period 0 and must select a combination of projects that maximizes total NPV.
